```mermaid
    p5 --> p5a["Pulverization of in-situ soil"]
    p5 --> p5b["Spreading of stabilizer at design dosage"]
    p5 --> p5c["Mixing with rotary mixer or reclaimer"]
    p5 --> p5d["Compaction with sheep foot or vibratory roller"]
    p5 --> p5e["Curing for 3 to 7 days"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define soil stabilization. List any four objectives of soil stabilization in ground improvement.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Soil Stabilization** is the process of modifying the engineering properties of a soil (such as strength, compressibility, permeability, and durability) by mechanical means, chemical additives, or a combination of both, to make it suitable for construction.

**Four Objectives of Soil Stabilization:**

1. **Strength Improvement:** To increase the bearing capacity and shear strength of weak subgrade soils, enabling them to support traffic or structural loads.
2. **Reduction of Compressibility:** To minimize long-term settlement by enhancing the soil skeleton and reducing the void ratio.
3. **Control of Shrink-Swell Behaviour:** To mitigate the volume-change potential of expansive clays (e.g., black cotton soil in northern Kerala).
4. **Reduction of Permeability:** To create impervious barriers in canal linings, earthen dam cores, and landfills.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between mechanical stabilization and chemical stabilization of soils.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Aspect | Mechanical Stabilization | Chemical Stabilization |
|-------|--------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Mechanism | Physical alteration of gradation or density | Chemical reaction between soil and binder |
| 2 | Additives | Granular soils, sand, gravel | Lime, cement, bitumen, fly-ash |
| 3 | Time Effect | Immediate strength gain | Strength develops over time (curing-dependent) |
| 4 | Permanence | Reversible on saturation | Permanent (irreversible) |
| 5 | Cost | Low to moderate | Moderate to high |
| 6 | Suitable Soils | Well-graded granular soils | Clayey and fine-grained soils |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Convention (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **chemical reactions involved in lime stabilization** of expansive clay. With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the **Eades & Grim pH test** used to determine the Lime Fixation Point (LFP).

**(b)** A clayey soil has an Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) of **$480 \text{ kPa}$** in its natural state. After treatment with **6% hydrated lime** and 7 days of moist curing, the UCS rises to **$1380 \text{ kPa}$**. Compute the **Strength Improvement Ratio (SIR)** and comment on whether the dosage is acceptable as per **IRC:SP:89** (minimum SIR = 100%).

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Identification of clay mineral surface and adsorbed cations.**

Expansive clays (such as montmorillonite) carry a net negative charge on their silicate layers. This is balanced by **adsorbed cations** such as $Na^+$, $K^+$, $Ca^{2+}$, and $Mg^{2+}$ sitting in the **diffuse double layer**.

**Step 2 — Addition of lime.**

Hydrated lime $Ca(OH)_2$ dissociates in pore water:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ca(OH)_2 &\rightarrow Ca^{2+} + 2(OH)^- \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cation exchange and flocculation.**

The highly reactive $Ca^{2+}$ ions replace the monovalent $Na^+$ and $K^+$ ions on the clay surface:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Clay} - Na^+ + Ca^{2+} &\rightarrow \text{Clay} - Ca^{2+} + 2Na^+ \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

This causes a **reduction in the diffuse double layer thickness**, leading to **flocculation** of clay platelets and a sharp drop in plasticity.

**Step 4 — Carbonation (minor, surface phenomenon).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 &\rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Pozzolanic reaction (the major strength-gaining reaction).**

In the high-pH environment (pH > 12), reactive silica ($SiO_2$) and alumina ($Al_2O_3$) from the clay dissolve and react with $Ca^{2+}$ and $OH^-$ to form **cementitious gels**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ca^{2+} + SiO_2 + OH^- &\rightarrow \text{C-S-H gel} \\[6pt]
Ca^{2+} + Al_2O_3 + OH^- &\rightarrow \text{C-A-H gel} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

These gels coat the soil grains and bind them together, producing a hard, durable matrix.

**Step 6 — Eades & Grim pH Test (Sketch & Procedure).**

The **Lime Fixation Point (LFP)** is the minimum lime content at which the soil pH stabilizes at **12.4** — the saturated pH of $Ca(OH)_2$.

**Procedure:**

1. Take 5 soil samples of 20 g each in separate beakers.
2. Add 0%, 2%, 4%, 6%, and 8% hydrated lime (by dry soil weight) respectively.
3. Add 100 mL of distilled water to each beaker (soil-water ratio = 1:5).
4. Stir/shake for 30 minutes; allow to settle for 1 hour.
5. Measure pH of supernatant using a calibrated pH meter.
6. Plot pH (y-axis) vs % Lime (x-axis).
7. The **LFP** is the lime % beyond which the pH curve flattens at 12.4.

**Labelled Sketch (Conceptual):**

```
pH
12.6 |          __________________  (Plateau)
12.4 |---------/   <-- LFP marker
12.2 |       /
12.0 |      /
11.8 |    /
11.6 |  /
11.4 |/
     +------------------------- % Lime
     0    2    4    6    8  10
          ↑
        LFP
```

**Step 7 — Optimum Lime Content (OLC).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OLC &= LFP + 1\% \text{ to } 2\% \text{ safety margin} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Write the SIR formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SIR &= \frac{q_{u,\text{stabilized}} - q_{u,\text{original}}}{q_{u,\text{original}}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical data.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SIR &= \frac{1380 - 480}{480} \times 100 \\[6pt]
SIR &= \frac{900}{480} \times 100 \\[6pt]
SIR &= 1.875 \times 100 \\[6pt]
SIR &= 187.5\% \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with IRC:SP:89 requirement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SIR_{\text{obtained}} &= 187.5\% \\[6pt]
SIR_{\text{required}} &= 100\% \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

Since $187.5\% \gg 100\%$, the **lime dosage of 6% is well within the acceptable range** and may be adopted for field application. (Examiner's tip: also mention that the dosage corresponding to *peak SIR* is the truly optimum, and a dose-response curve is recommended for confirmation.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanism of cement stabilization** of soils. How does cement differ from lime in its stabilizing action? Comment on the suitability of cement for **granular versus cohesive** soils.

**(b)** A soil-cement mix is compacted at **OMC = 13.8%** to a **Maximum Dry Density (MDD) of 1.92 g/cc**. For a field sample of mass **$M = 3.5 \text{ kg}$** compacted with **8% cement**, compute: **(i)** the mass of cement required, and **(ii)** the porosity of the compacted stabilized mass, given $G_s = 2.70$.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Cement composition.**

Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) consists of four major mineral phases: $C_3S$ (alite), $C_2S$ (belite), $C_3A$ (tricalcium aluminate), and $C_4AF$ (tetracalcium aluminoferrite).

**Step 2 — Cement hydration reactions.**

When cement contacts water, the following reactions occur:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_3S + H_2O &\rightarrow C-S-H \text{ gel} + Ca(OH)_2 \\[6pt]
C_2S + H_2O &\rightarrow C-S-H \text{ gel} + Ca(OH)_2 \\[6pt]
C_3A + H_2O &\rightarrow C-A-H \text{ gel} \\[6pt]
C_4AF + H_2O &\rightarrow C-A-F-H \text{ gel} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

The **C-S-H gel** is the principal strength-giving product. The liberated $Ca(OH)_2$ may further react with pozzolanic soil constituents (if any), but unlike pure lime stabilization, this secondary reaction is **not essential** to cement stabilization.

**Step 3 — Direct grain-to-grain bonding.**

In cement-stabilized soil, the cementitious gel **glues adjacent soil grains** into a rigid skeleton. This is fundamentally a **direct hydration** mechanism, independent of the soil's mineralogy.

**Step 4 — Cement vs Lime: Key Differences.**

| Property | Lime | Cement |
|----------|------|--------|
| Primary reaction | Cation exchange + pozzolanic | Direct hydration |
| Soil-type suitability | Clayey (plastic) soils | Granular and clayey both |
| Time to gain strength | 7–28 days (pozzolanic-dependent) | 1–7 days (rapid) |
| Sensitivity to organics | Highly sensitive | Less sensitive |
| Plasticity requirement | High plasticity preferred | Wide range |

**Step 5 — Suitability for granular vs cohesive soils.**

- **Granular soils:** Cement works exceptionally well due to good grain-to-grain contact, low water demand, and rapid strength gain. Cement is the **first choice** for sandy/gravelly subgrades.
- **Cohesive soils:** Cement is *less effective* than lime in heavy clays because:
  - High water demand increases the difficulty of compaction.
  - Clay particles inhibit uniform cement dispersion.
  - Shrinkage cracking may occur at high cement dosages.
- For cohesive soils, lime is preferred; for granular soils, cement is preferred.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Part (i) — Mass of cement required.**

**Step 1 — Calculate the dry mass of the soil in the field sample.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{dry,soil}} &= M \times \left(1 + \frac{OMC}{100}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 3.5 \text{ kg} \times \left(1 + 0.138\right) \\[6pt]
&= 3.5 \text{ kg} \times 1.138 \\[6pt]
&= 3.983 \text{ kg} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the cement mass at 8% dosage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{cement}} &= M_{\text{dry,soil}} \times \frac{8}{100} \\[6pt]
&= 3.983 \text{ kg} \times 0.08 \\[6pt]
&= 0.3186 \text{ kg} \\[6pt]
&\approx 318.6 \text{ g} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Porosity of the compacted mass.**

**Step 3 — Apply the dry density – specific gravity – porosity relation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 1 - \frac{\rho_d}{G_s \cdot \rho_w} \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{1.92}{2.70 \times 1.00} \\[6pt]
&= 1 - 0.7111 \\[6pt]
&= 0.2889 \\[6pt]
&\approx 28.89\% \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final engineered answer.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{cement}} &\approx 318.6 \text{ g} \\[6pt]
n &\approx 28.89\% \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A porosity of 28.89% in the cement-stabilized compacted mass is well within the **22% to 35%** acceptable range for subgrade quality, indicating good compaction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Soil Stabilization Answers (PECET633 Module 3):**
>
> 1. **Confusing stabilization with compaction.** Always state clearly that compaction is a *subset* of mechanical stabilization, not its synonym.
> 2. **Forgetting the curing period.** Cement- and lime-stabilized soils need **3–7 days of moist curing**; missing this in the methodology costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping units in the final answer.** A numerical answer without units (kPa, %, g/cc) loses 1 mark in KTU evaluation.
> 4. **Not citing Indian Standards.** A theory answer without a reference to **IS 2720** or **IRC codes** is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Mixing up pozzolanic reaction and cement hydration.** Examiners deduct 2 marks if these two distinct reaction mechanisms are conflated.
> 6. **Omitting the safety margin on LFP.** Always add **+1% to +2%** over the LFP when stating the Optimum Lime Content.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Soil stabilization = alteration of soil properties (physical/chemical/mechanical) to meet engineering requirements.
- **Two main branches:** Mechanical Stabilization and Chemical Stabilization.
- **Compaction is a subset** of mechanical stabilization; not its synonym.
- **Lime** is preferred for **clayey, plastic, expansive** soils.
- **Cement** is preferred for **granular, sandy, gravelly** soils.
- **Bitumen** is preferred for **waterproofing** and dust control.
- **Fly-ash** is a pozzolanic material — best when blended with **lime** for active pozzolanic reaction.
- **Four fundamental reaction mechanisms** in chemical stabilization: (1) Cation exchange, (2) Flocculation, (3) Carbonation, (4) Pozzolanic reaction.
- **Cement stabilization** works via **direct hydration** of $C_3S$, $C_2S$, $C_3A$, $C_4AF$ phases — produces C-S-H and C-A-H gels.
- **Eades & Grim pH test** determines Lime Fixation Point (LFP) at **pH = 12.4**.
- **OLC = LFP + 1% to 2%** safety margin.
- **Target $q_u$** for stabilized subgrade $\geq$ **$1.7 \text{ MPa}$** (AASHTO/IRC guidelines).
- **Target CBR** for stabilized sub-base $\geq$ **$30\%$** (IS 2720 Part 16).
- **Compaction quality** is acceptable when $n$ lies between **$22\%$ and $35\%$**.
- **Always cure** stabilized soil for **3–7 days** under moist conditions before opening to traffic.
- **Always cite Indian Standards** (IS 2720 Parts 5, 7, 8, 10, 16) and IRC codes (IRC:SP:89, IRC:37) in your answers.
- **Indian soils & Kerala context:** Laterite, Kuttanad clay, marine clay, and black cotton patches — each demands a different stabilizer choice.
- **Field sequence:** Investigation $\rightarrow$ Lab Testing $\rightarrow$ Mix Design $\rightarrow$ Trial Section $\rightarrow$ Bulk Stabilization $\rightarrow$ QC Monitoring.

> **End of Module 3 - Introduction Notes (PECET633) | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibro Compaction – Blasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET633 - GROUND IMPROVEMENT TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibro Compaction – Blasting**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vibro Compaction (Vibroflotation)

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
> **Vibro Compaction**, also known as **Vibroflotation**, is a deep ground improvement technique that uses a depth vibrator (called a *vibroflot* or *vibroprobe*) to densify granular, cohesionless soils in-situ. The technique employs a large cylindrical vibrating probe that is inserted into the ground using a combination of self-weight, vibration, and water jetting. The horizontal vibrations, typically generated at frequencies of **30–50 Hz**, cause the surrounding granular soil grains to rearrange into a denser packing configuration, thereby increasing the relative density, shear strength, and stiffness of the deposit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Vibro Compaction is the **most effective** in-situ densification method for loose, saturated, cohesionless sands with **less than 15–20% fines content (silt + clay)**. It becomes progressively **inefficient** as fines content exceeds 20% because the fines clog the pore space and dampen vibration transmission.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Milkshake & Sandcastle" Concept
Imagine you have a jar of loose, dry sand and you tap the side of the jar repeatedly. The sand grains momentarily "float" (lose contact with each other) and then settle into a much tighter arrangement. The volume of sand drops, the jar feels heavier per unit volume, and it can support more weight before failing. **Vibro Compaction is essentially doing this — but 30 metres deep, using a vibrating poker instead of your finger.** Water jets act like a lubricant to help the probe slide into the ground and the grains to rearrange. The "looseness" of the sand is measured by the **Standard Penetration Test (SPT) N-value** or **Cone Penetration Test (CPT) qc** value — both of which **increase dramatically** after treatment.

---

## 1.2 Blasting (Explosive Densification)

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
> **Blasting**, also termed **Explosive Compaction** or **Blast Densification**, is a deep ground improvement technique in which explosive charges are detonated within a loose, saturated, cohesionless soil mass to induce temporary liquefaction, followed by reconsolidation of the soil grains into a denser state. The detonation generates shock waves and high pore-water pressures that momentarily destroy the soil structure, after which the grains settle into a denser packing as the excess pore pressure dissipates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operational Metric:** Charge weights typically range from **2 to 30 kg of TNT equivalent per hole**, placed in boreholes spaced on a **5–10 m grid pattern**, and detonated in **delayed sequences** to maximize densification while controlling ground vibration.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Underwater Earthquake & Reset"
Picture a glass of muddy water that has been stirred and then left to settle. The silt particles fall down and pack into a tight, dense layer at the bottom. **Blasting does the same thing — but it uses a controlled explosion instead of gravity.** The blast acts like a tiny, focused earthquake that shakes the soil grains apart (liquefying them temporarily), and then they fall back together in a much tighter, denser arrangement. Engineers actually *exploit* liquefaction (which is normally a disaster) to *improve* the ground. The catch: you can only do this safely in **saturated, cohesionless soils** far from existing buildings, because the explosion can damage nearby structures.

---

## 1.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Snapshot

| Property | Vibro Compaction | Blasting |
|---|---|---|
| Best suited soil | Clean sand to gravelly sand | Loose saturated sand (often underwater) |
| Fines limit | < 15–20% | Low fines preferred |
| Effective depth | Up to **30–40 m** | Up to **50+ m** (limited by borehole drilling) |
| Method of densification | Mechanical vibration | Shock wave + liquefaction |
| Speed of treatment | Moderate (point-by-point) | Very fast (large area quickly) |
| Proximity to structures | Generally safe | Strict setback required |
| Cost-effectiveness | High for medium depths | Highest for very large areas |
| Quality control | Excellent (SPT/CPT verifiable) | Difficult (heterogeneous results) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vibro Compaction — Theory of Operation

The vibroflot is a **torpedo-shaped cylindrical probe** (≈ 300–400 mm diameter, 3–5 m long) that contains:

- **Upper motor** (electric or hydraulic) rated typically **100–200 kW**
- **Eccentric weights** mounted on a horizontal shaft
- **Water jets** at the tip and upper side for jetting assistance
- **Slick joints** for extension tubes (allowing deeper penetration)

### Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Slick Connection:** The vibroflot is attached to extension tubes and suspended from a crane (the "follower" or "leader" mast).
2. **Water Jetting & Vibration:** High-pressure water jets at the tip (≈ **400–800 kPa**) liquefy the sand locally, while vibration loosens the surrounding soil. The probe sinks under its own weight to the design depth.
3. **Compaction Phase:** At the target depth, vibration continues. The probe is **withdrawn in 0.3–0.5 m increments**, each level being vibrated for **30–60 seconds** to allow full densification.
4. **Crater & Backfill:** A conical depression (crater) forms at the surface. It is continuously backfilled with **coarse sand or gravel** ("vibropribble material") to replace the densified volume and provide lateral confinement.
5. **Pattern Progression:** Probes are moved across the site in a **triangular or square grid** (typically 1.5–3.0 m spacing) until the entire area is treated.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Horizontal vibrations** (not vertical) are critical because they create shear waves that are most effective at rearranging granular grains.
- **Water jetting** is essential — dry sand absorbs vibration energy and the probe cannot penetrate.
- **Incremental withdrawal** ensures uniform densification layer by layer (avoids leaving loose pockets).
- **Triangular pattern** is preferred because it provides 15% more uniform coverage than a square pattern at the same spacing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Pearl:** The radius of influence of a vibroflot is typically **1.5 to 3.0 m** in loose sand and depends on grain size, fines content, confining stress, and vibration amplitude. This radius determines the **grid spacing** of the probes.

### Centrifugal Force Generated by the Vibroflot

The horizontal vibrations are produced by eccentric masses rotating at high speed. The horizontal component of centrifugal force per unit length of probe is:

$$F = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

Where:
- $F$ = horizontal centrifugal force (kN)
- $m_e$ = total mass of eccentric weights (kg)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) = $\dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ (N = RPM)
- $r$ = eccentricity (radius of rotation of the masses) (m)

The **amplitude of vibration** at the probe surface is:

$$a = \dfrac{m_e \cdot r}{M}$$

Where $M$ = total mass of the vibrating body (probe + eccentric weights) (kg).

### Densification Effect & Verification
The success of vibro compaction is verified by:
- **Before/After SPT N-values** (typically increase from 5–10 to 25–40+)
- **CPT tip resistance (qc)** measurements
- **Relative density (Dr)** improvements from 30–40% to 70–85%
- **Liquefaction resistance** (cyclic resistance ratio, CRR) increases

---

## 2.2 Blasting — Theory of Operation

Blasting exploits a controlled **liquefaction mechanism**. The sequence of events is:

1. **Borehole Drilling:** Boreholes are drilled through the loose sand layer to a depth of **0.5–0.7 × thickness of loose layer**. Typical diameter = **75–150 mm**.
2. **Charge Placement:** Explosive charges (typically **ANFO — Ammonium Nitrate Fuel Oil**, or slurry explosives) are placed at the bottom of each hole. Charges are **deck-loaded** at multiple depths for thick deposits.
3. **Stemming:** The upper part of the hole is filled with **sand or stemming material** to confine the explosion downward and outward.
4. **Detonation Sequence:** Charges are detonated in a **predetermined delay pattern** (using detonators with millisecond delays) so that shock waves interfere constructively and re-liquefy adjacent zones.
5. **Reconsolidation:** After each blast, pore-water pressure rises to ≈ overburden stress → **100% pore pressure ratio (ru = 1)** → soil temporarily flows. Over the next **hours to days**, the excess pore pressure dissipates and grains settle into a denser state.
6. **Verification:** SPT, CPT, or shear wave velocity ($V_s$) measurements are repeated to confirm densification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Practice:** Multiple blasts (typically 3–5 rounds) are conducted at the **same location with several days between blasts** to allow progressive densification. Each blast removes a portion of the void ratio.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Stemming** is critical — without it, the explosive energy blows out vertically (a "blow-out") and does no useful densification work.
- **Delay sequencing** prevents a single violent upheaval and allows energy to be released into the soil progressively.
- **Multiple blasts** are needed because one blast cannot fully densify a thick deposit; each round handles a portion of the void ratio reduction.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Master These Equations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Centrifugal force of vibroflot eccentric weights | N |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity of eccentric shaft | rad/s (N = RPM) |
| 3 | $a = \dfrac{m_e \cdot r}{M}$ | Vibration amplitude of probe | m |
| 4 | $V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{G_{max}}{\rho}}$ | Shear wave velocity (used in pre/post blasting QA) | m/s |
| 5 | $CRR_{7.5} = \dfrac{\tau_{cyc}}{\sigma_{vo}'}$ | Cyclic Resistance Ratio (liquefaction assessment) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $r_u = \dfrac{u_e}{\sigma_{vo}}$ | Pore pressure ratio during blast liquefaction | 0 ≤ ru ≤ 1 |
| 7 | $q_c = N_{60} \cdot \sigma_{vo}' \cdot C_N$ | Normalized CPT tip resistance correlation | MPa |
| 8 | $D_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{N_{60}}{60}}$ (Skempton, clean sand) | Relative density from SPT (empirical) | ratio (0 to 1) |
| 9 | $W = C \cdot d^n$ | Charge weight scaling (where d = distance) | kg |
| 10 | $L = \alpha \cdot H$ | Length of charge column (α = 0.5 to 0.7) | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Board Exam Note:** In exam answers, **always state assumptions** (e.g., "assuming clean sand", "for typical vibroflot operating at 1800 RPM"). The Board Examiner deducts marks for unsubstantiated formula usage.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Vibro Compaction / Blasting |
|---|---|
| **Port & Harbour Construction** | Densify reclaimed sand fills beneath quay walls and container yards |
| **Offshore Wind Farms** | Blast-densify loose marine sand at turbine foundations |
| **LNG / Oil Storage Tanks** | Mitigate liquefaction risk beneath large-diameter tanks |
| **Highway & Railway Embankments** | Vibro-compact sand fills to prevent settlement under cyclic train/vehicle loads |
| **Earthquake-Prone Regions** | Eliminate liquefaction susceptibility beneath critical infrastructure |
| **Land Reclamation** | Both methods densify hydraulically placed sand fills |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Force and Amplitude of Vibroflot

> **Given:** A vibroflot has two eccentric masses, each of mass $m = 12 \text{ kg}$, mounted on a horizontal shaft. The eccentricity (radius of rotation) is $r = 0.05 \text{ m}$. The shaft rotates at $N = 1800 \text{ RPM}$. The total vibrating mass (probe + eccentrics) is $M = 1800 \text{ kg}$. Compute the centrifugal force and amplitude.

### Step 1 — Convert RPM to Angular Velocity

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi \times 1800}{60} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi \times 30}{1} \\
&= 60 \pi \text{ rad/s} \\
&\approx 188.50 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Total Mass of Eccentric Weights

The probe carries two eccentric weights:

$$m_e = 2 \times m = 2 \times 12 = 24 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Centrifugal Force Generated

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \\
&= 24 \times (60 \pi)^2 \times 0.05 \\
&= 24 \times 3600 \pi^2 \times 0.05 \\
&= 24 \times 1776.48 \times 0.05 \\
&\approx 2131.8 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 2.13 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** A force of **2.13 kN** pulsating horizontally at 30 Hz (1800 RPM) is enough to rearrange loose sand grains into a dense state.

### Step 4 — Amplitude of Vibration

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{m_e \cdot r}{M} \\
&= \frac{24 \times 0.05}{1800} \\
&= \frac{1.2}{1800} \\
&\approx 6.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
&\approx 0.67 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

This **0.67 mm** peak displacement is the typical operating amplitude for vibroflotation in loose sand.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relative Density Improvement After Vibro Compaction

> **Given:** A loose sand deposit has an average SPT N-value of $N_1 = 8$ (uncorrected) before treatment, and $N_2 = 32$ after vibro compaction. Effective overburden pressure is $\sigma_{vo}' = 100 \text{ kPa}$. Estimate the change in relative density using Skempton's correlation. Take $C_N = 1.0$ (already corrected).

### Step 1 — Convert SPT to Normalized N-value

Assuming the values are already corrected ($N_{60,1} \approx 8$ and $N_{60,2} \approx 32$).

### Step 2 — Apply Skempton's Correlation for Clean Sand

$$D_r = \sqrt{\frac{N_{60}}{60}}$$

For **before treatment**:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{r,1} &= \sqrt{\frac{8}{60}} = \sqrt{0.1333} \\
&\approx 0.365 = 36.5\%
\end{aligned}$$

For **after treatment**:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{r,2} &= \sqrt{\frac{32}{60}} = \sqrt{0.5333} \\
&\approx 0.730 = 73.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Net Improvement in Relative Density

$$\Delta D_r = D_{r,2} - D_{r,1} = 0.730 - 0.365 = 0.365 = 36.5\%$$

> **Result:** The relative density increased from a **loose state (~36.5%)** to a **medium-dense to dense state (~73%)** — a textbook success criterion for vibro compaction in liquefaction-mitigation projects.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Charge Weight Estimation for Blasting Densification

> **Given:** A loose saturated sand deposit is **10 m thick** with the water table at the surface. Boreholes are to be drilled to a depth of **7 m** (i.e., 0.7 × thickness). The grid spacing is **6 m × 6 m** (triangular pattern equivalent area ≈ 31.2 m² per hole). Estimate the **charge weight per delay** to achieve liquefaction.

### Step 1 — Charge Depth Ratio

$$\frac{d}{H} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.7$$

This is within the **optimal range (0.5–0.75)** for blasting densification.

### Step 2 — Empirical Charge Weight (Lane's Guideline)

A widely used empirical formula for granular densification is:

$$W = k \cdot A \cdot d$$

Where:
- $W$ = charge weight (kg of TNT equivalent)
- $k$ = empirical constant ≈ **0.02 to 0.05 kg/m³** for loose sand
- $A$ = area of influence per hole (m²) = **31.2 m²**
- $d$ = depth of charge (m) = **7 m**

### Step 3 — Calculate Charge Weight

Take $k = 0.03$ kg/m³ (typical for medium-loose sand):

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 0.03 \times 31.2 \times 7 \\
&= 0.03 \times 218.4 \\
&\approx 6.55 \text{ kg per hole}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Total Charge per Round (assuming 4 holes per delay)

$$W_{delay} = 4 \times 6.55 = 26.2 \text{ kg per delay}$$

> **Result:** Each blast round requires approximately **6.55 kg of explosive per hole** to liquefy a column of 31.2 m² × 7 m. This is well within the **typical engineering range of 2–30 kg/hole** documented in KTU textbooks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Design Decision Tree

| Site Condition | Recommended Method | Primary Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Clean sand, depth < 30 m, near structures | **Vibro Compaction** | Controlled, low vibration, verifiable QC |
| Loose saturated sand, depth > 30 m, large area, no nearby structures | **Blasting** | Faster and cheaper for very large offshore sites |
| Sand with 5–15% fines | **Vibro with stone columns** (hybrid) | Pre-bore or use replacement probe |
| Sand with > 20% fines | **Not suitable for either** | Use Deep Soil Mixing or Dynamic Compaction |
| Above water table, partially saturated | **Vibro Compaction** (with copious water) | Blasting needs full saturation for liquefaction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** If a question gives a sand with fines content > 20%, the answer is **"Not suitable for Vibro Compaction"** with reason = "Vibration energy is damped by fines, and pore-pressure dissipation is slow due to low permeability."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vibroflot Apparatus — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crane and Leader Mast] --> B[Extension Tubes Slick Joint]
    B --> C[Vibroflot Probe Assembly]
    C --> D[Electric Hydraulic Motor 100 to 200 kW]
    C --> E[Horizontal Shaft with Eccentric Weights]
    C --> F[Water Jets at Tip and Upper Side]
    E --> G[Horizontal Vibration Output 30 to 50 Hz]
    G --> H[Granular Soil Grains Rearranged into Dense Packing]
    F --> I[Liquefies Sand Locally for Probe Penetration]
    I --> J[Probe Sinks to Design Depth]
    H --> K[Crater Forms at Surface]
    K --> L[Continuous Backfill with Coarse Sand or Gravel]
    L --> M[Surface Settlement Closed]
    H --> N[Post Treatment SPT and CPT Verification]
```

## 4.2 Blasting Densification — Process Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Investigation SPT and CPT] --> B[Borehole Drilling 75 to 150 mm dia]
    B --> C[Charge Placement ANFO or Slurry]
    C --> D[Stemming with Sand Plug]
    D --> E[Detonation Sequence with Millisecond Delays]
    E --> F[Shock Wave Induces Pore Pressure Spike]
    F --> G[Temporary Liquefaction ru approaches 1]
    G --> H[Soil Grains Rearrange into Denser Packing]
    H --> I[Excess Pore Pressure Dissipates over Hours to Days]
    I --> J{Verification SPT and CPT Pass}
    J -->|No| K[Repeat Blast Round 2 to 5 cycles]
    J -->|Yes| L[Treatment Complete]
    K --> D
```

## 4.3 Comparative Suitability Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Loose Granular Soil Deposit] --> B{Selection Criteria}
    B --> C1[Fines less than 15 percent]
    B --> C2[Fines between 15 and 20 percent]
    B --> C3[Fines greater than 20 percent]
    B --> C4[Saturated Loose Sand Large Area]
    C1 --> D1[Vibro Compaction]
    C2 --> D2[Vibro with Stone Columns Hybrid]
    C3 --> D3[Not Suitable Choose Dynamic Compaction or Deep Mixing]
    C4 --> D4[Blast Densification]
    D1 --> E1[SPT and CPT Verification]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E2[SPT CPT and Shear Wave Velocity Verification]
```

## 4.4 Probe Grid Pattern — Plan View Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Probe at Position 1] --> B[Probe at Position 2]
    B --> C[Probe at Position 3]
    C --> D[Triangular Grid Spacing 1.5 to 3 m]
    D --> E[Overlapping Compaction Zones Form Continuous Densified Mass]
    E --> F[Coverage Uniformity 15 percent Better than Square Grid]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**"State the working principle of Vibro Compaction. Mention the soils for which it is most suitable."** (3 Marks)

### ✅ Model Answer (Point-wise, Board-Exam Style)

1. **Definition:** Vibro Compaction (Vibroflotation) is a deep ground improvement method that densifies loose, granular, cohesionless soils using a depth vibrator (vibroflot) that emits **horizontal vibrations** at **30–50 Hz**. (1 mark)
2. **Working Principle:** A cylindrical probe with eccentric weights is lowered into the ground using self-weight, vibration, and water jetting. The vibrations cause grain rearrangement into a denser packing. The probe is withdrawn in stages (0.3–0.5 m), with **backfill material** added at the surface to fill the resulting crater. (1 mark)
3. **Suitable Soils:** Most suitable for **clean sands and gravelly sands** with **fines content (silt + clay) < 15%**. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** Many students lose this 1 mark by writing "sandy soils" without specifying the **fines limit**. Always quantify.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**"Explain the mechanism of densification by blasting in loose saturated sands."** (3 Marks)

### ✅ Model Answer (Point-wise, Board-Exam Style)

1. **Mechanism:** Blasting densification exploits the principle of **controlled liquefaction**. Explosive charges placed in boreholes within the loose saturated sand are detonated. (1 mark)
2. **Process:** The blast generates **shock waves** that drastically raise the **pore-water pressure** ($u_e$) until $r_u = u_e / \sigma_{vo} \rightarrow 1$. The sand momentarily behaves as a heavy liquid (**liquefaction**). (1 mark)
3. **Reconsolidation:** As the excess pore pressure dissipates over hours/days, grains settle into a **denser packing** under their own weight, increasing relative density and eliminating future liquefaction susceptibility. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "blasting" with "removal of rock" (as in mining). In KTU Ground Improvement context, blasting is for **densification, NOT excavation**. Clearly state that the goal is grain rearrangement, not removal.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Vibro Compaction Focus**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**a) Describe with a neat sketch the equipment and procedure of Vibro Compaction. Discuss the factors affecting the depth and extent of densification.** (7 Marks)

**b) A loose sand deposit 8 m thick has pre-treatment SPT N-value of 7. After Vibro Compaction, the average N-value becomes 30. Estimate the percentage increase in relative density using Skempton's correlation. Comment on the liquefaction resistance. Assume clean sand and $C_N = 1.0$.** (7 Marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Equipment (3 marks):**
- Crane / leader mast (rig frame)
- Vibroflot probe with electric/hydraulic motor (100–200 kW)
- Horizontal shaft with eccentric weights
- Water jets (high pressure 400–800 kPa)
- Extension tubes (slick joints for deeper penetration)
- Power pack & control panel

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Set-up:** Rig the crane at the design grid point.
2. **Penetration:** Lower the vibroflot with water jets ON; the probe sinks under self-weight + vibration + jetting to design depth.
3. **Compaction:** At depth, jets may be reduced; vibration continues for 30–60 sec/level.
4. **Withdrawal in stages:** Probe withdrawn in 0.3–0.5 m increments, each stage vibrated for 30–60 sec.
5. **Backfilling:** Add coarse sand/gravel at the surface crater continuously.
6. **Pattern shift:** Move to the next grid point; triangular or square pattern at 1.5–3 m spacing.

**Factors affecting densification (1 mark):**
- Fines content (> 20% → ineffective)
- Grain size and gradation
- Confining stress (depth of deposit)
- Vibration frequency and amplitude
- Probe spacing and pattern

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $N_1 = 7$, $N_2 = 30$, clean sand, $C_N = 1.0$

**Skempton's Correlation:**

$$D_r = \sqrt{\frac{N_{60}}{60}}$$

**Before Treatment:**

$$D_{r,1} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{60}} = \sqrt{0.1167} \approx 0.342 = 34.2\%$$

**After Treatment:**

$$D_{r,2} = \sqrt{\frac{30}{60}} = \sqrt{0.5000} \approx 0.707 = 70.7\%$$

**Percentage Increase in Relative Density:**

$$\%\text{ Increase} = \frac{D_{r,2} - D_{r,1}}{D_{r,1}} \times 100 = \frac{0.707 - 0.342}{0.342} \times 100 \approx 106.7\%$$

**Comment on Liquefaction Resistance (2 marks):**
- Pre-treatment: $D_r = 34.2\%$ → **loose, highly liquefiable**.
- Post-treatment: $D_r = 70.7\%$ → **medium-dense, significantly improved liquefaction resistance**.
- Cyclic Resistance Ratio (CRR) typically rises from ~0.10 (loose) to ~0.20+ (medium-dense), meaning the soil can now resist **earthquake-induced cyclic shear stresses of twice the magnitude** compared to its original state.

---

### **Question B — Blasting Focus**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**a) Explain the process of densification of loose saturated sand deposits by blasting. Discuss the limitations of the method.** (7 Marks)

**b) A loose saturated sand layer 12 m thick is to be densified by blasting. The charge holes are spaced at 5 m × 5 m in a square pattern. Calculate the charge weight per hole using the empirical formula $W = k \cdot A \cdot d$, where $k = 0.03 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and the charge is placed at $d = 0.7 \times H$. Also state the delay sequence recommendation.** (7 Marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Process of Blasting Densification (5 marks):**
1. **Borehole Drilling:** 75–150 mm dia holes drilled to depth $d = 0.5H$ to $0.7H$.
2. **Charge Placement:** ANFO or slurry explosives placed at the bottom; multiple deck-loading for thick deposits.
3. **Stemming:** Hole sealed above charge with sand plug.
4. **Detonation:** Multiple holes detonated in **delayed sequence** (millisecond delays of 25–500 ms) to control uplift and maximize liquefaction.
5. **Liquefaction:** Shock wave raises pore pressure → $r_u \rightarrow 1$ → temporary liquefaction.
6. **Reconsolidation:** Grains settle in denser packing as pore pressure dissipates (hours/days).
7. **Verification:** SPT, CPT, or $V_s$ tests confirm densification.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Cannot be used **near existing structures** (ground vibration & heave damage).
- Requires **full saturation** of the deposit (ineffective above water table).
- **Quality control is poor** — results are heterogeneous and unpredictable.
- **Safety hazards** — handling, storage, transport of explosives.
- Environmental concerns (noise, flyrock, vibration, water contamination).
- Several blast rounds (3–5) required with waiting periods.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $H = 12 \text{ m}$, square grid $5 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m}$, $k = 0.03 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $d = 0.7H$

**Step 1 — Depth of Charge:**

$$d = 0.7 \times 12 = 8.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Area of Influence per Hole:**

$$A = 5 \times 5 = 25 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Charge Weight per Hole:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= k \cdot A \cdot d \\
&= 0.03 \times 25 \times 8.4 \\
&= 0.03 \times 210 \\
&= 6.30 \text{ kg per hole}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Delay Sequence Recommendation (2 marks):**

A **"ring firing" or "V-pattern" delay sequence** with **millisecond delays of 25–100 ms** between holes is recommended. This ensures:
- Progressive shock wave interference
- Controlled energy release (avoids excessive heave)
- Allows adjacent holes to liquefy the soil mass cumulatively

For 4 holes detonated simultaneously as one round, each delay = 25 ms, total elapsed time = ~100 ms per cycle. Total charge per delay should not exceed **25–30 kg** to control peak particle velocity (PPV < 50 mm/s at nearest structure).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS — Vibro Compaction & Blasting**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "fines content < 15–20%"** for vibro compaction. This single omission can cost **2 marks** in Part A.
> 2. **Confusing "vibroflotation" with "dynamic compaction".** Vibro = depth vibrator; Dynamic = surface weight drop.
> 3. **Not sketching the apparatus** in Part B (a). A neat labelled diagram carries **at least 2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Using wrong N-value correlation** for relative density. Skempton's $D_r = \sqrt{N_{60}/60}$ is for **clean sand** only.
> 5. **Confusing blasting with rock excavation blasting.** In ground improvement, blasting = **densification**, not fragmentation.
> 6. **Skipping the delay-sequence discussion** in blasting answers. KTU examiners specifically test for this.
> 7. **Using `|x|` notation** in tables or equations — the pipe character breaks the markdown table structure. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 8. **Writing answers in paragraph form** — KTU valuation expects **bullet points / numbered steps** for full credit.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vibro Compaction & Blasting**

- ✅ **Vibro Compaction** uses a **vibroflot** with **horizontal eccentric vibrations** at **30–50 Hz**.
- ✅ Most effective for **clean, granular, cohesionless soils** with **fines < 15–20%**.
- ✅ Maximum effective depth ≈ **30–40 m**.
- ✅ Probe is **lowered with water jetting**, **vibrated in stages**, and **withdrawn in 0.3–0.5 m steps**.
- ✅ **Backfill with coarse material** is essential at the surface crater.
- ✅ **Triangular grid pattern** at 1.5–3.0 m spacing gives 15% better coverage than square.
- ✅ **Centrifugal force** $F = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$; **Amplitude** $a = m_e \cdot r / M$.
- ✅ **Blasting** uses explosive charges at $d = 0.5H$ to $0.7H$ in **saturated, loose, cohesionless** sand.
- ✅ Mechanism = **controlled liquefaction** ($r_u \rightarrow 1$) followed by **reconsolidation**.
- ✅ **Stemming** is mandatory to prevent blow-out; **delay sequencing** controls energy release.
- ✅ Empirical charge weight: $W = k \cdot A \cdot d$ with $k \approx 0.02$–$0.05$ kg/m³.
- ✅ **Multiple blast rounds (3–5)** required for full densification.
- ✅ **Skempton's correlation** $D_r = \sqrt{N_{60}/60}$ is the KTU exam standard for clean sand.
- ✅ **Cannot be used near structures** (vibration limits) — keep setback distance.
- ✅ Above the water table, blasting is **ineffective** — only vibro compaction (with water) works.
- ✅ Verification: **SPT, CPT, or shear wave velocity ($V_s$)** before and after treatment.
- ✅ For fines > 20%, **neither method works** — use dynamic compaction or deep mixing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibratory Probe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET633 - GROUND IMPROVEMENT TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibratory Probe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Vibratory Probe (Vibro Probe)**
> A deep ground improvement technique used for the in-situ densification of loose, granular, cohesionless soils (typically clean sands and sandy gravels) by inserting a cylindrical probe equipped with a horizontally vibrating eccentric weight assembly. The cyclic shear strains induced in the surrounding soil mass cause the grain skeleton to collapse into a denser packing configuration, thereby increasing relative density, shear strength, and liquefaction resistance.

The technique is formally classified under **Deep Vibratory Compaction Methods** in modern ground engineering practice, and is a direct predecessor / companion method to Vibroflotation (the difference being probe geometry, frequency range, and the use of water/air jetting).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Shaking a Jar of Sugar to Pack It Tighter"**
> Imagine a glass jar loosely filled with sugar. If you tap the side of the jar repeatedly, the sugar grains shuffle past one another, the air gaps disappear, and the level of sugar drops — the same mass now occupies a smaller volume. The **Vibratory Probe** does precisely this, but deep underground. A vibrating poker is pushed into loose sand, and the **horizontal shaking** makes the sand grains re-arrange into a tight, dense mass. The result is ground that is far stronger, less compressible, and resistant to liquefaction during earthquakes.

## 1.3 Salient Physical Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| **Probe diameter** | **300 mm to 400 mm** |
| **Probe length** | **3 m to 5 m** (modular extensions for deeper work) |
| **Vibration frequency** | **1500 rpm to 3000 rpm (25 Hz to 50 Hz)** |
| **Eccentric moment** | **0.05 N·m to 0.5 N·m** |
| **Centrifugal force** | **100 kN to 500 kN** |
| **Effective depth of treatment** | **Up to 30 m** (with extensions) |
| **Probe spacing (triangular pattern)** | **1.5 m to 3.5 m centre-to-centre** |
| **Water jet pressure (assisted penetration)** | **400 kPa to 1000 kPa** |
| **Power rating of vibrator motor** | **30 kW to 120 kW** |
| **Treatment cycle time per probe** | **10 min to 30 min** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the eccentric weight matters:**
> The probe contains a rotating eccentric mass whose rotation frequency $\omega$ generates a centrifugal force $F = m_e \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ (where $m_e$ = eccentric mass and $e$ = eccentricity). This is the engine that drives the densification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The vibratory probe densifies granular soils by exploiting the **threshold shear strain** concept of soil mechanics. The mechanism unfolds as follows:

- **Step 1 — Penetration Phase:** The probe is suspended from a crawler crane or a purpose-built mast. It penetrates the ground under its own weight, assisted by **water jetting at the tip** (liquefying the soil locally) and by the **self-weight + vibration** combined effect.
- **Step 2 — Vibration Activation:** Once the design depth is reached, the eccentric motor is engaged. Horizontal vibrations radiate outward radially from the probe axis.
- **Step 3 — Particle Rearrangement (Densification):** Soil grains at the threshold cyclic shear strain ($\gamma_{thr} \approx 10^{-2}\%$ to $10^{-1}\%$) lose their inter-particle friction momentarily and slide into a tighter packing. Excess pore-water pressure momentarily develops in saturated sands, then dissipates rapidly due to the high permeability of granular soils.
- **Step 4 — Backfilling (Top Feed / Bottom Feed):** Granular material (sand, gravel, crushed stone) is added at the surface or through a conduit at the probe tip to compensate for the volume reduction (settlement crater) and to enhance the densification column.
- **Step 5 — Withdrawal Phase:** The probe is slowly withdrawn in stages (typically 0.3 m to 0.5 m lifts), with vibration maintained, while backfill is continuously added.
- **Step 6 — Repeat at Spacing:** The process is repeated at the design grid spacing (triangular or square) until the desired areal extent is treated.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Zone of Influence" Concept**
> Each probe influences a cylindrical zone around it. The radius of influence (typically **0.6 m to 1.5 m**) depends on soil type, probe power, and treatment duration. Probe spacing is designed such that the zones **overlap** to ensure uniform densification.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

- **Why vibration works:** Cyclic shearing at the correct amplitude mobilises grain rearrangement without causing soil to dilate (which would happen under static monotonic shearing of dense sand).
- **How the densification is measured:** Pre- and post-treatment SPT-N values, CPT tip resistance ($q_c$), or shear wave velocity ($V_s$) measurements confirm the improvement.
- **Target outcome:** Achieve a design **relative density $D_r \geq 70\%$ to 75\%$** for liquefaction-resistant ground (per Seed-Idriss criteria).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 — Vibratory Probe Design**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e_0}{e_{max} - e_{min}}$ | Relative density of soil (pre- or post-treatment) |
| 2 | $F = m_e \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ | Centrifugal force generated by the eccentric vibrator |
| 3 | $N_{1,60} = C_N \cdot N_{field}$ | SPT-N corrected for overburden pressure (with hammer efficiency & rod corrections) |
| 4 | $C_N = \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)^{0.5}$ | Overburden correction factor (Liao & Whitman) — $P_a = 100$ kPa |
| 5 | $CRR_{7.5} = \dfrac{1}{34 - (N_1)_{60,cs}} + \dfrac{(N_1)_{60,cs}}{135} + \dfrac{50}{\left[10 \cdot (N_1)_{60,cs} + 45\right]^2} - \dfrac{1}{200}$ | Cyclic Resistance Ratio for $M_w = 7.5$ earthquake (Seed-Idriss-Idriss simplified procedure) |
| 6 | $FS_{liq} = \dfrac{CRR}{CSR}$ | Factor of Safety against liquefaction (typically $FS \geq 1.2$ required) |
| 7 | $CSR = 0.65 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_v}{\sigma'_v} \cdot \dfrac{a_{max}}{g} \cdot r_d$ | Cyclic Stress Ratio induced by design earthquake |
| 8 | $V_{s1,cs} = K_{v} \cdot \left[(N_1)_{60,cs}\right]^{0.25}$ | Shear wave velocity correlation with cleaned SPT-N |
| 9 | $s = 1.5 \cdot r_i$ | Triangular probe spacing, where $r_i$ = radius of influence |
| 10 | $V_{improve} = A_{cell} \cdot \Delta h \cdot n_{holes}$ | Total volume of soil improved (for QA estimation) |
| 11 | $\gamma_{thr} = 10^{-2}\%$ to $10^{-1}\%$ | Threshold shear strain for granular rearrangement |
| 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{treated}}{A_{total}}$ | Treatment efficiency / coverage ratio |

> [!TIP]
> **Units discipline (KTU Examiner's expectation):**
> - $F$ (centrifugal force) → **kN** (multiply by $10^{-3}$ if N)
> - $\sigma_v$ and $\sigma'_v$ → **kPa** consistently
> - $V_s$ → **m/s**
> - $D_r$ → **dimensionless** (always in % when stated)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Specific Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Liquefaction mitigation** | Reclaiming land in seismic zones (e.g., Niigata 1964, Kobe 1995 post-recovery projects) |
| **Port & harbour engineering** | Densifying hydraulic fill behind quay walls |
| **Highway & rail embankments** | Treatment of loose sandy subgrade |
| **Tank & silo foundations** | Reducing settlement under cyclic loading |
| **Offshore wind turbine foundations** | Improving monopile bearing capacity in sandy seabeds |
| **Reclaimed land sites** | Singapore, Dubai, Hong Kong — land reclamation densification |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Centrifugal Force Generated by the Eccentric Weight

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle (Newtonian mechanics):**
> An unbalanced mass rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ about an axis generates a centripetal reaction, which manifests as a vibratory force on the housing of the motor.

**Step 1 — Express the centripetal acceleration.**
The eccentric mass $m_e$ rotating at radius $e$ experiences centripetal acceleration:

$$
a_c = e \cdot \omega^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law.**
The centrifugal force (the equal and opposite reaction transmitted to the probe housing) is:

$$
F = m_e \cdot a_c = m_e \cdot e \cdot \omega^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the angular velocity in terms of rotational speed.**
If the motor rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ rad/s.

$$
F = m_e \cdot e \cdot \left(\dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical simplification.**

$$
F = m_e \cdot e \cdot \dfrac{4 \pi^2 N^2}{3600} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{900} \cdot m_e \cdot e \cdot N^2
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**

> **Given:** $m_e = 10$ kg, $e = 0.05$ m, $N = 1800$ rpm.
> **Find:** Centrifugal force $F$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \dfrac{\pi^2}{900} \times 10 \times 0.05 \times (1800)^2 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{9.8696}{900} \times 0.5 \times 3{,}240{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 0.01097 \times 1{,}620{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 17{,}766 \text{ N} \approx 17.77 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the typical **100 kN to 500 kN** range when $m_e$ and $e$ are scaled up industrially (e.g., $m_e = 50$ kg, $e = 0.1$ m, $N = 3000$ rpm).

## 3.2 Derivation II — SPT Overburden Correction (Liao & Whitman, 1986)

**Step 1 — Define raw field SPT-N value.**
The field N-value is obtained from the standard penetration test at depth $z$ with effective overburden stress $\sigma'_{v0}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the correction factor.**
Liao & Whitman proposed a simple power-law correction:

$$
C_N = \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)^{0.5}
$$

where $P_a = 100$ kPa is the atmospheric pressure reference, and $C_N \leq 1.7$ is the upper bound commonly enforced.

**Step 3 — Compute the corrected SPT-N value.**

$$
(N_1)_{60} = C_N \cdot N_{field}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.**

> **Given:** $\sigma'_{v0} = 50$ kPa at depth of interest, $N_{field} = 12$ blows.
> **Find:** $(N_1)_{60}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_N &= \left(\dfrac{100}{50}\right)^{0.5} = (2)^{0.5} = 1.414 \\[4pt]
(N_1)_{60} &= 1.414 \times 12 = 16.97 \approx 17
\end{aligned}
$$

This corrected value is the **input** to liquefaction assessment using the CRR formula.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Factor of Safety Against Liquefaction

**Step 1 — Compute the Cyclic Stress Ratio (CSR) induced by the design earthquake.**

$$
CSR = 0.65 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_v}{\sigma'_v} \cdot \dfrac{a_{max}}{g} \cdot r_d
$$

where $r_d$ is the depth reduction factor (commonly $r_d = 1.0 - 0.00765 \cdot z$ for $z \leq 9.15$ m, after Seed & Idriss).

**Step 2 — Compute the Cyclic Resistance Ratio (CRR) of the soil using the corrected SPT.**

$$
CRR_{7.5} = \dfrac{1}{34 - (N_1)_{60,cs}} + \dfrac{(N_1)_{60,cs}}{135} + \dfrac{50}{\left[10 \cdot (N_1)_{60,cs} + 45\right]^2} - \dfrac{1}{200}
$$

where $(N_1)_{60,cs} = (N_1)_{60} + \Delta(N_1)_{60}$ (fines correction applied).

**Step 3 — Scale for design earthquake magnitude (Magnitude Scaling Factor MSF).**

$$
CRR_M = CRR_{7.5} \cdot MSF
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Factor of Safety.**

$$
FS_{liq} = \dfrac{CRR_M}{CSR}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**

> **Given:** Depth $z = 6$ m, $\sigma_v = 110$ kPa, $\sigma'_v = 60$ kPa, $a_{max} = 0.20g$, $M_w = 7.0$, $(N_1)_{60,cs} = 15$.
> **Find:** $FS_{liq}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CSR &= 0.65 \times \dfrac{110}{60} \times 0.20 \times \left[1.0 - 0.00765 \times 6\right] \\[4pt]
&= 0.65 \times 1.833 \times 0.20 \times 0.9541 \\[4pt]
&= 0.2273
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRR_{7.5} &= \dfrac{1}{34 - 15} + \dfrac{15}{135} + \dfrac{50}{[10(15) + 45]^2} - \dfrac{1}{200} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{19} + 0.1111 + \dfrac{50}{[195]^2} - 0.005 \\[4pt]
&= 0.0526 + 0.1111 + 0.00131 - 0.005 \\[4pt]
&= 0.1600
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MSF \text{ (for } M_w = 7.0\text{)} &\approx 1.19 \\[4pt]
CRR_M &= 0.1600 \times 1.19 = 0.1904 \\[4pt]
FS_{liq} &= \dfrac{0.1904}{0.2273} = 0.838
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $FS_{liq} < 1.0$ → **Soil is liquefiable as-is** → Vibratory probe treatment required to raise $(N_1)_{60,cs}$ to a value that yields $FS_{liq} \geq 1.2$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Probe Spacing Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Decision Tree for Spacing**
> Use the matrix below to select the appropriate grid pattern.

| Soil Type | Radius of Influence $r_i$ (m) | Recommended Pattern | Spacing $s$ (m) | Treatment Duration per Point (min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clean medium sand | 1.0 to 1.5 | Triangular (equilateral) | 1.5 to 2.25 | 15 to 20 |
| Fine sand (with silt < 5%) | 0.8 to 1.2 | Triangular | 1.2 to 1.8 | 20 to 25 |
| Silty sand (5%–12% fines) | 0.6 to 1.0 | Triangular + secondary pass | 0.9 to 1.5 | 25 to 30 |
| Sandy gravel | 1.2 to 1.8 | Triangular | 1.8 to 2.7 | 10 to 15 |
| Loose reclaimed fill | 0.8 to 1.2 | Square (initial) + triangular (secondary) | 1.2 to 1.8 | 20 to 30 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Spacing Pitfall:** If the spacing is **too large**, untreated "soft pockets" remain between probe locations — the most common cause of post-treatment liquefaction failures. If **too small**, the project becomes uneconomical.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

The following block diagram illustrates the functional components and operational flow of a Vibratory Probe system, mapped from the equipment to the soil response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Crawler Crane / Mast] --> A2[Probe Suspension Cable]
    A2 --> A3[Probe Assembly]
    A3 --> A4[Eccentric Vibrator Motor]
    A4 --> A5[High-Speed Rotation of Eccentric Mass]
    A5 --> A6[Centrifugal Force Generation]
    A6 --> A7[Horizontal Cyclic Shear Waves]
    A7 --> A8[Surrounding Granular Soil]
    A8 --> A9{Grain Reorientation Threshold Reached?}
    A9 -->|Yes| A10[Particle Packing Densification]
    A9 -->|No| A11[Increase Power / Duration]
    A10 --> A12[Excess PWP Dissipation]
    A12 --> A13[Increased Relative Density]
    A13 --> A14[Improved Bearing Capacity and Liquefaction Resistance]

    P1[Granular Backfill Stockpile] --> P2[Hopper / Conveyor]
    P2 --> P3[Backfill Feed at Crater]
    P3 --> A3

    W1[Water Pump] --> W2[Jet Pipes at Probe Tip]
    W2 --> A3

    QC1[Pre-Treatment SPT / CPT] --> QC2[Design Spacing and Pattern]
    QC2 --> A1
    QC3[Post-Treatment SPT / CPT] --> QC4{Improvement Achieved?}
    QC4 -->|Yes| QC5[Acceptance and Handover]
    QC4 -->|No| QC6[Secondary Pass / Closer Spacing]
    QC6 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Probe Components (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Top Connector to Crane Cable] --> B2[Upper Tube Section]
    B2 --> B3[Extension Rods - Modular]
    B3 --> B4[Vibrator Housing]
    B4 --> B5[Eccentric Weight Assembly]
    B5 --> B6[Lower Tube Section]
    B6 --> B7[Water Jet Nozzles at Tip]
    B6 --> B8[Bottom-Feed Port for Backfill]
    B8 --> B9[Surrounding Granular Soil Mass]
    B5 --> B10[Electric / Hydraulic Power Cable from Generator]
    B10 --> B4
```

## 4.3 Treatment Grid Pattern (Triangular Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Design Treatment Area] --> G2[Set Triangular Grid]
    G2 --> G3[Probe Point P1 - First Insertion]
    G3 --> G4[Probe Point P2 - Offset by s]
    G4 --> G5[Probe Point P3 - Completes Equilateral Triangle]
    G5 --> G6[Continue Across Grid]
    G6 --> G7[Quality Control Testing]
    G7 --> G8{Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    G8 -->|Yes| G9[Project Complete]
    G8 -->|No| G10[Secondary Treatment Pass]
    G10 --> G2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams in the exam:** When KTU asks for a "sketch of vibratory probe," the examiner expects: (i) labelled probe with motor at top, (ii) eccentric mass indication, (iii) jetting tubes, (iv) backfill feed, and (v) zone of influence around the probe. Memorise the components in order.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the "Vibratory Probe" method of ground improvement. State any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Vibratory Probe** is a deep ground improvement technique used for densification of loose, granular, cohesionless soils (clean sands and sandy gravels). A cylindrical probe containing a horizontally rotating eccentric weight is lowered into the ground, typically with the assistance of water jetting. The horizontal vibrations induce cyclic shear strains in the surrounding soil, causing grain reorientation and densification, often with the addition of granular backfill at the surface.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. **Ineffective in cohesive soils** (clays, silts with high plasticity) — the pore-water pressure cannot dissipate rapidly, and grain rearrangement does not occur.
2. **Restricted to granular soils** below the water table or in dry condition; cohesionless fines are difficult to densify.
3. **Depth limitation** — practical limit of around 30 m; very deep treatment requires multiple extensions and becomes uneconomical.
4. **Noise and vibration nuisance** to adjacent structures and inhabitants in urban settings.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the "zone of influence" in vibratory probe treatment? How does it govern the choice of probe spacing?**

**Model Answer:**

The **zone of influence** is the cylindrical volume of soil around each probe within which effective densification is achieved due to the radiating shear waves from the vibrating eccentric mass. Its radius $r_i$ typically ranges from **0.6 m to 1.5 m**, depending on soil gradation, probe power, and treatment duration.

**Governance of probe spacing:**
The probes are placed in a **triangular (equilateral) or square grid** such that the zones of influence of adjacent probes **overlap** to ensure uniform treatment. For a triangular pattern, the spacing is commonly:

$$
s = 1.5 \cdot r_i
$$

This overlap eliminates untreated pockets and ensures the design relative density (typically $D_r \geq 70\%$) is achieved throughout the treated area.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has internal choice. Below, two alternatives (Question A and Question B) are provided so the student can practise either.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the various components and working of a **Vibratory Probe** used for ground improvement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **design procedure** for vibratory probe treatment, including the determination of probe spacing and the quality control measures to be adopted. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

The vibratory probe system consists of the following components:

1. **Probe Assembly:** A cylindrical steel tube, typically 300–400 mm in diameter and 3–5 m long (modular extensions for greater depths). It houses the vibrator motor at its lower end.

2. **Eccentric Vibrator Motor:** A high-speed electric or hydraulic motor with an eccentric weight that rotates at 1500–3000 rpm, generating centrifugal forces of 100–500 kN.

3. **Water Jetting System:** Pumps and pipes that deliver water at 400–1000 kPa pressure to nozzles at the probe tip, assisting penetration by locally liquefying the soil.

4. **Backfill Feed System:** A hopper and conveyor (or bottom-feed conduit) supplying granular material (sand, gravel) to fill the settlement crater at the surface.

5. **Crane or Mast:** A crawler crane or purpose-built leader mast (15–30 m tall) that suspends and positions the probe vertically.

6. **Power Supply & Control Unit:** Diesel generator (typically 100–250 kVA) and control panel to regulate motor speed, water flow, and lift rate.

**Working Principle:**

- The probe is positioned vertically at the design grid point.
- Water jets at the tip are activated, and the probe is allowed to penetrate under self-weight + vibration to the design depth.
- At depth, the eccentric motor is run at full power, and the probe is slowly withdrawn in 0.3–0.5 m stages.
- Granular backfill is added at the crater during withdrawal.
- The process is repeated across the design grid.

**Neat sketch** (to be drawn in the exam):

> ```
>        CRANE CABLE
>            |
>   ======== | ========   ← Probe (top connector)
>       |          |
>       |  MOTOR   |     ← Eccentric weight housing
>       |   ●      |     ← Rotating eccentric
>       |          |
>       |   TUBE   |     ← Probe tube
>       |          |
>   ====|==JET==== |     ← Water jet nozzle
>            |          ← Penetration direction
>   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  ← Granular soil
>   ░░░ (Zone of influence) ░░░
> ```

**Key Marking Points (7 marks):**
- Components listed: 4 marks
- Working sequence: 2 marks
- Sketch: 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Design Procedure for Vibratory Probe Treatment:**

1. **Site Investigation:**
   - Boreholes, SPT, CPT, and laboratory index tests (gradation, fines content).
   - Establish pre-treatment $D_r$, SPT-N, $q_c$, and $V_s$ profiles.

2. **Establish Design Criteria:**
   - Target $D_r$ (typically $\geq 70\%$ to 75\%$).
   - Target $(N_1)_{60,cs}$ for liquefaction resistance (e.g., $(N_1)_{60,cs} \geq 20$ for $M_w = 7.5$, $\sigma'_v = 100$ kPa).
   - Design Factor of Safety against liquefaction $\geq 1.2$.

3. **Determine Radius of Influence:**
   - From prior experience or trial section: $r_i = 0.6$ to $1.5$ m.

4. **Compute Probe Spacing:**
   - Triangular pattern: $s = 1.5 \cdot r_i$.
   - Example: if $r_i = 1.2$ m, then $s = 1.8$ m.

5. **Estimate Treatment Duration:**
   - 10–30 minutes per point (function of soil type and depth).

6. **Compute Backfill Quantity:**
   - Volume of densification column $\times$ number of probes.

7. **Trial Section:**
   - Execute 5–10 trial probes and verify improvement with SPT/CPT.
   - Adjust spacing if required.

**Quality Control (QC) Measures:**

- **Pre-treatment baseline** SPT/CPT at multiple locations.
- **Post-treatment verification** at the same locations (compare $(N_1)_{60}$).
- **Continuous monitoring** of probe depth, vibration time, water pressure, and backfill quantity.
- **Settlement monitoring** of ground surface.
- **Statistical sampling:** Test 1 in every 25 to 50 probe locations randomly.
- **Acceptance criteria:** $D_r \geq 75\%$, $q_c \geq 15$ MPa, $FS_{liq} \geq 1.2$.

**Key Marking Points (7 marks):**
- Design procedure steps: 4 marks
- QC measures: 2 marks
- Justification / formulas: 1 mark

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question A:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Confusing Vibratory Probe with Vibroflotation** — remember: Vibratory Probe uses a slender probe with dry/wet top-feed, Vibroflotation uses a larger-diameter vibroflot with bottom-feed and high-volume water jets.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention backfill** — without backfill, the surface crater is left untreated.
> 3. **Skipping the trial section** — the trial section is mandatory and is worth at least 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of densification** by vibratory probe in granular soils. How is the effectiveness measured in the field? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A loose sand deposit at a site has the following data:
- Depth = 8 m, $\sigma_v = 144$ kPa, $\sigma'_v = 80$ kPa
- Corrected SPT-N value $(N_1)_{60,cs} = 12$
- Design earthquake: $M_w = 7.5$, $a_{max} = 0.25g$
- Depth reduction factor $r_d = 0.95$

**Compute the Factor of Safety against liquefaction. Comment on the need for vibratory probe treatment.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Mechanism of Densification:**

The vibratory probe densifies granular soils through a sequence of coupled mechanisms:

1. **Cyclic Shear Strain Generation:** The horizontal vibration from the eccentric mass produces a cyclic shear strain field that radiates outward from the probe. The amplitude of shear strain decays with radial distance.

2. **Threshold Strain Mobilisation:** When the cyclic shear strain exceeds a **threshold value ($\gamma_{thr} \approx 10^{-2}\%$ to $10^{-1}\%$)** for granular soils, the inter-particle contacts begin to slip, and the soil structure loses its stability.

3. **Grain Rearrangement:** Under sustained cyclic excitation, grains slide, rotate, and fall into neighbouring voids. The packing density increases as the soil transitions from a loose to a medium-dense or dense state.

4. **Pore-Water Pressure Build-up and Dissipation:** In saturated sands, cyclic shearing generates **excess pore-water pressure ($u_e$)**. However, due to the high permeability of clean granular soils, $u_e$ dissipates rapidly (almost instantaneously), preventing the soil from reaching the **initial liquefaction** state. This is in stark contrast to silty sands, where dissipation is slow and liquefaction becomes a real risk.

5. **Volume Reduction (Densification):** The rearrangement produces a measurable **surface settlement** (typically 5% to 15% of the treated depth), confirming the volume reduction and densification.

**Field Measurement of Effectiveness:**

| Method | Measured Parameter | Acceptance Criterion (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **SPT** (pre vs. post) | $N$ value (blow count) | $(N_1)_{60,cs} \geq 20$ to 25 |
| **CPT** (pre vs. post) | Tip resistance $q_c$ | $q_c \geq 15$ MPa |
| **Shear wave velocity ($V_s$)** | $V_s$ via seismic CPT or downhole | $V_{s1} \geq 200$ m/s |
| **Surface settlement** | Total settlement at crater | 5% to 15% of treated depth |
| **Pressuremeter** | Limit pressure $p_L$ | $p_L \geq 2 \times$ pre-treatment value |

**Key Marking Points (7 marks):**
- Mechanism (threshold strain, grain rearrangement, PWP): 4 marks
- Field measurement methods: 3 marks

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute Cyclic Stress Ratio (CSR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CSR &= 0.65 \times \dfrac{\sigma_v}{\sigma'_v} \times \dfrac{a_{max}}{g} \times r_d \\[4pt]
&= 0.65 \times \dfrac{144}{80} \times 0.25 \times 0.95 \\[4pt]
&= 0.65 \times 1.8 \times 0.25 \times 0.95 \\[4pt]
&= 0.2779
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cyclic Resistance Ratio (CRR) for $M_w = 7.5$.**

Using the simplified Seed-Idriss formula with $(N_1)_{60,cs} = 12$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRR_{7.5} &= \dfrac{1}{34 - 12} + \dfrac{12}{135} + \dfrac{50}{[10(12) + 45]^2} - \dfrac{1}{200} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{22} + 0.0889 + \dfrac{50}{[165]^2} - 0.005 \\[4pt]
&= 0.0455 + 0.0889 + 0.00184 - 0.005 \\[4pt]
&= 0.1312
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Factor of Safety.**

Since the design earthquake is already $M_w = 7.5$, MSF = 1.0:

$$
FS_{liq} = \dfrac{CRR_{7.5}}{CSR} = \dfrac{0.1312}{0.2779} = 0.472
$$

**Step 4 — Comment.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Engineering Interpretation:**
> $FS_{liq} = 0.472 \ll 1.0$. The soil is **highly susceptible to liquefaction** under the design earthquake. The as-is condition is unacceptable. **Vibratory probe treatment is mandatory** to raise $(N_1)_{60,cs}$ from 12 to a target value that gives $FS_{liq} \geq 1.2$.

**Step 5 — Reverse-calculate the required $(N_1)_{60,cs}$.**

Setting $FS_{liq} = 1.2$ and $CSR = 0.2779$:

$$
CRR_{required} = 1.2 \times 0.2779 = 0.3335
$$

By iterative trial (or a design chart), the required $(N_1)_{60,cs} \approx 22$ to 25. Hence, the vibratory probe must be designed to **nearly double** the corrected SPT-N — achievable in clean sands with a properly executed triangular grid of 1.5–2.0 m spacing and 20–25 minutes of vibration per point.

**Key Marking Points (7 marks):**
- Correct CSR calculation: 2 marks
- Correct CRR calculation: 2 marks
- FS calculation: 1 mark
- Engineering comment and design recommendation: 2 marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question B:**
> 1. **MSF application:** If the design earthquake is **not** $M_w = 7.5$, students forget to apply the Magnitude Scaling Factor. The CRR formula given above is **for $M_w = 7.5$ only**.
> 2. **Fines correction:** If the problem gives a **clean sand equivalent** value, the formula applies directly. If raw $(N_1)_{60}$ is given with fines, students must first apply $\Delta(N_1)_{60}$ — losing 1 mark if forgotten.
> 3. **Engineering comment is mandatory** — a numerical answer without a concluding design recommendation is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vibratory Probe**

- **Definition:** Deep ground improvement for **granular, cohesionless soils** using a probe with a horizontally vibrating eccentric weight.
- **Mechanism:** Cyclic shear strain → threshold $\gamma_{thr} \approx 10^{-2}\%$ to $10^{-1}\%$ → grain rearrangement → densification.
- **Probe diameter:** 300–400 mm; **Frequency:** 25–50 Hz; **Centrifugal force:** 100–500 kN.
- **Centrifugal force formula:** $F = m_e \cdot e \cdot \omega^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2}{900} \cdot m_e \cdot e \cdot N^2$.
- **Radius of influence:** 0.6–1.5 m; **Spacing (triangular):** $s = 1.5 \cdot r_i$.
- **Backfill:** Granular sand/gravel added at surface or via bottom-feed.
- **Depth of treatment:** Up to 30 m (modular extensions).
- **Limitations:** Not effective in clays; restricted to granular soils; noise in urban areas; depth limits.
- **QC methods:** SPT, CPT, $V_s$, surface settlement, pressuremeter.
- **Liquefaction verification:** $FS_{liq} = CRR / CSR \geq 1.2$.
- **Required SPT after treatment:** $(N_1)_{60,cs} \geq 20$ to 25 for $M_w = 7.5$ design.
- **Confusion guard:** Vibratory Probe ≠ Vibroflotation — different probe geometry and feed systems.
- **Examiner's hot keywords:** "Threshold shear strain," "Zone of influence," "Triangular grid," "Backfill," "Relative density $\geq 70\%$," "Seed-Idriss simplified procedure."
- **Real-world projects to cite:** Singapore Changi (reclamation), Kobe post-1995, Dubai Palm Jumeirah (fill densification).
- **Always** end numerical problems with an **engineering comment** — KTU examiners reward interpretation marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibro Displacement Compaction – Displacement Piles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET633 - GROUND IMPROVEMENT TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibro Displacement Compaction – Displacement Piles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vibro Displacement Compaction** is a deep ground improvement technique that uses a depth vibrator (commonly called a *vibroflot*) to densify granular, cohesionless soils by means of horizontal vibrations combined with the introduction of granular backfill material. The vibrator penetrates the ground under its own weight assisted by vibration, displacing the in-situ soil laterally and simultaneously compacting the surrounding ground. When the technique is executed with the explicit objective of forming load-bearing columns of granular material (sand, gravel, or crushed stone) driven into the ground without excavation, the resulting structural elements are termed **Displacement Piles** (also referred to as *Vibro Displacement Piles* or *Sand Compaction Piles* when sand is the infill material).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In Module 3, *Vibro Displacement* is distinguished from *Vibro Replacement* (stone columns) by the fact that in vibro displacement, the in-situ soil is **pushed aside and densified**, whereas in vibro replacement, the in-situ soil is **displaced and replaced** with imported granular material. The "displacement pile" represents the intermediate-to-stiff column of densified native soil and granular infill left behind after probe withdrawal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine pushing a vibrating electric toothbrush straight down into a cup of dry rice. Three things happen:

1. The rice grains **jump and rearrange** into a tightly packed configuration around the shaft.
2. The toothbrush shaft itself **creates a cavity** that can be filled with new material.
3. When the toothbrush is withdrawn, a **dense cylindrical zone** of compacted rice remains, reinforced where backfill was added.

Replace "rice" with "loose sandy soil" and "toothbrush" with a "vibroflot" — and you have the exact mechanism of **Vibro Displacement Compaction**. The vibration liquefies (momentarily) the granular matrix, allowing grains to slide into a denser packing arrangement, while the probe itself displaces soil sideways to make room for either compaction or imported aggregate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Construction Analogy:** This is the same principle by which a vibrating pile driver sinks a steel casing into the ground with far less force than a non-vibrating hammer. The vibrations momentarily reduce inter-particle friction, allowing the pile to "swim" downward. Vibro displacement uses this same physical principle for ground densification.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following metrics govern the technique in KTU-board standard practice:

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Vibroflot diameter** | 300 mm – 500 mm (typical: 380 mm) |
| **Operating frequency** | 30 Hz – 50 Hz (typical: 30 Hz) |
| **Amplitude** | 2 mm – 10 mm |
| **Centrifugal force** | 100 kN – 500 kN |
| **Probe length** | 3 m – 30 m |
| **Hole spacing (triangular grid)** | 1.5 m – 3.0 m |
| **Depth of treatment** | Up to 30 m (limited by crane capacity) |
| **Required SPT N-value (pre-treatment)** | N < 15 (loose to medium-loose) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suitability Window:** Vibro displacement is **most effective in granular soils with fines content (passing 75µm sieve) < 10–15%**. Soils with higher silt/clay content cannot be effectively densified because the vibrations dissipate through the cohesive matrix.

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (For KTU Exam Quick Recall)

- **Dry Process (Bottom Feed):** Used above the water table. Granular backfill is fed through a tremie pipe at the tip of the vibroflot.
- **Wet Process (Top Feed):** Used below the water table. Water jets (termed *vibro jets*) assist penetration, and granular material is fed from the surface.
- **Vibro Compaction:** No backfill — purely densification of native soil.
- **Vibro Replacement:** Full replacement with imported stone (stone columns).
- **Vibro Displacement (Displacement Piles):** Hybrid — densification + backfill forming a column.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

The vibro displacement process unfolds in **four operational phases**. Each phase has a specific geotechnical objective:

### **Phase 1: Penetration (Sinking the Probe)**

- The vibroflot, attached to a crane or rig, is positioned vertically at the design point.
- Vibration is initiated, and the probe **self-penetrates** under its own weight + dynamic forces.
- In wet processes, **water jets at the tip** fluidize the soil ahead, easing penetration.
- The probe advances to the **design depth of treatment** (typically up to a dense/competent stratum).
