> **Why this works:** Vibrations reduce inter-particle friction in granular soils (a phenomenon called *cyclic liquefaction* or *shear thinning*). The granular skeleton momentarily behaves like a viscous fluid, allowing rapid penetration with minimal resistance.

### **Phase 2: Compaction (Lateral Densification)**

- Once at design depth, the probe is **withdrawn in incremental stages** (typically 0.3 m to 0.5 m lifts).
- At each stage, the probe is held momentarily (residence time 30–60 seconds) while vibration continues.
- The horizontal vibrations **radiate outward** from the probe, densifying a cylindrical zone of surrounding soil.
- In the wet method, the **vibroflot creates a crater** at the surface, and granular backfill is added.

> **Why this works:** The vibration energy dissipates radially. Grains rearrange from loose packing (relative density ~ 30–40%) to dense packing (relative density ~ 70–85%). Each withdrawal-reinsertion cycle densifies one "lift."

### **Phase 3: Backfilling (Forming the Displacement Pile)**

- Granular material (sand, gravel, crushed stone) is introduced into the annular space around the probe.
- The vibrator **re-penetrates** the backfilled material, compacting it in stages.
- The result is a **dense column of granular material** locked into the in-situ soil.
- This column is the **"displacement pile"** — a non-structural ground improvement element.

> **Why this works:** The backfill creates a stiff, high-permeability column that reinforces the surrounding densified soil, redistributes loads, accelerates consolidation (drainage path), and reduces settlement.

### **Phase 4: Quality Verification**

- **Cone Penetration Tests (CPT)** or **Standard Penetration Tests (SPT)** are conducted at midpoints between probes.
- **Target SPT N-value:** Typically N ≥ 15–20 for design verification.
- **Plate load tests** may be performed on individual displacement piles for bearing capacity validation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are high-yield for KTU board exams. The unit conversions and applicability windows are critical for full marks.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Applicability / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equivalent radius of compacted zone** | $r_e = 1.5 \times r_v$ | m | $r_v$ = vibroflot radius; empirical |
| 2 | **Area replacement ratio** | $a_s = A_c / A_e$ | — | $A_c$ = pile area, $A_e$ = tributary area |
| 3 | **Tributary area (triangular grid)** | $A_e = \sqrt{3}/4 \cdot s^2$ | m² | $s$ = probe spacing |
| 4 | **Tributary area (square grid)** | $A_e = s^2$ | m² | $s$ = probe spacing |
| 5 | **Equivalent diameter (triangular)** | $D_e = 1.05 \cdot s$ | m | For triangular layout |
| 6 | **Equivalent diameter (square)** | $D_e = 1.13 \cdot s$ | m | For square layout |
| 7 | **Improvement factor (SPT)** | $n = N_{\text{after}} / N_{\text{before}}$ | — | Target: n ≥ 1.5–2.0 |
| 8 | **Liquefaction resistance ratio** | $\tau / \sigma'_v$ | — | Compared with CSR curve |
| 9 | **Reduction factor (settlement)** | $\beta = 1 / (1 + (n-1) \cdot a_s)$ | — | For composite ground analysis |
| 10 | **Stress concentration factor** | $n_c = \sigma_c / \sigma_s$ | — | Typically 2–5 for granular piles |
| 11 | **Hole spacing (Mitchell & Katti)** | $s = 1.5 \cdot d_v$ to $1.8 \cdot d_v$ | m | $d_v$ = vibroflot diameter |
| 12 | **Depth of probe penetration** | $z_{\max} \leq 30$ m | m | Crane-dependent limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Reminder:** When using the area replacement ratio, ensure that $A_c$ (column cross-section) uses the **as-built pile diameter**, not the vibroflot diameter. The as-built diameter is typically 1.2–1.5× the vibroflot diameter due to backfill swelling.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vibro displacement compaction with displacement piles finds primary application in:

1. **Foundation preparation** for structures on loose sandy soils (e.g., ports, airports, industrial facilities).
2. **Liquefaction mitigation** in seismic zones (the densified soil + drainage columns prevent pore pressure buildup).
3. **Tank farms and embankments** where differential settlement control is critical.
4. **Slope stabilization** in granular slopes adjacent to infrastructure.
5. **Pre-treatment for offshore wind turbine foundations** in marine sand deposits.

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix: Vibro Displacement vs. Vibro Replacement

| Feature | Vibro Displacement (Displacement Piles) | Vibro Replacement (Stone Columns) |
|---|---|---|
| **Native soil handling** | Displaced and densified laterally | Excavated and replaced |
| **Backfill** | Limited granular infill | Full column of imported aggregate |
| **Column stiffness** | Moderate (native + infill) | High (stone skeleton) |
| **Fines content tolerance** | < 10–15% | Up to 20–25% |
| **Typical depth** | 5–30 m | 5–20 m |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Higher (more imported material) |
| **Drainage function** | Yes (granular column) | Yes (stone column) |
| **Primary objective** | Densification + light reinforcement | Reinforcement + drainage |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Diameter of Treated Zone

**Statement:** For a triangular grid of displacement piles with center-to-center spacing $s$, derive the equivalent diameter of the tributary area assigned to each pile.

### **Step 1: Visualize the Tributary Region**

For an equilateral triangular grid, the tributary region around each pile is a regular hexagon. For a square grid, it is a square.

For a triangular grid, the side length of the equilateral triangle forming one-sixth of the hexagon is $s/\sqrt{3}$.

### **Step 2: Calculate the Area of the Hexagonal Tributary Zone**

The area of a regular hexagon with side length $a$ is:
$$A_{\text{hex}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} a^2$$

Substituting $a = s/\sqrt{3}$:
$$A_e = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2$$

### **Step 3: Simplify the Expression**

$$A_e = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{s^2}{3}$$

$$A_e = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} s^2$$

$$\boxed{A_e = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot (\sqrt{3} \cdot s)^2 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} s^2}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The hexagonal tributary area in a triangular grid equals the area of the equilateral triangle of side $s$ times 2, giving $\sqrt{3}/2 \cdot s^2$. This is **not the same** as $s^2$ (which applies to square grids).

### **Step 4: Equate to Circular Area for Equivalent Diameter**

Setting $A_e = \pi D_e^2 / 4$:
$$\frac{\pi D_e^2}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} s^2$$

$$D_e^2 = \frac{4 \cdot \sqrt{3}}{2\pi} s^2 = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} s^2$$

$$D_e = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi}}$$

Numerically:
$$D_e \approx s \cdot 1.05 \quad \text{(triangular grid)}$$

$$\boxed{D_e = 1.05 \cdot s}$$

> **For square grid (analogous derivation):**
> $A_e = s^2$, hence $D_e = 2s/\sqrt{\pi} = 1.13 s$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Settlement Reduction Factor (Composite Ground Theory)

**Statement:** Derive the settlement reduction factor $\beta$ for a composite ground reinforced with displacement piles.

### **Step 1: Define the Composite Modulus**

For composite ground (granular column + surrounding soil), the equivalent modulus of deformation is:
$$E_{\text{eq}} = E_s \cdot (1 - a_s) + E_c \cdot a_s$$

where $a_s = A_c / A_e$ is the area replacement ratio, $E_s$ is the native soil modulus, and $E_c$ is the column modulus.

### **Step 2: Apply the Stress Concentration Principle**

Because the column is stiffer than the surrounding soil, the column carries a higher stress:
$$\sigma_c = n_c \cdot \sigma_s$$

where $n_c$ is the stress concentration factor. Force equilibrium on the tributary area:
$$A_e \cdot \sigma_{\text{avg}} = A_c \cdot \sigma_c + (A_e - A_c) \cdot \sigma_s$$

$$\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \sigma_s \cdot \left[1 + (n_c - 1) \cdot a_s\right]$$

### **Step 3: Derive the Settlement Ratio**

Settlement is inversely proportional to stiffness. The settlement of unimproved ground:
$$S_0 = \frac{\sigma_{\text{avg}} \cdot H}{E_s}$$

The settlement of composite ground (assuming equal strain assumption):
$$S = \frac{\sigma_{\text{avg}} \cdot H}{E_{\text{eq}}}$$

The reduction factor is:
$$\beta = \frac{S}{S_0} = \frac{E_s}{E_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{1 + (n_c - 1) \cdot a_s}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{1}{1 + (n_c - 1) \cdot a_s}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **Priebe** style formulation. For typical vibro displacement with $n_c \approx 3$ and $a_s \approx 0.15$, $\beta \approx 1/(1 + 0.30) = 0.77$, i.e., **23% reduction in settlement**. This number is a high-yield exam value.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Probe Spacing from Required SPT Improvement

**Statement:** Given a target SPT N-value after improvement ($N_{\text{req}}$) and a known native N-value ($N_0$), determine the probe spacing $s$ such that the area replacement ratio yields the required improvement.

### **Step 1: Establish the Improvement Factor**

The improvement factor $n$ relates the post-treatment to pre-treatment SPT:
$$n = \frac{N_{\text{after}}}{N_0}$$

### **Step 2: Relate Improvement Factor to Area Replacement Ratio**

Empirically (Mitchell, 1981), for granular soils:
$$n \approx 1 + a_s \cdot (k - 1)$$

where $k$ is a soil-dependent coefficient (typically 3–6 for clean sands, reflecting the column-to-soil stiffness ratio).

### **Step 3: Substitute the Area Ratio for Triangular Grid**

$$a_s = \frac{A_c}{A_e} = \frac{\pi d_c^2 / 4}{\sqrt{3} s^2 / 2} = \frac{\pi d_c^2}{2\sqrt{3} s^2}$$

### **Step 4: Solve for Spacing**

$$n - 1 = \frac{\pi d_c^2 (k - 1)}{2\sqrt{3} s^2}$$

$$s^2 = \frac{\pi d_c^2 (k - 1)}{2\sqrt{3} (n - 1)}$$

$$\boxed{s = d_c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi (k - 1)}{2\sqrt{3} (n - 1)}}}$$

### **Step 5: Numerical Worked Example**

> Given: $N_0 = 8$, $N_{\text{req}} = 20$, pile diameter $d_c = 0.6$ m, $k = 4$.

**Solution:**
- $n = 20/8 = 2.5$
- $n - 1 = 1.5$, $k - 1 = 3$
- $s^2 = \pi \cdot (0.6)^2 \cdot 3 / (2\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.5) = 3.1416 \cdot 0.36 \cdot 3 / 5.196 = 0.6527$
- $s = 0.808$ m → **round up to 0.85 m or 0.9 m for constructability**

> **Exam Note:** Always round up spacings to nearest 0.1 m or 0.5 m for practical grid layout. Show both the calculated and recommended value in your answer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (For KTU Problem-Solving)

| Problem Type | Key Parameters to Identify | Governing Formula | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spacing from improvement factor | $N_0, N_{\text{req}}, d_c$ | Section 3.3 | Using wrong grid (square vs. triangular) |
| Settlement reduction | $E_s, E_c, a_s$ | Section 3.2 | Forgetting $a_s \cdot (n_c-1)$ in denominator |
| Tributary area | Grid type, $s$ | Section 3.1 | Confusing $A_e = s^2$ vs. $\sqrt{3}/2 \cdot s^2$ |
| Suitability check | Fines content, SPT, depth | Rule-of-thumb | Applying to soils with fines > 15% |
| Cost estimation | $s, depth, no. of piles$ | $N = A_{\text{total}} / A_e$ | Forgetting corner/edge boundary effects |

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Vibro Displacement Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Site Preparation and Grid Marking] --> B[Step 2: Rig Setup and Probe Positioning]
    B --> C[Step 3: Vibroflot Activation]
    C --> D[Step 4: Probe Penetration with Vibration]
    D --> E[Step 5: Reach Design Depth of Treatment]
    E --> F[Step 6: Granular Backfill Addition]
    F --> G[Step 7: Stage Withdrawal in 0.3 to 0.5 m Lifts]
    G --> H[Step 8: Re-penetration and Compaction of Each Lift]
    H --> I{All Lifts Completed?}
    I -- No --> F
    I -- Yes --> J[Step 9: Final Withdrawal and Surface Finishing]
    J --> K[Step 10: Quality Control via SPT and CPT]
    K --> L[N-value Target Achieved]
    L --> M[Step 11: Move to Next Grid Point]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of Vibro Displacement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerUnit[Power and Control Unit]
        P1[Electric or Hydraulic Motor]
        P2[Vibration Generator at 30 to 50 Hz]
        P3[Control Console with Depth Indicator]
        P1 --> P2
        P2 --> P3
    end
    subgraph ProbeAssembly[Probe Assembly]
        Q1[Vibroflot Body with Eccentric Weights]
        Q2[Water Jet System for Wet Process]
        Q3[Tremie Pipe for Bottom Feed Backfill]
        Q1 --> Q2
        Q1 --> Q3
    end
    subgraph HoistingSystem[Hoisting System]
        R1[Crane or Rig Mast]
        R2[Cable and Pulley Mechanism]
        R3[Positioning Winch for Grid Movement]
        R1 --> R2
        R2 --> R3
    end
    P3 -. supplies power to .-> Q1
    P3 -. depth telemetry from .-> Q1
    R2 -. lifts and lowers .-> Q1
    Q3 -. delivers backfill to .-> Q1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Soil State Transformation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initial Loose Granular Soil: Dr 30 to 40 percent] --> V1[Application of Horizontal Vibration]
    V1 --> V2[Shear Thinning: Friction Reduced Momentarily]
    V2 --> V3[Particle Rearrangement Toward Dense Packing]
    V3 --> S1[Compacted Soil: Dr 70 to 85 percent]
    S1 --> V4[Backfill Insertion into Annular Space]
    V4 --> S2[Composite Ground with Displacement Pile]
    S2 --> V5[Improved Load-Bearing and Drainage Capacity]
    V5 --> S3[Final Foundation-Ready Ground]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Notes (Text-Based Functional Description)

> [!NOTE]
> **Probe Geometry (Physical Description):**
> The vibroflot is essentially a cylindrical steel tube (length 3–5 m, diameter 300–500 mm) with:
> - **Eccentric weights** mounted on a horizontal shaft at the top, driven by a motor at 1,800 rpm (≈ 30 Hz) or 3,000 rpm (≈ 50 Hz).
> - **Water jets** at the tip (wet process) fed through internal pipes at 600–1,000 kPa.
> - **Tremie pipe** (dry/bottom-feed process) for placing granular infill at the probe tip.
> - **Follower tubes** (extension rods) added to reach depths > 5 m.
> - **Suspension cable** connected to a crane, providing both support and depth telemetry.

> **The "vibration radius" extends 1.5 to 2.5 times the probe radius**, defining the effective zone of densification. Probe spacing on a triangular grid is therefore typically **1.5–2.0 × vibroflot diameter** to ensure overlap of treated zones.

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Q: Distinguish between Vibro Compaction, Vibro Replacement, and Vibro Displacement methods of ground improvement. Mention the soil conditions suitable for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Method | Mechanism | Suitable Soil |
|---|---|---|
| **Vibro Compaction** | Pure densification of native granular soil using depth vibrator; no backfill added. | Clean sands with fines < 5%, SPT N < 15. |
| **Vibro Replacement** | Native soil is excavated and fully replaced with imported granular material (stone column). | Sandy soils with fines 5–15%; also used in marginal soils. |
| **Vibro Displacement** | Native soil is displaced laterally and densified; limited backfill forms a column (displacement pile). | Loose to medium-dense sands with fines < 10–15%. |

**Key Distinguishing Feature:** The defining difference lies in the **degree of native soil removal and the role of the backfill**. In vibro compaction, no backfill is used. In vibro replacement, the native soil is excavated out. In vibro displacement, the native soil is **pushed aside** (not excavated), and a moderate amount of backfill is introduced to form a column.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is the role of a vibroflot in ground improvement? List the key components of a typical vibroflot assembly.**

**Model Answer:**

A **vibroflot** is a depth vibrator that transmits horizontal vibrations into the ground to densify granular soils. It performs three simultaneous functions: (i) penetrates the ground under its own weight, (ii) transmits horizontal vibrations to densify surrounding soil, and (iii) creates an annular space for backfill introduction.

**Key Components of a Vibroflot:**

1. **Eccentric weights** mounted on a horizontal shaft — generate centrifugal force at the operating frequency.
2. **Drive motor** (electric or hydraulic) — rotates the eccentric shaft at 1,800–3,000 rpm.
3. **Water jet system** — for wet-process penetration assistance.
4. **Tremie pipe / bottom-feed pipe** — for granular backfill delivery in dry processes.
5. **Follower tubes (extension rods)** — for reaching treatment depths beyond the basic probe length.
6. **Suspension cable and connector** — links the vibroflot to the crane and provides depth monitoring.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU ESE Pattern Note: Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: (a) Describe in detail the Vibro Displacement Compaction method of ground improvement. Explain the construction sequence with a neat sketch. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A loose sandy site is to be improved using vibro displacement displacement piles. The native SPT N-value is 8. Design the spacing of piles (triangular grid) such that the post-treatment SPT N-value is at least 20. The vibroflot diameter is 380 mm, and the expected pile diameter after backfilling is 600 mm. Assume the soil-dependent coefficient k = 4. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The Vibro Displacement Compaction method uses a depth vibrator (vibroflot) to densify granular soils by horizontal vibrations, simultaneously displacing native soil laterally and forming granular backfill columns. The construction sequence is as follows:

**Step 1 — Site Investigation:** Confirm soil suitability (granular soil, fines < 10–15%, water table location).

**Step 2 — Grid Layout:** Mark triangular or square grid with spacing 1.5–3.0 m based on design calculations.

**Step 3 — Probe Penetration:** Position the vibroflot vertically. Start vibration (30–50 Hz) and lower the probe under its own weight + water jet assistance (wet process) to design depth.

**Step 4 — Granular Backfill Introduction:** Feed granular material (sand, gravel, or crushed stone) via top feed (surface) or bottom feed (tremie pipe) as the probe is withdrawn in 0.3–0.5 m lifts.

**Step 5 — Stage Compaction:** At each lift, hold the probe momentarily (30–60 s) to allow lateral densification of surrounding soil and compaction of the backfill.

**Step 6 — Final Withdrawal and Finishing:** After reaching the surface, finish the column top and shift to the next grid point.

**Step 7 — Quality Control:** Conduct SPT/CPT at midpoints between piles. Verify SPT N ≥ 15–20 and surface settlement < 25 mm under proof rolling.

**Sketch (Conceptual — Refer Section 4):**
```
        Surface
          |
          ▼
   [Top of column]
   [Compacted sand]
   [Compacted sand]   ← Probe withdrawal in stages
   [Compacted sand]
   [Vibroflot tip]    ← Design depth
   [Dense native soil]
```

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $N_0 = 8$ (initial SPT)
- $N_{\text{req}} = 20$ (target SPT)
- $d_v = 0.380$ m (vibroflot diameter)
- $d_c = 0.600$ m (pile diameter)
- $k = 4$ (soil coefficient)
- Grid: Triangular

**Step 1: Compute the Improvement Factor**
$$n = \frac{N_{\text{req}}}{N_0} = \frac{20}{8} = 2.5$$

**Step 2: Apply the Spacing Formula (Triangular Grid)**
$$s = d_c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi (k - 1)}{2\sqrt{3} (n - 1)}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**
$$s = 0.600 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi \cdot (4 - 1)}{2\sqrt{3} \cdot (2.5 - 1)}}$$

$$s = 0.600 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3\pi}{2\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.5}}$$

$$s = 0.600 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{9.4248}{5.1962}}$$

$$s = 0.600 \cdot \sqrt{1.8138} = 0.600 \cdot 1.3468$$

$$\boxed{s = 0.808 \text{ m} \approx 0.85 \text{ m (recommended for constructability)}}$$

**Step 4: Verify the Area Replacement Ratio**
$$A_c = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.6^2}{4} = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_e = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 0.85^2 = 0.6258 \text{ m}^2$$
$$a_s = 0.2827 / 0.6258 = 0.452$$

**Step 5: Comment**
With $a_s = 0.452$, the design is robust (high area replacement). The recommended spacing is **0.85 m center-to-center on a triangular grid**, which yields 1.6 piles per m². For a 50 m × 50 m site, this corresponds to approximately **4,000 displacement piles** at the calculated spacing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a) Explain the design principles of Vibro Displacement Compaction. Discuss the criteria for selection of backfill material and the factors controlling the probe spacing. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A triangular grid of displacement piles is to be installed in a loose sand deposit (SPT N = 10). The pile diameter is 0.5 m, and the spacing is 1.5 m. The stress concentration factor is 4, and the area replacement ratio is 0.10. Determine: (i) the equivalent diameter of the tributary area, (ii) the settlement reduction factor, and (iii) the expected post-treatment SPT N-value assuming an improvement factor formula $n = 1 + a_s(k-1)$ with $k = 3$. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Design Principles of Vibro Displacement Compaction:**

1. **Soil Suitability Assessment:** Granular soils with SPT N < 15 and fines content < 10–15%.
2. **Treatment Depth Selection:** Determined by the thickness of loose stratum and crane capacity (typically up to 30 m).
3. **Probe Spacing Design:** Based on the required improvement factor and area replacement ratio.
4. **Backfill Selection:** Cohesionless, well-graded granular material with high permeability and high friction angle.
5. **Quality Control Framework:** Pre- and post-treatment SPT/CPT, plate load tests.

**Backfill Material Criteria:**

| Property | Requirement | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Grain size** | 0.5 mm – 50 mm | Free-draining, non-cohesive |
| **Fines content** | < 5% | Prevent clogging of drainage path |
| **Friction angle** | $\phi \geq 38°$ | High column stiffness |
| **Durability** | Sound, non-degradable | Long-term performance |
| **Particle shape** | Angular preferred | Better interlock and stiffness |

**Factors Controlling Probe Spacing:**

1. **Soil type and gradation** — finer soils require closer spacing.
2. **Initial SPT N-value** — looser soils may need more aggressive treatment.
3. **Target improvement factor** — higher N-value target → closer spacing.
4. **Pile diameter (as-built)** — larger pile diameter → larger spacing for same $a_s$.
5. **Vibroflot power and amplitude** — more powerful equipment enables wider spacing.
6. **Treatment depth** — deeper treatment may require closer spacing due to energy dissipation.
7. **Groundwater table position** — wet vs. dry process selection.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Grid: Triangular
- $s = 1.5$ m (spacing)
- $d_c = 0.5$ m (pile diameter)
- $n_c = 4$ (stress concentration factor)
- $a_s = 0.10$ (area replacement ratio)
- $N_0 = 10$, $k = 3$

**Part (i): Equivalent Diameter of Tributary Area**

For a triangular grid:
$$D_e = 1.05 \cdot s = 1.05 \cdot 1.5 = 1.575 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D_e = 1.575 \text{ m}}$$

**Part (ii): Settlement Reduction Factor**

$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 + (n_c - 1) \cdot a_s} = \frac{1}{1 + (4 - 1) \cdot 0.10}$$

$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 + 0.30} = \frac{1}{1.30}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = 0.769}$$

This indicates a **settlement reduction of approximately 23%** due to the displacement pile installation.

**Part (iii): Post-Treatment SPT N-value**

Improvement factor formula:
$$n = 1 + a_s \cdot (k - 1) = 1 + 0.10 \cdot (3 - 1) = 1 + 0.20 = 1.20$$

$$N_{\text{after}} = n \cdot N_0 = 1.20 \cdot 10$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{after}} = 12}$$

**Discussion:** The achieved SPT improvement (from 10 to 12) is **modest** because the area replacement ratio is only 10%. For a more substantial improvement, either closer spacing (smaller $s$) or larger pile diameter should be considered. For example, reducing $s$ to 1.0 m increases $a_s$ to approximately 0.227, yielding $N_{\text{after}} \approx 14.5$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Vibro Displacement Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the grid formula:** Using $A_e = s^2$ (square grid) when the problem specifies triangular grid. Always check the grid type explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing diameters:** Using vibroflot diameter $d_v$ instead of as-built pile diameter $d_c$ in the spacing formula. The two are **not** the same; $d_c$ is typically 1.2–1.5 × $d_v$.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Reporting spacing in mm or area in cm². KTU board examiners will **deduct 1 mark** for missing or inconsistent units.
> 4. **Skipping the constructive recommendation:** Students often quote the calculated spacing (e.g., 0.808 m) without rounding to a constructable value (0.85 m or 0.9 m). This loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Misclassifying soil suitability:** Stating that vibro displacement works in clay. **It does NOT** — the technique is fundamentally for granular (cohesionless) soils.
> 6. **Omitting the sketch/diagram in 7-mark questions:** KTU ESE requires a labeled diagram. **Always include a flowchart or cross-section sketch**, even if the question does not explicitly request one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Vibro Displacement Compaction – Displacement Piles**

- ✅ **Definition:** Vibro displacement is a deep ground improvement technique using a vibratory probe to densify granular soils and form granular backfill columns (displacement piles).
- ✅ **Suitability:** Granular soils (sands, gravels) with fines content < 10–15%, SPT N < 15, loose to medium-dense state.
- ✅ **Vibroflot:** A depth vibrator operating at 30–50 Hz, generating 100–500 kN centrifugal force, with typical diameter 300–500 mm.
- ✅ **Two Process Variants:** **Wet (top feed)** — used below water table; **Dry (bottom feed)** — used above water table.
- ✅ **Construction Sequence:** Penetration → Backfill introduction → Stage withdrawal → Compaction of each lift → Quality control via SPT/CPT.
- ✅ **Probe Spacing Rule:** 1.5–1.8 × vibroflot diameter for granular soils; up to 2.5 m maximum.
- ✅ **Equivalent Diameter:** $D_e = 1.05 s$ (triangular) or $D_e = 1.13 s$ (square).
- ✅ **Tributary Area:** $A_e = \sqrt{3}/2 \cdot s^2$ (triangular) or $A_e = s^2$ (square).
- ✅ **Area Replacement Ratio:** $a_s = A_c / A_e$, typically 0.10–0.40 for displacement piles.
- ✅ **Settlement Reduction Factor:** $\beta = 1 / [1 + (n_c - 1) \cdot a_s]$, where $n_c$ is the stress concentration factor (typically 3–5).
- ✅ **Improvement Factor:** $n = N_{\text{after}} / N_{\text{before}}$, target 1.5–2.5.
- ✅ **Distinction from Related Methods:** Vibro compaction = no backfill; Vibro replacement = full stone column; Vibro displacement = hybrid (displacement + limited backfill).
- ✅ **Primary Applications:** Liquefaction mitigation, foundation preparation for ports/airports, settlement control, slope stabilization.
- ✅ **Depth Limit:** Typically 30 m (crane-dependent); minimum 3 m.
- ✅ **Key Empirical Coefficients:** $k$ (soil-dependent stiffness ratio) = 3–6 for clean sands.
- ✅ **Exam Keyword Triggers:** When you see "depth vibrator" + "granular soil" + "backfill column" → **Vibro Displacement**.
- ✅ **Backfill Criteria:** Well-graded, angular, sound, free-draining, $\phi \geq 38°$, fines < 5%.
- ✅ **Common Confusions to Avoid:** Vibro displacement ≠ Vibro replacement. Both use vibroflots, but the former densifies native soil with limited backfill, while the latter excavates and fully replaces.
- ✅ **Energy Dissipation:** Vibration influence radius ≈ 1.5–2.5 × probe radius; this governs the maximum allowable probe spacing for overlapping treated zones.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibro Flotation (Vibrocompaction):** A deep ground improvement technique that uses a depth vibrator (vibroflot) — a cylindrical probe with eccentric weights rotating at high speed — to densify loose, granular, cohesionless soils (typically sands and gravels) in-situ. The probe is inserted into the ground using water jetting (or air jetting), and the combined action of vibration, vibration-induced liquefaction, and backfilling with granular material produces a densified composite ground mass with improved bearing capacity and reduced settlement.

**Synonyms used in KTU textbooks:**
- Vibrocompaction
- Vibroflotation Method
- Deep Vibratory Compaction
- Sand Compaction Pile (when granular backfill is added during withdrawal)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Electric Beater in a Cup of Sugar":**
> Imagine holding a hand-held electric beater (mixer) inside a tall transparent glass filled with loose sugar. The beater spins rapidly at the bottom. What you observe is:
> 1. The sugar particles momentarily "float" as the vibrations shake the grains apart (liquefaction).
> 2. The grains then rearrange, with smaller particles falling into the voids between larger ones.
> 3. The sugar level in the glass drops noticeably — it has become denser.
>
> A **vibroflot** is essentially the same beater, but it is a 2–4 m long, 300–400 mm diameter cylindrical probe that is lowered deep into the ground and spun by a motor at the surface. Water jets help it sink and wash the surrounding soil into a fluidized state. Coarse sand or gravel is then fed in from the surface to fill the resulting void, creating a dense, stone-column-like compacted zone.
>
> **The "Why"** — Loose sands are dangerous because under earthquake shaking or machine vibration they suddenly lose strength (liquefaction). Vibroflotation pre-densifies them so they cannot collapse in the future.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Bold) |
|---|---|
| Vibroflot diameter | **300 – 400 mm** |
| Vibroflot length | **2 – 4 m** |
| Eccentric weight rotation speed | **1800 – 3000 rpm (30 – 50 Hz)** |
| Centrifugal force generated | **100 – 500 kN** (typically **~200 kN**) |
| Amplitude of vibration | **5 – 25 mm** |
| Effective compaction radius | **1.5 – 3.0 m** (depending on soil grading) |
| Jetting water pressure | **400 – 1000 kPa** |
| Water flow rate | **200 – 1000 L/min** |
| Typical depth of treatment | **Up to 30 m** (deepest ~50 m) |
| Power rating of motor | **75 – 200 kW** |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Vibroflotation is a **granular soil** technique. For **cohesive soils** (clays), the equivalent method is **Vibro Replacement (Stone Columns)** — a closely related but distinct topic covered in the same module.

## 1.4 Mechanism of Densification (Three Simultaneous Effects)

1. **Vibration** — The eccentric weights generate horizontal vibrations (usually) that propagate into the surrounding soil mass.
2. **Liquefaction (Temporary Loss of Shear Strength)** — In loose saturated sands, the cyclic shearing momentarily raises pore water pressure, effectively making the sand behave like a heavy liquid. Particles are free to rearrange.
3. **Re-arrangement under Overburden** — The sand particles re-settle into a denser packing (typically reaching **Relative Density, Dr ≥ 70–80%**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

The vibroflotation process is executed in **three sequential phases**:

### Phase 1: Insertion (Penetration)
- The vibroflot is suspended from a crawler crane or a specially designed mast (called a **vibro-rig**).
- **Water jetting at the tip** (and sometimes at the top) fluidizes the soil locally, allowing the probe to sink under its own weight + vibration.
- The eccentric weights are **rotating** during penetration to assist downward motion.
- Penetration rate: typically **0.5 – 2.0 m/min** depending on soil density.

### Phase 2: Compaction (At Design Depth)
- Once the design depth is reached, water jetting at the tip is **reduced or stopped**.
- The vibrator is held at depth for **30 – 60 seconds** to compact the surrounding soil in a horizontal direction.
- During this phase, surface settlement of **5 – 15% of the treated depth** is observed (a visible "crater" forms at the surface).

### Phase 3: Withdrawal & Backfilling
- The probe is slowly withdrawn (typically **0.5 – 1.0 m/min**) while **granular backfill** (sand, gravel, crushed stone) is continuously fed into the annular space at the surface.
- The backfill descends around the probe and is compacted by the vibration, forming a **densified column or zone**.
- For pure compaction in clean sand, no backfill is strictly needed (the probe alone rearranges grains). For **vibro-replacement** (clayey soils), backfill is essential.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why jet water at the tip?** Reduces tip resistance by replacing soil with a water-soil slurry.
- **Why hold at depth?** Allows radial densification of the soil "ring" around the probe.
- **Why add backfill?** Fills the surface crater and ensures compaction in the upper zone; in clayey soils, it creates a stiff stone column.
- **Why use eccentric weights?** A simple rotating mass produces a centrifugal force F = m·ω²·r that translates into soil vibration.

## 2.3 Soil Suitability Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Granular soils suitable for Vibrocompaction must have:**
> - Less than **10 – 15% fines (passing 75 μ sieve)** — preferably < 5%.
> - **Saturated** or near-saturated state (pore pressure dissipation drives densification).
> - Loose to medium-dense state initially (Dr < 50–60%).
> - Depth of loose layer > **2 m** (otherwise surface methods suffice).

Soils **NOT suitable** for plain vibrocompaction: **Clays, silts, organic soils** (use Vibro Replacement / Stone Columns instead).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal Force (vibrator) | $F_c = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ | N | $m$ = eccentric mass (kg), $e$ = eccentricity (m), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) |
| 2 | Angular velocity relation | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $N$ = rpm |
| 3 | Centrifugal force in terms of rpm | $F_c = m \cdot e \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi N}{30}\right)^2$ | N | Common form used in KTU problems |
| 4 | Influence radius | $r_i = 1.5 \text{ to } 3.0$ m | m | Empirical; depends on soil |
| 5 | Spacing of compaction points (triangular) | $S = 1.05 \cdot r_i \cdot \sqrt{3}$ | m | For triangular grid |
| 6 | Spacing of compaction points (square) | $S = 1.0 \cdot r_i \cdot \sqrt{2}$ | m | For square grid |
| 7 | Required Relative Density after treatment | $D_r \geq 0.70$ to $0.80$ | — | KTU design threshold |
| 8 | Increase in SPT N-value | $N_{1,after} \geq 15 \text{ to } 20$ | blows/300mm | Acceptance criterion |
| 9 | Settlement Reduction Factor | $n_s = \dfrac{S_{after}}{S_{before}} \approx 0.20 \text{ to } 0.50$ | — | 50–80% reduction typical |
| 10 | Liquefaction Resistance (Cyclic Stress Ratio) | $CRR_{M=7.5, \sigma'=1} = \dfrac{1}{34 - (N_1)_{60,cs}} + \dfrac{(N_1)_{60,cs}}{135} + \dfrac{50}{[10 \cdot (N_1)_{60,cs} + 45]^2} - \dfrac{1}{200}$ | — | Idriss & Boulanger (2008); for post-treatment check |

> [!TIP]
> **The Mason & Lambe (1983) influence-radius correlation** (often quoted in KTU Module 3):
> For **clean sand (Sandy Gravel, SP)**: $r_i \approx 2.5$ to $3.0$ m
> For **fine to medium sand (SW-SM)**: $r_i \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ m
> For **silty sand (SM)**: $r_i \approx 1.0$ to $1.5$ m
> **The higher the fines content, the smaller the influence radius.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Liquefaction mitigation** in earthquake-prone zones (port cities of Kerala, Japan, USA West Coast).
- **Foundation support** for oil tanks, silos, high-rise buildings on reclaimed land.
- **Hydraulic-fill reclamation** projects (e.g., Singapore, Dubai, Mumbai coastal zones).
- **Underground tank & pipeline bedding** in loose alluvial deposits.
- **Wind turbine foundations** in sandy coastal sites.

## 2.6 Advantages and Limitations

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Deep treatment (up to 30–50 m) | Ineffective in cohesive soils |
| Significant increase in Dr (up to 80–90%) | Requires large water supply |
| Reduces settlement by 50–80% | Generates spoil water (slurry) — needs disposal |
| Mitigates liquefaction risk | Vibration nuisance to nearby structures (safe distance ~30–50 m) |
| Faster than preloading for thick deposits | Not suitable if cobbles/boulders obstruct penetration |
| Verified by simple SPT/CPT tests | Specialized equipment required (high mobilization cost) |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Centrifugal Force Exerted by a Vibroflot

### Given Data (Typical KTU Problem)
- Eccentric mass: $m = 50 \text{ kg}$ per weight (assume 2 weights, so $m_{total} = 100$ kg)
- Eccentricity: $e = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Rotational speed: $N = 1800 \text{ rpm}$
- Find: Centrifugal force $F_c$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to angular velocity in rad/s.**

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1800}{60} = 60 \pi \approx 188.50 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard centrifugal force formula.**

The centrifugal force generated by a rotating eccentric mass is given by Newton's second law for circular motion:

$$
F_c = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2
$$

where:
- $m$ = eccentric mass (kg)
- $e$ = eccentricity — distance of the mass center from the rotation axis (m)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
F_c = 100 \times 0.05 \times (188.50)^2
$$

$$
F_c = 5 \times 35532.25
$$

$$
F_c \approx 177{,}661 \text{ N} \approx 177.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in kiloNewtons and final answer.**

$$
\boxed{F_c \approx 178 \text{ kN}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU students:** The force of ~178 kN is comparable to the weight of a fully loaded truck. When this force is applied cyclically (30 times per second) at the tip of the probe, the resulting soil vibrations extend 1.5–3.0 m radially outward, re-arranging the soil grains into a denser packing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spacing of Vibroflotation Compaction Points

### Given Data
- Soil type: Clean medium sand
- Influence radius: $r_i = 2.0 \text{ m}$
- Layout: Triangular (most common, KTU-recommended)
- Find: Spacing $S$ between probe insertion points

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of a triangular grid.**

In an equilateral triangular grid, the area covered per probe point (the "unit cell" or "Wigner-Seitz cell") is a regular hexagon. The relationship between spacing $S$ and the influence radius $r_i$ must ensure **full coverage** (no untreated "gap" between two adjacent compaction zones).

For full coverage, the distance from any probe to the centroid of the triangle must be ≤ $r_i$.

The distance from a vertex of an equilateral triangle of side $S$ to its centroid is:

$$
d = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $d = r_i$ for full coverage.**

$$
r_i = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

$$
S = \sqrt{3} \cdot r_i \approx 1.732 \cdot r_i
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $r_i = 2.0$ m.**

$$
S = 1.732 \times 2.0 = 3.464 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical spacing (with overlap factor).**

In practice, an **overlap factor of 1.0 to 1.1** is used to guarantee that every point in the soil mass lies within the influence radius:

$$
S_{practical} = \frac{1.05 \cdot r_i \cdot \sqrt{3}}{?}
$$

Wait — let us re-derive correctly. The KTU standard relation is:

$$
S = k \cdot r_i
$$

where $k = 1.5$ to $1.8$ for square grid and $k = 1.7$ to $2.0$ for triangular grid. Taking the geometric mean:

$$
S_{triangle} = \sqrt{3} \cdot r_i
$$

$$
S_{square} = \sqrt{2} \cdot r_i
$$

So for our case:

$$
\boxed{S_{triangle} = 1.732 \times 2.0 = 3.46 \text{ m}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Verification of Liquefaction Resistance After Treatment

### Given Data
- Loose sand SPT-N before treatment: $N = 8$ blows/300 mm
- Depth of test: $z = 6$ m
- Water table at ground surface
- Soil unit weight: $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Effective unit weight: $\gamma' = 8 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (submerged)
- Design earthquake magnitude: $M_w = 7.5$
- Peak ground acceleration: $a_{max}/g = 0.20$ (Kerala Zone III)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the Cyclic Stress Ratio (CSR) induced by the design earthquake (Seed & Idriss, 1971).**

$$
CSR = 0.65 \cdot \frac{\sigma_v}{\sigma'_v} \cdot \frac{a_{max}}{g}
$$

Total vertical stress:

$$
\sigma_v = \gamma \cdot z = 18 \times 6 = 108 \text{ kPa}
$$

Effective vertical stress:

$$
\sigma'_v = \gamma' \cdot z = 8 \times 6 = 48 \text{ kPa}
$$

Substituting:

$$
CSR = 0.65 \times \frac{108}{48} \times 0.20
$$

$$
CSR = 0.65 \times 2.25 \times 0.20 = 0.2925
$$

**Step 2 — Correct the field SPT-N to $(N_1)_{60,cs}$ (clean-sand equivalent normalized SPT).**

Apply overburden correction (Liao & Whitman, 1986):

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{48}} = 1.443
$$

Hammer energy ratio for Indian SPT: $C_E = 0.8$ (typical rope-pulley).
Borehole diameter correction: $C_B = 1.0$.
Rod length correction: $C_R = 0.95$.
Fines content assumed 5% → $C_{fine} \approx 1.0$ for now.

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_E \cdot C_B \cdot C_R
$$

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} = 8 \times 1.443 \times 0.8 \times 1.0 \times 0.95
$$

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} \approx 8.77
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Cyclic Resistance Ratio (CRR) for $M_w = 7.5$ using Idriss & Boulanger (2008).**

$$
CRR_{M=7.5} = \frac{1}{34 - (N_1)_{60,cs}} + \frac{(N_1)_{60,cs}}{135} + \frac{50}{[10 \cdot (N_1)_{60,cs} + 45]^2} - \frac{1}{200}
$$

Substituting $(N_1)_{60,cs} = 8.77$:

$$
CRR = \frac{1}{34 - 8.77} + \frac{8.77}{135} + \frac{50}{[10 \times 8.77 + 45]^2} - \frac{1}{200}
$$

$$
CRR = \frac{1}{25.23} + 0.0650 + \frac{50}{(132.7)^2} - 0.005
$$

$$
CRR = 0.0396 + 0.0650 + \frac{50}{17609} - 0.005
$$

$$
CRR = 0.0396 + 0.0650 + 0.00284 - 0.005
$$

$$
CRR \approx 0.102
$$

**Step 4 — Compare CSR vs CRR → Factor of Safety against Liquefaction.**

$$
FS_{liq} = \frac{CRR}{CSR} = \frac{0.102}{0.2925} \approx 0.349
$$

Since $FS < 1.0$, **liquefaction WILL occur** in untreated soil. Soil must be improved.

**Step 5 — Apply vibrocompaction to raise N from 8 to N = 20 (typical post-treatment target).**

Re-compute $(N_1)_{60,cs}$ for $N = 20$:

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} = 20 \times 1.443 \times 0.8 \times 1.0 \times 0.95 = 21.93
$$

Re-compute CRR:

$$
CRR = \frac{1}{34 - 21.93} + \frac{21.93}{135} + \frac{50}{[10 \times 21.93 + 45]^2} - \frac{1}{200}
$$

$$
CRR = \frac{1}{12.07} + 0.1624 + \frac{50}{(264.3)^2} - 0.005
$$

$$
CRR = 0.0829 + 0.1624 + \frac{50}{69854} - 0.005
$$

$$
CRR = 0.0829 + 0.1624 + 0.000716 - 0.005 \approx 0.241
$$

Re-compute FS:

$$
FS_{liq} = \frac{CRR}{CSR} = \frac{0.241}{0.2925} \approx 0.824
$$

**Step 6 — Iterate until $FS \geq 1.2$ (Kerala PWD/IS 1893 safety threshold).**

Try $N = 25$:

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} = 25 \times 1.443 \times 0.8 \times 0.95 = 27.42
$$

$$
CRR = \frac{1}{34 - 27.42} + \frac{27.42}{135} + \frac{50}{[10 \times 27.42 + 45]^2} - \frac{1}{200}
$$

$$
CRR = \frac{1}{6.58} + 0.2031 + \frac{50}{(319.2)^2} - 0.005
$$

$$
CRR = 0.1520 + 0.2031 + 0.000491 - 0.005 \approx 0.350
$$

$$
FS_{liq} = \frac{0.350}{0.2925} \approx 1.20 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The design must specify vibrocompaction to achieve **SPT-N ≥ 25 blows/300 mm** at 6 m depth in this loose sand deposit, to obtain a Factor of Safety of **1.2** against liquefaction for a $M_w = 7.5$ earthquake in Kerala Zone III.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Settlement Reduction Estimate

### Given
- Loose sand layer thickness: $H = 8$ m
- Initial Relative Density: $D_{r,before} = 35\%$
- Target Relative Density: $D_{r,after} = 80\%$

**Settlement reduction** (empirical, Schmertmann's correlation):

$$
S_{before} = \frac{H \cdot C_c}{1+e_0} \cdot \log\left(\frac{\sigma'_v + \Delta \sigma}{\sigma'_v}\right)
$$

In practice for granular soils, a simpler approach via the relative density change is used:

$$
n_s = \frac{S_{after}}{S_{before}} \approx \left(\frac{1 - D_{r,after}/100}{1 - D_{r,before}/100}\right)^2
$$

Substituting:

$$
n_s = \left(\frac{1 - 0.80}{1 - 0.35}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.20}{0.65}\right)^2 = (0.3077)^2 = 0.0947
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Settlement reduction} = 1 - 0.0947 = 90.5\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is consistent with the KTU Module 3 statement: "Vibrocompaction reduces settlement of loose granular deposits by 50–90%."

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Vibroflotation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vibro Rig Mounted on Crawler Crane] --> B[Lower Vibroflot with Water Jet ON]
    B --> C[Eccentric Weights Rotate at 1800 to 3000 rpm]
    C --> D[Probe Penetrates to Design Depth 5 to 30 m]
    D --> E[Hold Position 30 to 60 seconds]
    E --> F[Lateral Vibration Propagates 1.5 to 3.0 m Radius]
    F --> G[Pore Pressure Rises Temporarily Liquefaction]
    G --> H[Sand Grains Rearrange into Denser Packing]
    H --> I[Add Granular Backfill at Surface]
    I --> J[Withdraw Probe Slowly 0.5 to 1.0 m per min]
    J --> K[Backfill Compacts around Probe Path]
    K --> L[Form Densified Sand Column or Zone]
    L --> M[Conduct SPT or CPT Quality Check]
    M --> N{Quality Acceptable N greater than 20}
    N -->|Yes| O[Treat Adjacent Points at 2.0 to 3.5 m Spacing]
    N -->|No| P[Re-treat or Adjust Spacing]
    O --> Q[Project Complete]
    P --> E
```

## 4.2 Equipment Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SURFACE_LAYER
        M[Electric or Hydraulic Motor 75 to 200 kW]
        C[Crawler Crane or Vibro Rig]
        H[Hopper for Granular Backfill]
        WP[Water Pump 400 to 1000 kPa]
    end
    subgraph SUBSURFACE_LAYER
        V[Vibroflot Probe 2 to 4 m long]
        E1[Eccentric Weight 1]
        E2[Eccentric Weight 2]
        JT[Water Jet Tip]
        JT2[Water Jet Top]
    end
    M --> V
    C --> V
    H --> V
    WP --> JT
    WP --> JT2
    V --> E1
    V --> E2
```

## 4.3 Process Phase State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Phase1Insertion
    Phase1Insertion : Phase 1 Insertion Water Jet ON Vibration ON
    Phase1Insertion --> Phase2Compaction : Reach Design Depth
    Phase2Compaction : Phase 2 Compaction Hold 30 to 60 sec Surface Crater Forms
    Phase2Compaction --> Phase3Backfill : Begin Withdrawal
    Phase3Backfill : Phase 3 Backfill Add Granular Material Withdraw Slowly
    Phase3Backfill --> Phase1Insertion : Shift to Next Probe Location
    Phase1Insertion --> [*] : All Points Completed
    Phase3Backfill --> QACheck : Site Complete
    QACheck : QA Check SPT or CPT
    QACheck --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Influence Zone Schematic (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Probe Location A] --> R1[Influence Radius 2.0 m]
    P2[Probe Location B] --> R2[Influence Radius 2.0 m]
    P3[Probe Location C] --> R3[Influence Radius 2.0 m]
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- P1
    R1 --- R2
    R2 --- R3
    R3 --- R1
    R1 -. Overlap .-> R2
    R2 -. Overlap .-> R3
    R3 -. Overlap .-> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The three circles (each of radius $r_i = 2.0$ m) around the probe locations A, B, C overlap one another. This triangular arrangement (with spacing $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot r_i \approx 3.46$ m) ensures that every point in the soil mass lies within the compaction influence zone of at least one probe — guaranteeing **100% coverage**.
