# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define Vibroflotation. List any three soils in which it is most effective.**

**Model Answer:**
Vibroflotation (or vibrocompaction) is an in-situ ground densification technique for loose granular soils. A depth vibrator (vibroflot) with rotating eccentric weights is lowered into the ground using water jets, inducing vibration and momentary liquefaction that rearranges grains into a denser packing.

The three most effective soil types are:
1. **Clean sand (SP, SW)** with less than 5% fines
2. **Sandy gravel (GP, GW)** with minimal fines
3. **Fine to medium sand (SP-SM, SW-SM)** with up to 10–15% fines

Soils NOT suited: clays, silts, organic soils.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Vibrocompaction and Vibro Replacement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Vibrocompaction | Vibro Replacement |
|---|---|---|
| Soil type | Cohesionless (sand, gravel) | Cohesive (clay, silt) |
| Backfill | Optional; small amount | Mandatory — coarse aggregate |
| Mechanism | In-situ rearrangement of grains | Displacement + stone column |
| End product | Densified sand mass | Stone columns (composite ground) |
| Typical spacing | 2.5 – 3.5 m | 1.5 – 2.5 m |
| Liquefaction mitigation | Yes | Indirect (drainage + stiffness) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**A(a)** Explain with neat sketch the working principle and equipment used in Vibroflotation method of ground improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition and Principle:**
Vibroflotation is a deep ground improvement technique for densifying loose, granular, cohesionless soils in-situ. A vibroflot (a cylindrical probe with internal eccentric weights) is lowered into the ground using water jetting. The high-frequency vibration fluidizes the surrounding sand momentarily, allowing grains to rearrange into a denser packing under the overburden pressure.

**Equipment Used (refer to diagrams in Section 4.2):**
1. **Vibroflot probe** — 300–400 mm diameter, 2–4 m long steel cylinder.
2. **Eccentric weights** — rotated by a motor at 1800–3000 rpm, generating centrifugal force of 100–500 kN.
3. **Water jets** — at the tip and top, to assist penetration and fluidize soil.
4. **Vibro-rig / Crane** — to suspend and lower the probe.
5. **Generator / Motor** — 75–200 kW power supply.
6. **Granular backfill hopper** — supplies sand or gravel during withdrawal.

**Process Phases (refer to Section 4.1 flow chart):**
- **Insertion** — Probe lowered with jet water on; vibration assists penetration.
- **Compaction** — Held at design depth for 30–60 sec; soil densifies radially.
- **Withdrawal & Backfill** — Probe raised slowly while backfill is added from surface, forming a densified column.

**Applications:**
- Liquefaction mitigation
- Foundation support for tanks, buildings
- Reclaimed land densification

---

**A(b)** A vibroflot has two eccentric weights, each of mass 60 kg, with an eccentricity of 60 mm. The motor rotates at 2400 rpm. Determine the centrifugal force developed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Mass per weight: 60 kg → Total mass $m = 2 \times 60 = 120$ kg
- Eccentricity: $e = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- Rotational speed: $N = 2400 \text{ rpm}$

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to angular velocity.**

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 2400}{60} = 80 \pi \approx 251.33 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply centrifugal force formula.**

$$
F_c = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2
$$

$$
F_c = 120 \times 0.06 \times (251.33)^2
$$

$$
F_c = 7.2 \times 63165
$$

$$
\boxed{F_c \approx 454{,}789 \text{ N} \approx 454.8 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The vibroflot generates a cyclic force of **~455 kN** at 40 Hz, sufficient to densify clean sand within a 1.5–3.0 m radius.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**B(a)** What are the factors affecting the choice of Vibroflotation? Discuss the soil suitability criteria and limitations of the method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Factors Affecting the Choice of Vibroflotation:**

1. **Soil Gradation** — Effective only in granular soils with fines content < 10–15%. Higher fines reduce permeability and prevent pore-pressure dissipation needed for densification.

2. **Initial Density** — Method is most effective for loose sands (Dr < 50–60%). Denser soils yield marginal improvement.

3. **Depth of Loose Stratum** — Typically > 2 m. Shallow deposits are better treated by surface compaction.

4. **Groundwater Table** — Vibratory densification works best below the water table where saturated conditions allow full liquefaction and re-sedimentation.

5. **Required Degree of Improvement** — Determined by design SPT-N or CPT-qc targets; influences spacing and number of probe points.

6. **Site Constraints** — Vibration may affect nearby structures (safe distance 30–50 m). Slurry disposal is required.

7. **Accessibility & Logistics** — Crane access, water supply, electrical power availability.

**Limitations:**
- Ineffective in clays and organic soils.
- High water demand and slurry management.
- Specialized equipment → high mobilization cost.
- Penetration obstructed by cobbles, boulders, or hard layers.

---

**B(b)** A loose sand deposit at a site has an initial SPT-N value of 9 at 6 m depth. The water table is at 2 m depth. The soil has 5% fines, unit weight 18 kN/m³, and effective unit weight 9 kN/m³. The design earthquake corresponds to $M_w = 7.5$ and $a_{max}/g = 0.20$. Determine the factor of safety against liquefaction. If the target FS required is 1.3, recommend the required SPT-N value after vibrocompaction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute stresses at 6 m depth.**

$$
\sigma_v = 18 \times 2 + (18 - 9.81) \times 4 = 36 + 32.76 = 68.76 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\sigma'_v = (18 - 9.81) \times 4 = 32.76 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{(water table at 2 m, so soil above is dry-moist)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CSR.**

$$
CSR = 0.65 \times \frac{68.76}{32.76} \times 0.20 = 0.65 \times 2.099 \times 0.20 = 0.2729
$$

**Step 3 — Correct SPT-N to $(N_1)_{60,cs}$.**

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{100}{32.76}} = 1.747
$$

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} = 9 \times 1.747 \times 0.8 \times 0.95 = 11.94
$$

**Step 4 — Compute CRR using Idriss & Boulanger (2008).**

$$
CRR = \frac{1}{34 - 11.94} + \frac{11.94}{135} + \frac{50}{[10 \times 11.94 + 45]^2} - \frac{1}{200}
$$

$$
CRR = \frac{1}{22.06} + 0.0884 + \frac{50}{(164.4)^2} - 0.005
$$

$$
CRR = 0.0453 + 0.0884 + 0.00185 - 0.005 = 0.1305
$$

**Step 5 — Initial Factor of Safety.**

$$
FS = \frac{CRR}{CSR} = \frac{0.1305}{0.2729} \approx 0.478
$$

**FS < 1 → Liquefaction is likely; vibrocompaction is required.**

**Step 6 — Iterate to find required SPT-N for FS = 1.3.**

Required CRR: $CRR_{req} = FS \times CSR = 1.3 \times 0.2729 = 0.3548$

Try $N = 30$:

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} = 30 \times 1.747 \times 0.8 \times 0.95 = 39.79
$$

For large $(N_1)_{60,cs} \geq 30$, Idriss-Boulanger's formula caps; use NCEER (1997) simpler form:

$$
CRR \approx \frac{(N_1)_{60,cs}}{135} + \text{correction terms} \approx \frac{39.79}{135} \approx 0.295
$$

Insufficient. Try $N = 35$:

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} = 35 \times 1.747 \times 0.8 \times 0.95 = 46.42
$$

$$
CRR \approx \frac{46.42}{135} + \frac{50}{[10(46.42)+45]^2} - 0.005 \approx 0.344 + 0.0002 - 0.005 = 0.339
$$

Still slightly under 0.3548. Try $N = 37$:

$$
(N_1)_{60,cs} = 37 \times 1.326 = 49.06
$$

$$
CRR \approx \frac{49.06}{135} - 0.005 \approx 0.358
$$

$$
FS = \frac{0.358}{0.2729} \approx 1.31 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Vibrocompaction must achieve **SPT-N ≥ 37 blows/300 mm** at 6 m depth to obtain $FS \geq 1.3$ against liquefaction for the design earthquake.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "0.65" stress reduction factor** in the Seed-Idriss CSR formula — costs 2 marks easily.
> 2. **Using raw field N-value** in the CRR equation instead of the corrected $(N_1)_{60,cs}$ — entire answer is wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up** vibrocompaction (sand) with vibro replacement (clay) — examiner deducts up to 4 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the depth of influence** (1.5–3.0 m) when discussing spacing — a common theoretical loss.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in centrifugal force (must be in N or kN, not kg·m/s² without conversion).
> 6. **Skipping the QA check** (SPT/CPT) in the process flow — Module 3 explicitly tests this.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision — Vibro Flotation (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** In-situ deep vibratory densification of loose granular soils using a depth vibrator (vibroflot) with rotating eccentric weights and water jetting.
- **Suitable for:** Clean sands (SP, SW), sandy gravels (GP, GW), silty sands (SM) with **< 10–15% fines**.
- **Not suitable for:** Clays (CL, CH), silts (ML), organic soils (OL, OH), peats (Pt).
- **Centrifugal force formula:** $F_c = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ (N), with $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s.
- **Typical operating parameters:** Diameter 300–400 mm; Length 2–4 m; Speed 1800–3000 rpm; Centrifugal force 100–500 kN; Influence radius 1.5–3.0 m; Depth up to 30 m.
- **Three phases of operation:** (1) Insertion (jet on), (2) Compaction (hold 30–60 s), (3) Withdrawal + Backfill.
- **Influence radius depends on fines content:** Higher fines → smaller $r_i$.
- **Spacing for full coverage:** $S_{triangle} = \sqrt{3} \cdot r_i$ ≈ 1.73 $r_i$; $S_{square} = \sqrt{2} \cdot r_i$ ≈ 1.41 $r_i$.
- **Target Relative Density after treatment:** $D_r \geq$ **70–80%**.
- **Target SPT-N after treatment:** $N \geq$ **15–20** (for normal loading) or **25–37** (for liquefaction-prone sites).
- **Liquefaction check (Seed-Idriss + Idriss-Boulanger 2008):** $FS_{liq} = CRR / CSR \geq 1.2$ to 1.3.
- **Settlement reduction achieved:** **50–90%** of original loose-sand settlement.
- **Process QA:** Pre- and post-treatment SPT, CPT, or pressuremeter tests at random grid points.
- **Closely related method:** **Vibro Replacement / Stone Columns** — for clayey soils (covered separately in Module 3).
- **Kerala context:** Reclaimed lands (Kochi, Kozhikode backwaters), port projects, high-rise foundations on coastal alluvium.
- **Examiner focus areas:** (i) Centrifugal force numerical; (ii) Spacing calculation; (iii) Liquefaction FS verification; (iv) Differentiate vibrocompaction vs vibro replacement; (v) Process phases with sketch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sand Compaction Piles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET633 - GROUND IMPROVEMENT TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sand Compaction Piles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sand Compaction Piles (SCPs)** are a category of *in-situ* ground improvement systems in which highly densified, well-graded sand columns are constructed within a soft or loose soil mass using a casing pipe and a mandrel, without removing the host soil. The displaced soil is compacted laterally into the surrounding matrix, simultaneously densifying the in-situ soil and forming a high-modulus, high-permeability vertical reinforcement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET633 Module 3):**
> *Sand compaction piles are a ground improvement method in which a sand-filled casing pipe is driven into the ground, sand is introduced as the pipe is withdrawn, and the sand is compacted to form a stiff, dense column. The method is used to increase bearing capacity, reduce settlement, accelerate consolidation, and prevent liquefaction in soft clays and loose sands.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology Map

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Pile Diameter | $D$ or $D_p$ | Outer diameter of the sand column (0.5 – 2.0 m) |
| Unit Cell Diameter | $D_e$ | Effective tributary diameter around each pile |
| Spacing | $S$ | Centre-to-centre distance between piles |
| Replacement Ratio | $a_s$ | Fraction of ground cross-section replaced by sand |
| Improvement Ratio | $n$ | Ratio of unit cell area to pile area |
| Stress Concentration Factor | $n_s$ | Ratio of vertical stress in pile to that in clay |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Reinforced Cake"

Imagine you have a soft, spongy cake (the soft clay). If you insert hard, dense cylindrical biscuits into the cake in a regular grid pattern, the cake becomes firmer and can hold more weight. The biscuits act like **sand compaction piles**, and the cake represents the **soft soil matrix**.

- The **biscuit (sand pile)** carries a large share of any applied load because it is much stiffer.
- The **cake (surrounding soil)** shares the remaining load.
- Water trapped in the cake can drain quickly *into* the biscuits and escape upward, speeding up consolidation.
- Liquefaction resistance increases because the dense biscuit columns are already tightly packed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin Note (Syllabus Highlight):** The Sand Compaction Pile method was pioneered in **Japan (1957)** by Nozawa, primarily to mitigate liquefaction-induced damage observed during past earthquakes. It is now codified in the Japanese Geotechnical Society (JGS) standards.

### 1.4 Functional Objectives of SCPs

1. **Densification of loose sandy soils** – prevents earthquake-induced liquefaction.
2. **Reinforcement of soft clays** – improves composite shear strength and bearing capacity.
3. **Acceleration of consolidation** – sand piles act as vertical drains.
4. **Reduction of total and differential settlement** – stress transferred to stiff sand columns.
5. **Improvement of slope stability** – higher composite shear parameters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Method of Construction – Operational Logic

The construction sequence is a critical theoretical component for KTU exams. The steps are:

1. **Driving the Casing Pipe:** A hollow steel casing with a closed bottom (or with a separate mandrel) is driven into the ground using a vibratory hammer, drop hammer, or static jack. The soil is **displaced laterally**, not excavated.
2. **Feeding Sand:** After reaching the design depth, sand is fed into the casing through a hopper at the top.
3. **Withdrawal and Re-driving:** The casing is partially withdrawn, then re-driven to compact the sand charge. This compactor-re-driving cycle is repeated as the casing is gradually extracted.
4. **Formation of the Pile:** A continuous, densely compacted sand column is formed.
5. **Repeating the Process:** Piles are installed in a pre-determined grid pattern (square, triangular, or hexagonal).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention that SCPs are a **displacement-type** method (no soil removed), in contrast to *replacement* methods like bored piles. This distinction is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Improvement – Three Pillars

#### (a) Densification Effect (Dominant in Loose Sands)
The driving and re-driving action radially compresses the surrounding loose sand, increasing its relative density and effective stress. This is the *primary* mechanism for **liquefaction mitigation**.

#### (b) Reinforcement Effect (Dominant in Soft Clays)
The sand column acts as a stiff, high-modulus inclusion. Load applied at the surface is shared between the pile and the clay, with the pile carrying a disproportionately larger share.

#### (c) Drainage Effect (Dominant in Soft Clays)
The high permeability of the sand column ($k_{sand} \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ m/s) provides radial drainage paths for pore-water pressure dissipation, accelerating consolidation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical**. Memorize the symbols, units, and physical meaning of each.

| # | Formula Name | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Replacement Ratio | $a_s \;=\; \dfrac{A_p}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{D^2}{D_e^{\,2}}$ | dimensionless | Determines fraction of soil replaced |
| 2 | Improvement Ratio | $n \;=\; \dfrac{A}{A_p} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{a_s}$ | dimensionless | Inverse of replacement ratio |
| 3 | Unit Cell Area (Square) | $A \;=\; S^2$ | m² | Square grid layout |
| 4 | Unit Cell Area (Triangular) | $A \;=\; \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, S^2$ | m² | Triangular grid layout |
| 5 | Unit Cell Diameter | $D_e \;=\; S\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2\beta_w}}$ | m | General layout parameter |
| 6 | Stress Concentration Factor | $n_s \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_c}$ | dimensionless | Typically 3 to 5 |
| 7 | Composite Modulus | $E_{comp} \;=\; a_s \, E_p + (1-a_s) \, E_c$ | kPa | Settlement analysis |
| 8 | Composite Cohesion | $c_{comp} \;=\; (1-a_s) \, c_c$ | kPa | Stability analysis (φ=0 method) |
| 9 | Composite Unit Weight | $\gamma_{comp} \;=\; a_s \, \gamma_p + (1-a_s) \, \gamma_c$ | kN/m³ | Weight correction |
| 10 | Settlement Reduction Factor | $\beta \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + a_s (n-1)}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of improved to unimproved settlement |
| 11 | SPT Improvement (Sands) | $\Delta N \;=\; \dfrac{N_{imp}-N_0}{N_0} \times 100\%$ | % | Liquefaction evaluation |
| 12 | Equivalent Hydraulic Path | $k_{h,\text{eff}} \;=\; k_h + a_s \cdot k_p$ | m/s | Combined radial drainage |

**Note on Notations:**
- $A_p$ = Cross-sectional area of one pile
- $A$ = Tributary area of unit cell
- $E_p$, $E_c$ = Young's modulus of pile and clay
- $\sigma_p$, $\sigma_c$ = Average vertical stress in pile and clay
- $\beta_w$ = Geometric factor ($\beta_w = \pi/4$ for square, $\pi/(2\sqrt{3})$ for triangular)

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Port & Harbour Engineering** | Foundations behind quay walls, reclamation fills |
| **Highway / Railway Embankments** | Soft alluvial deposits, marine clays |
| **Tank / Silo Foundations** | Highly loaded shallow foundations on weak soils |
| **Seismic Zones** | Liquefaction remediation under buildings, bridges, lifelines |
| **Land Reclamation** | Improving hydraulically filled sandy ground |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stress Concentration and Composite Modulus

We consider a **unit cell** concept: a single SCP with its surrounding tributary cylindrical zone of clay. Equilibrium and compatibility at the surface give the two governing equations.

**Step 1: Equilibrium of Vertical Stresses**
The total applied surface load $p$ is shared between the pile and the clay.

$$
p \cdot A \;=\; \sigma_p \cdot A_p \;+\; \sigma_c \cdot (A - A_p)
$$

**Step 2: Definition of Stress Concentration Factor**

$$
n_s \;=\; \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_p \;=\; n_s \, \sigma_c
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the Equilibrium Equation**

$$
p \, A \;=\; n_s \, \sigma_c \, A_p \;+\; \sigma_c (A - A_p)
$$

$$
p \, A \;=\; \sigma_c \left[ n_s A_p + A - A_p \right]
$$

$$
p \, A \;=\; \sigma_c \left[ A + A_p (n_s - 1) \right]
$$

**Step 4: Solve for Stress in Clay**

$$
\sigma_c \;=\; \frac{p \, A}{A + A_p (n_s - 1)}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $A$:

$$
\sigma_c \;=\; \frac{p}{1 + a_s (n_s - 1)}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for Stress in Pile**

$$
\sigma_p \;=\; n_s \cdot \sigma_c \;=\; \frac{n_s \, p}{1 + a_s (n_s - 1)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** The pile stress $\sigma_p$ is *larger* than the applied stress $p$ (i.e., stress concentration), while $\sigma_c < p$. The pile "shields" the soft clay.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Settlement Reduction Factor $\beta$

**Step 1: Composite Settlement using Equal-Strain Assumption**

Under the unit cell concept, both the pile and the clay are assumed to settle equally at the surface (no differential strain at the top).

$$
S_{comp} \;=\; \frac{p \, H}{E_{comp}}
$$

where $H$ is the compressible layer thickness.

**Step 2: Substituting Composite Modulus**

$$
S_{comp} \;=\; \frac{p \, H}{a_s E_p + (1 - a_s) E_c}
$$

**Step 3: Settlement of Unimproved Ground (Reference)**

$$
S_{0} \;=\; \frac{p \, H}{E_c}
$$

**Step 4: Form the Reduction Factor**

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{S_{comp}}{S_0} \;=\; \frac{E_c}{a_s E_p + (1 - a_s) E_c}
$$

**Step 5: Express in Terms of Modulus Ratio $E_p/E_c$**

Dividing numerator and denominator by $E_c$:

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{1}{a_s \, \dfrac{E_p}{E_c} + (1 - a_s)}
$$

Let $\eta = E_p / E_c$ (modulus ratio). Then:

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + a_s (\eta - 1)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The settlement reduction factor $\beta$ has the *same* mathematical form as the stress reduction factor, but uses the modulus ratio $\eta$ in place of the stress concentration factor $n_s$. Examiners love to test this parallel.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 2/3 Mark Style)

**Problem:** A soft clay deposit is improved with sand compaction piles of diameter $D = 0.8$ m installed in a square grid with spacing $S = 2.0$ m. The stress concentration factor $n_s = 4$. A uniform surcharge $p = 100$ kPa is applied. Determine:
1. Replacement ratio $a_s$
2. Stress in clay $\sigma_c$
3. Stress in pile $\sigma_p$
4. Composite cohesion, given $c_c = 20$ kPa

**Step 1: Compute Unit Cell and Pile Areas**

For square grid: $A = S^2 = (2.0)^2 = 4.0 \text{ m}^2$

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.8)^2}{4} = 0.5027 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Replacement Ratio**

$$
a_s = \frac{A_p}{A} = \frac{0.5027}{4.0} = 0.1257 \;\approx\; 0.126
$$

**Step 3: Stress in Clay**

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{p}{1 + a_s (n_s - 1)} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.126 (4 - 1)} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.378} = \frac{100}{1.378} = 72.57 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4: Stress in Pile**

$$
\sigma_p = n_s \cdot \sigma_c = 4 \times 72.57 = 290.28 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Composite Cohesion**

$$
c_{comp} = (1 - a_s) c_c = (1 - 0.126)(20) = 0.874 \times 20 = 17.48 \text{ kPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $\sigma_p > p > \sigma_c$ confirms pile stress concentration. Composite cohesion is *less* than clay cohesion because sand piles contribute no cohesion — this is a KTU favourite "trick" conceptual point.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Design Checks for KTU

| Check | Governing Condition | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|
| Pile Spacing | $S = (2.0 \text{ to } 3.0) \, D$ | "Spacing should be..." type questions |
| Replacement Ratio | $a_s = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.40$ | Common range for typical designs |
| Stress Concentration | $n_s = 3 \text{ to } 5$ (clay), $\approx 1$ (sand) | Justify based on soil type |
| Liquefaction Safety | $\text{FS}_{\text{liq}} \geq 1.2$ to $1.5$ | Mention Ishihara's chart / Seed-Idriss |
| Group Behaviour | Assume composite or use individual pile | Differentiate "single" vs "group" methods |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Installation Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Site Investigation and Marking"] --> B["Position Casing Pipe at Design Location"]
    B --> C["Drive Casing with Vibratory or Drop Hammer to Design Depth"]
    C --> D["Soil is Displaced Laterally Densifying Surrounding Matrix"]
    D --> E["Feed Well-Graded Sand into Casing Through Hopper"]
    E --> F["Partially Withdraw Casing Leaving Sand Plug at Base"]
    F --> G["Re-Drive Casing to Compact Sand Charge"]
    G --> H{"Is Column Built to Required Length?"}
    H -->|No| E
    H -->|Yes| I["Withdraw Casing Fully and Move to Next Position"]
    I --> J["Repeat in Pre-Planned Square or Triangular Grid"]
    J --> K["END: SCP Field Completed and Load Test Conducted"]
```

### 4.2 Unit Cell Concept – Composite Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UC["UNIT CELL OF DIAMETER De"]
        P["SAND PILE\nDiameter D\nModulus Ep\nStress σp"]
        C["SURROUNDING CLAY\nOuter Zone\nModulus Ec\nStress σc"]
    end
    APPLY["APPLIED SURCHARGE p"] --> P
    APPLY --> C
    P --> EQUIL["EQUILIBRIUM:\np·A = σp·Ap + σc·(A-Ap)"]
    C --> EQUIL
    P --> COMP["COMPATIBILITY:\nεp = εc = εcomp"]
    C --> COMP
    EQUIL --> OUT["COMPOSITE STRESS AND SETTLEMENT"]
    COMP --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture – Mechanism Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT["SOIL TYPE INPUT\nLoose Sand OR Soft Clay"]
    INPUT --> A1["Loose Sand Path"]
    INPUT --> A2["Soft Clay Path"]
    A1 --> M1["PRIMARY MECHANISM: Densification\n+ Secondary Reinforcement"]
    A2 --> M2["PRIMARY MECHANISM: Reinforcement + Drainage\n+ Secondary Densification"]
    M1 --> OBJ1["Liquefaction Mitigation\n+ Settlement Reduction"]
    M2 --> OBJ2["Bearing Capacity Increase\n+ Consolidation Acceleration\n+ Slope Stabilization"]
    OBJ1 --> DES["Design Verification:\nSPT-N Check\nCPT-qc Check\nShear Wave Velocity"]
    OBJ2 --> DES2["Design Verification:\nComposite Cc and cv\nBearing Capacity Eqn\nSettlement Reduction β"]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of SCP Field (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GROUND["GROUND CROSS-SECTION"]
        direction TB
        TOP["GROUND SURFACE - APPLIED LOAD p"]
        TOP --> S1["SAND PILE 1 - Diameter D"]
        TOP --> S2["SAND PILE 2 - Diameter D"]
        TOP --> S3["SAND PILE 3 - Diameter D"]
        TOP --> S4["SAND PILE 4 - Diameter D"]
        TOP --> CLAY["IMPROVED CLAY MATRIX\nIncreased Density and Modulus"]
        S1 --> CLAY
        S2 --> CLAY
        S3 --> CLAY
        S4 --> CLAY
        CLAY --> BED["FIRM BEARING STRATUM"]
    end
    NOTE["Square Grid Spacing S\nUnit Cell Diameter De\nPile Acts as Drain and Reinforcement"] -.-> GROUND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Hint:** Each pile carries $\sigma_p$, the surrounding clay carries $\sigma_c$, and the applied load $p$ is the *area-weighted* average — this is the core concept behind every composite-property formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A – 3 MARK QUESTIONS

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Define Sand Compaction Piles. State any two situations where they are preferred over other ground improvement methods.

**Model Answer:**
Sand compaction piles are vertical columns of densely compacted sand formed in the ground by driving a casing pipe, introducing sand, and re-driving to compact it, all without excavating the host soil. They are preferred in:
1. **Liquefaction-susceptible loose sandy deposits**, where radial densification is the dominant mechanism.
2. **Highly compressible soft clay layers** of large lateral extent, where they act simultaneously as vertical drains and as reinforcement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse Sand Compaction Piles (displacement method) with Sand Drains (replacement/bored method). They are conceptually related but *not identical* — KTU examiners deduct marks for this mix-up.

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
List the three primary mechanisms by which sand compaction piles improve soft ground. Identify the dominant mechanism for (a) loose sands and (b) soft clays.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Densification** of surrounding soil by radial displacement.
2. **Reinforcement** due to higher stiffness of sand columns.
3. **Drainage** of pore water through the high-permeability sand column.
- (a) Loose sands → Densification is dominant.
- (b) Soft clays → Reinforcement + Drainage are dominant.

---

### PART B – 14 MARK QUESTIONS (MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a) Describe in detail the construction procedure of sand compaction piles. Add neat sketches to illustrate the various stages of installation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Sand compaction piles are installed using a steel casing pipe and a mandrel/hummer. The construction stages are:

**Stage 1 – Setup and Positioning:** The pile layout (square or triangular grid) is marked on the prepared ground surface. The casing pipe with a driving shoe at the bottom is positioned vertically using a crane-mounted rig.

**Stage 2 – Driving the Casing:** The casing is driven into the ground using a vibratory hammer or drop hammer. As the pipe penetrates, the soil is *radially displaced and compacted*. This produces immediate densification of the surrounding granular soil. Driving continues until the design tip elevation is reached.

**Stage 3 – Sand Feeding:** With the casing at full depth, well-graded sand (typically medium to coarse, with less than 5% fines) is fed into the pipe through a top hopper under gravity or air pressure.

**Stage 4 – Compaction Cycles:** The casing is partially withdrawn by 0.5 – 1.0 m, allowing the sand to partially exit the pipe. The casing is then re-driven, which compacts the sand charge laterally and vertically. The casing is then withdrawn by another 0.5 m, and the cycle repeats.

**Stage 5 – Completion:** Once the full length of pile is constructed, the casing is fully withdrawn, and a sand mound may remain at the top, which is trimmed flush. The rig moves to the next grid location.

**Stage 6 – Quality Control:** SPT or CPT tests are conducted between piles to verify densification. Load tests may be performed on trial piles.

> [!TIP]
> **Drawing Requirement:** In KTU 14-mark answers, always draw a labelled cross-section showing the casing, sand charge, direction of compaction, and surrounding densified soil. Marks are explicitly awarded for diagrams.

---

**(b) A soft clay site is improved with sand compaction piles of diameter 1.0 m, arranged in a square pattern with spacing 2.5 m. The stress concentration factor is 4.0 and the modulus of the sand pile is 30 MPa while that of the clay is 2 MPa. Calculate: (i) replacement ratio, (ii) improvement ratio, (iii) composite modulus, and (iv) settlement reduction factor for an applied load of 80 kPa. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 1.0$ m
- $S = 2.5$ m (square grid)
- $n_s = 4.0$
- $E_p = 30$ MPa, $E_c = 2$ MPa
- $p = 80$ kPa

**Step 1: Areas**

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.0)^2}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A = S^2 = (2.5)^2 = 6.25 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Replacement Ratio**

$$
a_s = \frac{A_p}{A} = \frac{0.7854}{6.25} = 0.1257
$$

**Step 3: Improvement Ratio**

$$
n = \frac{A}{A_p} = \frac{6.25}{0.7854} = 7.96
$$

(Equivalently, $n = 1/a_s = 1/0.1257 = 7.96$)

**Step 4: Composite Modulus**

$$
E_{comp} = a_s E_p + (1 - a_s) E_c
$$

$$
E_{comp} = (0.1257)(30) + (1 - 0.1257)(2)
$$

$$
E_{comp} = 3.771 + 1.749 = 5.520 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Settlement Reduction Factor**

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{1 + a_s \left( \dfrac{E_p}{E_c} - 1 \right)} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1257 (15 - 1)}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{1 + 1.7598} = \frac{1}{2.7598} = 0.3624
$$

**Step 6: Result Interpretation**

The improved ground settles only about **36.2%** of the unimproved settlement — a reduction of nearly **64%**. This demonstrates the high efficiency of SCPs in soft clays.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is to compute the composite modulus as a *simple arithmetic mean* $((E_p + E_c)/2)$ or as a *weighted by $a_s$ only* without the $(1 - a_s)$ term on clay. The correct form is the *area-weighted* sum: $a_s E_p + (1 - a_s) E_c$. Marks are deducted for this error.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

**(a) Explain the unit cell concept used in the design of sand compaction piles. Derive the expression for the stress concentration in the pile and the clay for a unit cell under uniform surcharge loading. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Unit Cell Concept:**
Because hundreds of SCPs are installed in a regular grid, the *behaviour of one pile* is studied by isolating a representative volume of soil around it — the **unit cell**. For a square grid of spacing $S$, the unit cell is a square of area $S^2$. For a triangular grid, the unit cell is a hexagon, but it is idealised as a circle of equivalent area (Barron's idealisation) with diameter $D_e$. The single pile in the centre carries the share $\sigma_p$ and the surrounding clay carries $\sigma_c$, and equilibrium + compatibility are enforced.

**Governing Equations:**

*Equilibrium:*

$$
p \cdot A = \sigma_p \cdot A_p + \sigma_c \cdot (A - A_p)
$$

*Stress concentration definition:*

$$
n_s = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_c}
$$

**Derivation:**

Substituting $\sigma_p = n_s \, \sigma_c$ into the equilibrium equation:

$$
p \, A = n_s \sigma_c A_p + \sigma_c (A - A_p)
$$

$$
p \, A = \sigma_c \left[ A + A_p (n_s - 1) \right]
$$

Solving for $\sigma_c$ and dividing by $A$:

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{p}{1 + a_s (n_s - 1)}
$$

And for the pile:

$$
\sigma_p = n_s \cdot \sigma_c = \frac{n_s \, p}{1 + a_s (n_s - 1)}
$$

These are the standard SCP design expressions. Typical values: $n_s = 3$ to $5$ for soft clays.

---

**(b) Discuss the design parameters governing the layout of sand compaction piles. How is the spacing selected for (i) liquefaction remediation and (ii) consolidation acceleration in soft clays? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Governing Design Parameters:**

1. **Pile Diameter $D$:** Fixed by equipment — typically 0.5 to 2.0 m. Larger $D$ requires more powerful rigs.
2. **Replacement Ratio $a_s$:** Usually 0.10 to 0.40. Higher $a_s$ gives more improvement but higher cost.
3. **Spacing $S$:** Related to $D$ and grid pattern.
   - Square grid: $D_e = 1.13 \, S$
   - Triangular grid: $D_e = 1.05 \, S$
4. **Stress Concentration Factor $n_s$:** Empirical; depends on relative stiffness and stress history of the clay.
5. **Composite Properties:** $c_{comp}$, $E_{comp}$, $k_{h,\text{eff}}$ — all are functions of $a_s$ and the constituent properties.

**Selection of Spacing:**

**(i) Liquefaction Remediation in Loose Sands:**
The design target is to increase the corrected SPT $N$-value (or CPT $q_c$) above the threshold line on the **Seed-Idriss** or **Ishihara** chart. Typical practice uses $S = 2.0 D$ to $2.5 D$ in a triangular grid, achieving $\geq 70\%$ improvement in penetration resistance within the unit cell.

**(ii) Consolidation Acceleration in Soft Clays:**
Spacing is governed by **Barron's equal-strain theory** for radial drainage. The time factor $T_h$ for 90% consolidation is related to the drainage path radius $D_e/2$ and the horizontal coefficient of consolidation $c_v$. Reducing $S$ (smaller $D_e$) accelerates consolidation. Typical $S = 2.0 D$ to $3.0 D$, with closer spacing used when rapid preloading-driven settlement is required.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- **Definition:** SCPs are *in-situ* displacement-type densified sand columns that improve soft ground by densification, reinforcement, and drainage.
- **Construction:** Casing driven → sand fed → casing partially withdrawn → re-driven (compaction cycle).
- **Three Mechanisms:** Densification (sands), Reinforcement (clays), Drainage (clays).
- **Replacement Ratio:** $a_s = A_p / A = D^2 / D_e^2$. Typical $0.10 - 0.40$.
- **Improvement Ratio:** $n = 1/a_s$.
- **Stress Concentration Factor:** $n_s = \sigma_p / \sigma_c$, typically $3 - 5$.
- **Stress in Clay:** $\sigma_c = p / [1 + a_s (n_s - 1)]$.
- **Stress in Pile:** $\sigma_p = n_s \, p / [1 + a_s (n_s - 1)]$.
- **Composite Modulus:** $E_{comp} = a_s E_p + (1-a_s) E_c$.
- **Composite Cohesion:** $c_{comp} = (1 - a_s) \, c_c$ (sand has no cohesion).
- **Settlement Reduction Factor:** $\beta = 1 / [1 + a_s (\eta - 1)]$, with $\eta = E_p / E_c$.
- **Spacing Rule:** $S = 2.0 D$ to $3.0 D$ in square or triangular grid.
- **Origin:** Japan, 1957, by Nozawa; primary use in liquefaction remediation.
- **Distinction from Sand Drains:** SCPs = displacement; Sand Drains = drilled and backfilled.
- **Liquefaction Check:** Verify post-improvement SPT $N$ or CPT $q_c$ meets the cyclic resistance ratio (CRR) curve.
- **Composite Shear Strength (φ=0 method):** $c_{comp} = (1 - a_s) c_c$; do NOT add sand cohesion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Board Caveat:** Always state the *soil type* first before describing the dominant mechanism. Examiners in Kerala frequently test whether students can distinguish "behaviour in loose sand" versus "behaviour in soft clay" — losing 2 marks for missing this is extremely common.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stone Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET633 - GROUND IMPROVEMENT TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stone Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STONE COLUMNS (Granular Columns / Aggregate Piers)**
> Stone columns are vertical cylindrical columns of compacted aggregate (typically **20 mm to 75 mm** graded crushed stone or gravel) constructed in situ within a soft, cohesive soil mass. They function as **inclusions** that reinforce soft clays and silts by acting as stiff, free-draining vertical drains, thereby simultaneously enhancing **bearing capacity**, **reducing total and differential settlement**, **accelerating consolidation**, and **increasing shear strength** of the treated composite ground. The technique falls under the broader category of **Deep Soil Mixing / Displacement-type Ground Improvement**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are trying to park a heavy truck on a soft, wet clay field — the tires sink deeply, and the surface deforms badly. Now, suppose you drive a steel pipe into the soft clay, remove the muddy core, and fill the resulting hole with tightly compacted, angular crushed rock. If you repeat this process in a regular grid pattern beneath the parking area, the truck now "rests" on a hybrid foundation — most of its weight is transferred through the **rigid rock "pillars"** (the stone columns) directly to a firmer stratum, while the surrounding soft clay acts merely as lateral confinement.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Magical Jobs of a Stone Column:**
> 1. **Stiffening** — The column is 10 to 50 times stiffer than soft clay, so it carries most of the applied vertical load.
> 2. **Drainage Highway** — The granular column provides a vertical drainage path, so pore water squeezed from the clay escapes rapidly (radial consolidation), accelerating settlement.
> 3. **Lateral Confinement Provider** — The frictional, interlocked crushed stone mobilizes **passive earth pressure** against the surrounding soft clay, increasing the composite shear strength.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Column diameter ($D_c$) | **0.6 m to 1.2 m** (typical **0.8 m to 1.0 m**) |
| Column length ($L$) | **3 m to 20 m** (sometimes up to 30 m) |
| Spacing ($s$) | **2.0 m to 4.0 m** center-to-center |
| Area replacement ratio ($a_s$) | **0.10 to 0.40** (typical **0.15 to 0.25**) |
| Column angle of internal friction ($\phi_c$) | **38° to 45°** |
| Stress concentration factor ($n$) | **2 to 6** (typical **3 to 5**) |
| Aggregate size | **20 mm to 75 mm** (well-graded, angular) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The functioning of stone columns can be decomposed into the following sequential engineering operations:

- **Step 1 — Hole Formation (Displacement or Replacement)**
  A hole is created in the soft clay either by (a) **vibro-displacement** (the vibrator pushes the soil laterally without removing it) or (b) **vibro-replacement** (a jet of water or air loosens and removes the soil, after which the hole is backfilled with stone).

- **Step 2 — Aggregate Feeding**
  Crushed stone is fed into the cavity in **lifts of 0.3 m to 0.5 m**, ensuring the column is built in stages.

- **Step 3 — Compaction**
  Each lift is compacted by reinserting the vibrator, which densifies the aggregate and simultaneously **laterally displaces the surrounding clay** — pre-stressing it and increasing its in-situ horizontal effective stress.

- **Step 4 — Composite Load Response**
  When a surface load $q$ is applied, the load splits between the column and the clay according to the **stress concentration factor** $n$:

$$n = \frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_s}$$

  where $\sigma_c$ is the vertical stress in the column and $\sigma_s$ is the vertical stress in the surrounding soil. The average applied stress is:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A_c + \sigma_s \cdot A_s}{A_c + A_s} = \sigma_c \cdot a_s + \sigma_s \cdot (1 - a_s)$$

- **Step 5 — Failure Mode Determination**
  Depending on column length-to-diameter ratio ($L/D_c$), three failure modes are possible:
  - **Bulging failure** (most common when $L/D_c > 4$ to $6$): lateral plastic flow of aggregate into surrounding clay.
  - **General shear failure** (short columns, $L/D_c < 3$ to $4$): classical Terzaghi-type bearing capacity failure.
  - **Sliding failure** (slender columns): vertical slip along column-soil interface.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** and appear repeatedly in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) of Ground Improvement Techniques.

### Table 2.1: Master Formula Reference (Use $\vert$ for absolute value, never $\mid$ inside cell)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_s = \dfrac{A_c}{A} = \dfrac{\pi D_c^{\,2}/4}{s^2}$ | Area replacement ratio (square pattern) | All design problems |
| 2 | $a_s = 0.907 \cdot \dfrac{D_c^{\,2}}{s^2}$ | Area replacement ratio (triangular pattern) | Triangular layout |
| 3 | $n = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_s}$ | Stress concentration factor | Load transfer |
| 4 | $\sigma_{avg} = a_s \cdot \sigma_c + (1-a_s)\sigma_s$ | Composite average stress | Composite modulus |
| 5 | $K_{p} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi_c}{2}\right)$ | Passive earth pressure coefficient (column) | Bulging resistance |
| 6 | $K_{ac} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi_c'}{2}\right)$ | Active earth pressure coefficient (column) | Priebe's method |
| 7 | $\sigma_{r,ult} = \sigma_{r0} \cdot K_p + 2 c_u \sqrt{K_p}$ | Vesic's radial confining stress at failure | Cavity expansion |
| 8 | $q_{ult} = \sigma_{r,ult} \cdot K_p$ (single column) | Ultimate vertical stress on column head | Bearing capacity |
| 9 | $q_{ult} = c_u \cdot N_c \cdot K_p$ | Hughes-Withers bearing capacity | Short columns |
| 10 | $n_1 = 1 + \dfrac{a_s}{K_{ac} - 1}$ | Priebe's settlement improvement factor | Settlement reduction |
| 11 | $n_2 = 1 + a_s \left(n - 1\right)$ | Composite stress ratio | Composite modulus |
| 12 | $E_{comp} = a_s \cdot E_c + (1 - a_s) E_s$ | Composite Young's modulus | Settlement |
| 13 | $D_{eq} = 1.13 \cdot s$ (square) | Equivalent unit cell diameter | Unit cell concept |
| 14 | $D_{eq} = 1.05 \cdot s$ (triangular) | Equivalent unit cell diameter | Unit cell concept |
| 15 | $S_{treated} = \dfrac{S_{untreated}}{n_1}$ | Treated ground settlement | Settlement |
| 16 | $t = \dfrac{T_v \cdot D_e^{\,2}}{c_v}$ | Time for consolidation | Radial drainage |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stone columns are extensively used in:
- **Highway and railway embankments** built over soft alluvial clays.
- **Tank farms and oil refineries** (light-weight yet settlement-sensitive structures).
- **Approach embankments of bridges** where differential settlement is critical.
- **Industrial building floor slabs** on soft coastal deposits (a very common KTU Kerala context).
- **Slope stabilization** in combination with geogrid-reinforced load transfer platforms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Ultimate Bearing Capacity of a Single Stone Column (Hughes-Withers Approach, 1974)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a single stone column of diameter $D_c$ embedded in a soft clay of undrained shear strength $c_u$. The column has unit weight $\gamma_c$ and effective friction angle $\phi_c'$. The column is assumed to fail by **bulging** at a depth of approximately **2 to 3 times the column diameter** below the top.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** At a depth $z$ below the column head, the horizontal effective stress acting on the column from the surrounding clay (at-rest condition, prior to column installation) is:

$$\sigma'_{h0} = K_0 \cdot \sigma'_{v0} = K_0 \cdot (\gamma_s \cdot z - u_0)$$

During installation of the column, the surrounding clay is displaced laterally. The horizontal stress acting on the column is conservatively taken as the **total horizontal stress** $\sigma_{h0}$ (since the clay is undrained):

$$\sigma_{h0} = \sigma_{v0} = \gamma_s \cdot z \quad (\text{assuming } K_0 = 1)$$

**Step 2:** When the column is loaded vertically, it tries to expand laterally. The clay resists this expansion by mobilizing its undrained shear strength $c_u$. The maximum lateral resistance the clay can provide to the column is its **passive resistance**:

$$\sigma_{r,ult} = \sigma_{h0} + 2 c_u \cdot \sqrt{K_p}$$

where $K_p$ is the passive earth pressure coefficient of the *clay* (often taken as 1 in undrained conditions, i.e., $\phi_u = 0$). For $\phi_u = 0$:

$$K_p = 1, \quad \sqrt{K_p} = 1$$

Hence, the simplified radial resistance is:

$$\sigma_{r,ult} = \sigma_{h0} + 2 c_u = \gamma_s \cdot z + 2 c_u$$

**Step 3:** The column itself, being a granular material, can sustain an axial vertical stress equal to its lateral confining stress multiplied by the column's own passive coefficient $K_{pc}$:

$$K_{pc} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi_c'}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** The ultimate vertical stress at the column head is therefore:

$$q_{ult} = \sigma_{r,ult} \cdot K_{pc} = \left(\gamma_s \cdot z + 2 c_u\right) \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi_c'}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** For design, the depth of critical bulging $z = 2 D_c$ is adopted, giving:

$$q_{ult} = \left(2 \gamma_s \cdot D_c + 2 c_u\right) \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi_c'}{2}\right)$$

This is the **Hughes-Withers (1974) bearing capacity equation** — a heavily tested KTU concept.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Priebe's Improvement Factor (1976)

Priebe proposed a method to estimate the **settlement reduction factor** $n_1$ based on a unit cell model and a deep vibratory replacement hypothesis.

### 3.2.1 Assumptions

- The unit cell is a cylinder of equivalent diameter $D_{eq} = 1.13 s$ (for square pattern).
- The column and surrounding soil experience the same settlement at the surface (compatibility).
- Failure occurs in the column by bulging; the soil-column interface mobilizes passive pressure.
- The composite ground behaves as an **equivalent homogeneous layer** of improved modulus.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider the column as a triaxial sample under a vertical stress $\sigma_c$, with radial confining stress $\sigma_r$. At failure (active state in the column):

$$\sigma_r = \sigma_c \cdot K_{ac} - 2 c'_c \sqrt{K_{ac}}$$

For clean crushed stone, $c'_c \approx 0$, hence:

$$\sigma_r = \sigma_c \cdot K_{ac}$$

**Step 2:** The lateral stress $\sigma_r$ acts on the surrounding soil. The soil's passive resistance (which is what the soil can offer without yielding) is:

$$\sigma_{r,\text{passive}} = \sigma_s \cdot K_{ps} + 2 c'_s \sqrt{K_{ps}}$$

For equilibrium and compatibility, we equate the two, with $K_{ps}$ for the soil in passive state and $K_{ac}$ for the column in active state.

**Step 3:** Priebe's key simplification assumes $K_{ps} = 1$ (at-rest to slightly active state for the soil) and uses the inverse of the column's active coefficient. After algebraic manipulation (see Priebe 1976), the basic improvement factor reduces to:

$$n_1 = 1 + \frac{a_s}{K_{ac} - 1}$$

**Step 4:** Where $a_s$ is the area replacement ratio and $K_{ac} = \tan^2(45° + \phi_c'/2)$.

**Step 5:** The treated settlement is therefore:

$$S_{treated} = \frac{S_{untreated}}{n_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:**
> For $\phi_c' = 40°$, $K_{ac} \approx 4.6$, and for $a_s = 0.25$, $n_1 = 1 + 0.25/3.6 \approx 1.07$ — meaning only a **7% reduction in settlement** from the simple formula. In practice, Priebe added depth-correction and over-bulging correction factors yielding $n_1$ values typically between **2 and 4** for soft clays.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:**
A soft clay deposit has undrained shear strength $c_u = 25$ kPa, unit weight $\gamma_s = 18$ kN/m³, and a stone column of diameter $D_c = 1.0$ m is to be installed in a square pattern with spacing $s = 2.5$ m. The column material has $\phi_c' = 42°$ and unit weight $\gamma_c = 20$ kN/m³. Determine:

**(a)** The area replacement ratio and stress concentration factor (assume $n = 4$).
**(b)** The ultimate bearing capacity of a single column using Hughes-Withers approach. Take $z = 2 D_c$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a):** Area replacement ratio (square pattern):

$$a_s = \frac{\pi D_c^{\,2}/4}{s^2} = \frac{\pi (1.0)^2/4}{(2.5)^2} = \frac{0.7854}{6.25} = 0.1257$$

Equivalent unit cell diameter:

$$D_{eq} = 1.13 \cdot s = 1.13 \cdot 2.5 = 2.825 \text{ m}$$

Given $n = 4$, the composite average stress if applied load is $q$:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \sigma_c \cdot a_s + \sigma_s(1-a_s) = \sigma_s \left[ n \cdot a_s + (1 - a_s) \right]$$

$$\sigma_{avg} = \sigma_s \left[ 4 \cdot 0.1257 + 0.8743 \right] = \sigma_s \left[ 0.5028 + 0.8743 \right] = 1.3771 \cdot \sigma_s$$

If $\sigma_{avg} = 100$ kPa, then $\sigma_s = 72.6$ kPa and $\sigma_c = 290.4$ kPa.

**Part (b):** Hughes-Withers ultimate bearing capacity:

$$K_{pc} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{42°}{2}\right) = \tan^2(66°) = (2.246)^2 = 5.04$$

Radial confining stress at depth $z = 2 D_c = 2.0$ m:

$$\sigma_{r,ult} = \gamma_s \cdot z + 2 c_u = 18 \cdot 2.0 + 2 \cdot 25 = 36 + 50 = 86 \text{ kPa}$$

Ultimate column head stress:

$$q_{ult} = \sigma_{r,ult} \cdot K_{pc} = 86 \cdot 5.04 = 433.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Priebe's improvement factor:**

$$K_{ac} = 5.04 \quad \text{(same as } K_{pc}\text{ for } c'_c = 0)$$

$$n_1 = 1 + \frac{a_s}{K_{ac} - 1} = 1 + \frac{0.1257}{4.04} = 1 + 0.0311 = 1.031$$

(For a real design, this is enhanced by depth factor to $n_1 \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$ with Priebe's correction curves.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Installation Sequence Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following flowchart maps the field-installation sequence of a typical Vibro-Replacement Stone Column. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to satisfy the Mermaid parser safeguard.

```mermaid
    A9[Repeat Lift and Compaction Cycles]
    A10[Form Complete Stone Column to Surface]
    A11[Move to Next Grid Location]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8

```


```mermaid

    C2[Equivalent Unit Cell Model]

    C6[Applied Surface Load q]

    C8[Vertical Stress in Soil sigma s]

    C10[Composite Settlement S treated equals S untreated over n1]
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3

```

## 4.4 Composite Ground Behaviour Schematic

```mermaid

    D1[Surface Load q Applied]
    D2[Stress Partitioning by Area Ratio]

    D4[Lower Stress sigma s in Soft Clay]

    D8[Increased Bearing Capacity]
    D9[Improved Slope Stability]
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
```
