- **Micropiles** are indispensable for **underpinning** of heritage buildings (e.g., restoration of damaged temples, colonial-era structures), **bridge retrofit** where new columns must be added in confined spaces, and **seismic retrofitting** of critical infrastructure in seismic Zone III–V regions of India.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pull-Out Resistance of a Single Soil Nail

We derive the **ultimate pull-out resistance** of a single soil nail embedded in a *cohesive* soil mass.

**Step 1 — Define geometry of the bond zone.**
Consider a soil nail of **drill-hole diameter** $D_{\text{DH}}$ installed at an angle $\theta$ below the horizontal. The **bond length** beyond the failure plane is $L_b$.

**Step 2 — Define the bond stress model.**
For cohesive soils, the unit bond stress is governed by the *undrained shear strength* of the remoulded soil adjacent to the grout column, modified by an *adhesion factor* $\alpha$:

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot c_u$$

**Step 3 — Set up the surface area of the bond zone.**
The lateral surface area of the cylindrical bond zone is:

$$A_s = \pi \cdot D_{\text{DH}} \cdot L_b$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ultimate pull-out force.**
The ultimate force is the product of the unit bond stress and the surface area:

$$Q_{\text{ult}} = f_s \cdot A_s = (\alpha \cdot c_u) \cdot (\pi \cdot D_{\text{DH}} \cdot L_b)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the factor of safety.**
For a target factor of safety of 1.5 (per FHWA recommendations):

$$Q_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{ult}}}{\text{FS}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{\text{DH}} \cdot L_b \cdot \alpha \cdot c_u}{1.5}$$

**Step 6 — Final expression.**

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{\text{DH}} \cdot L_b \cdot \alpha \cdot c_u}{1.5}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This same logic applies to **cohesionless** soils by replacing $\alpha \cdot c_u$ with $\mu \cdot \sigma_v'$, where $\mu$ is the soil–grout friction coefficient (typically 0.6 – 1.0) and $\sigma_v'$ is the effective vertical stress at the mid-depth of the bond zone.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Axial Capacity of a Micropile in Cohesionless Soil

We derive the **ultimate compression capacity** of a Type B (pressure-grouted) micropile in sandy soil.

**Step 1 — Identify the components of capacity.**
The capacity is the sum of **shaft resistance** ($Q_s$) and **end bearing** ($Q_b$):

$$Q_{\text{ult}} = Q_s + Q_b$$

**Step 2 — Express the shaft resistance.**
For a Type B micropile, the grouted diameter $D_s$ is approximately 1.2 × the drill-hole diameter. The unit shaft friction is:

$$f_s = K \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot \tan \delta$$

**Step 3 — Integrate along the bond length.**
The shaft resistance is:

$$Q_s = \pi \cdot D_s \cdot L_b \cdot f_s = \pi \cdot D_s \cdot L_b \cdot K \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot \tan \delta$$

**Step 4 — Express the end bearing.**
At the pile tip, the end bearing is:

$$Q_b = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot N_q \cdot \sigma_{vt}'$$

where $\sigma_{vt}'$ is the vertical effective stress at the tip and $N_q$ is the bearing capacity factor.

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$Q_{\text{ult}} = \pi \cdot D_s \cdot L_b \cdot K \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot \tan \delta + \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot N_q \cdot \sigma_{vt}'$$

**Step 6 — Apply the factor of safety.**

$$Q_{\text{all}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{ult}}}{\text{FS}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For *Type C/D* micropiles, the grouted zone is significantly enlarged, so $D_s$ can be **1.5 – 2.0 × the drill-hole diameter**. Designers must rely on the **results of pull-out load tests** for actual bond-stress calibration on a project site.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Soil Nail Pull-Out

> **Problem (KTU pattern):**
> A soil nail is installed in a stiff clay with undrained cohesion $c_u = 80$ kPa. The drill-hole diameter is $D_{\text{DH}} = 0.10$ m, and the bond length is $L_b = 5$ m. Take the adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.6$ and factor of safety FS = 1.5. Compute the **design pull-out load**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Unit bond stress:

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot c_u = 0.6 \times 80 = 48 \text{ kPa}$$

Step 2 — Bond surface area:

$$A_s = \pi \cdot D_{\text{DH}} \cdot L_b = \pi \times 0.10 \times 5 = 1.571 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 3 — Ultimate pull-out:

$$Q_{\text{ult}} = f_s \cdot A_s = 48 \times 1.571 = 75.40 \text{ kN}$$

Step 4 — Design pull-out:

$$Q_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{75.40}{1.5} = 50.27 \text{ kN}$$

**Result:** $Q_{\text{design}} \approx 50.3$ kN.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Micropile Axial Capacity

> **Problem (KTU pattern):**
> A Type B micropile of grouted diameter $D_s = 0.20$ m is installed through 8 m of soft clay into a dense sand stratum. The bond length in the sand is $L_b = 6$ m. Take $K = 1.5$, $\delta = 30°$, the average $\sigma_v' = 100$ kPa, $N_q = 40$, and tip $\sigma_{vt}' = 180$ kPa. Take FS = 2.0. Compute $Q_{\text{all}}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Unit skin friction:

$$f_s = K \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot \tan \delta = 1.5 \times 100 \times \tan 30° = 150 \times 0.5774 = 86.6 \text{ kPa}$$

Step 2 — Shaft resistance:

$$Q_s = \pi \cdot D_s \cdot L_b \cdot f_s = \pi \times 0.20 \times 6 \times 86.6 = 326.6 \text{ kN}$$

Step 3 — End bearing:

$$Q_b = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot N_q \cdot \sigma_{vt}' = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.20)^2 \times 40 \times 180$$

$$Q_b = 0.7854 \times 0.04 \times 7200 = 226.2 \text{ kN}$$

Step 4 — Ultimate capacity:

$$Q_{\text{ult}} = 326.6 + 226.2 = 552.8 \text{ kN}$$

Step 5 — Allowable capacity:

$$Q_{\text{all}} = \dfrac{552.8}{2.0} = 276.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Result:** $Q_{\text{all}} \approx 276$ kN.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Soil Nail | Micropile |
|---|---|---|
| Design philosophy | Reinforce *mass*; prevent wedge movement | Transfer load to *deep stratum*; act as pile |
| Primary load mode | Tension | Compression / tension / lateral |
| Length | 0.5 H – 1.2 H (short) | 5 m – 100 m+ (long) |
| Diameter | 75 mm – 150 mm | 100 mm – 300 mm |
| Reinforcement | Single bar (25 mm – 32 mm dia) | Steel casing + central bar + grout |
| Construction direction | Top-down, staged | Top-down single-pass or staged |
| Facing required? | Yes (shotcrete + mesh) | No (capped only) |
| Surcharge tolerance | Low to medium | High |
| Seismic suitability | Moderate (requires ductile facing) | High (composite action) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Soil Nailing — Construction Sequence (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Initial Site Survey and Slope Profiling] --> B[Step 2: Excavate Cut to First Bench Level 1.0 to 2.0 m]
    B --> C[Step 3: Drill Holes at 10 to 20 degree Inclination]
    C --> D[Step 4: Insert Nail Bars with Centralizers]
    D --> E[Step 5: Tremie Grout from Bottom of Hole]
    E --> F[Step 6: Place Bearing Plate and Nut at Nail Head]
    F --> G[Step 7: Apply Shotcrete Facing with Welded Mesh]
    G --> H[Step 8: Cure Grout and Shotcrete for 3 to 7 Days]
    H --> I[Step 9: Repeat Excavation for Next Bench Level]
    I --> J[Step 10: Install Drainage Strips Behind Facing]
    J --> K[Step 11: Final Surface Finish and Monitoring]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The *top-down* construction sequence is the defining feature of soil nailing. The facing is built progressively, allowing the ground mass to be reinforced in stages — this minimizes the risk of slope failure during construction.

---

## 4.2 Micropile — Construction Sequence (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Set Up Drilling Rig at Pile Location] --> B1[Step 2: Advance Casing Through Overburden]
    B1 --> C1[Step 3: Drill Through Casing using Rotary or Auger Method]
    C1 --> D1[Step 4: Reach Design Depth and Clean Hole with Air or Water Flush]
    D1 --> E1[Step 5: Insert Steel Reinforcement with Centralizers]
    E1 --> F1[Step 6: Place Tremie Pipe to Bottom of Hole]
    F1 --> G1[Step 7: Grout from Bottom Up under Gravity or Pressure]
    G1 --> H1[Step 8: Withdraw Casing as Grout Fills Annulus]
    H1 --> I1[Step 9: Add Pressure Grouting Stages for Type B or C or D]
    I1 --> J1[Step 10: Cut Off Pile Head and Attach Pile Cap]
    J1 --> K1[Step 11: Conduct Load Test if Required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **bottom-up grouting** is the most critical operation. It ensures that the grout column is free of voids and that the bond between grout and ground is fully developed along the entire bond length.

---

## 4.3 Soil Nail Wall — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SOIL[Soil Mass] -- Active Earth Pressure --> FACE[Shotcrete Facing]
    FACE -- Transfers --> NAIL[Soil Nail Bar]
    NAIL -- Carries Tensile Force --> GROUT[Grout Column]
    GROUT -- Shear Transfer --> STABLE[Stable Soil Behind Failure Plane]
    FACE -- Connects to --> PLATE[Bearing Plate and Nut]
    PLATE -- Distributes Load --> FACE
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **facing** does not carry significant structural load — it is essentially a *skin* that prevents local erosion and spalling. The **nails** are the primary structural element.

---

## 4.4 Micropile — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STR[Superstructure Load] --> CAP[Pile Cap]
    CAP --> PILEHEAD[Reinforced Pile Head]
    PILEHEAD --> STEEL[Steel Core and Casing]
    STEEL --> GROUT2[High Strength Grout Column]
    GROUT2 -- Skin Friction --> SOIL2[Ground Around Bond Zone]
    GROUT2 -- End Bearing --> STRATA[Load Bearing Stratum at Tip]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **composite steel–grout section** is the hallmark of a micropile. The grout transfers the load from the steel to the surrounding ground via shaft friction, while the steel core provides the tensile/compressive structural strength.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define **soil nailing**. List **any four advantages** of soil nailing over conventional reinforced concrete retaining walls.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Soil nailing is an *in-situ* ground reinforcement technique in which closely spaced, slender reinforcement elements (soil nails) are installed into the slope or excavation face to provide tensile resistance against soil deformation. The reinforcement is passive — it mobilizes only when the soil mass tends to move.

**Four advantages over conventional RC walls:**
1. **Faster construction** due to top-down staged excavation.
2. **Economical** for deep cuts where access is restricted.
3. **Flexible structure** that can accommodate differential settlement.
4. **Minimal site disturbance** — no large excavation or shoring required.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between a **micropile** and a **conventional bored pile** based on (a) diameter, (b) reinforcement, and (c) load transfer mechanism.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Micropile | Conventional Bored Pile |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Diameter | ≤ **300 mm** | > **300 mm** (typically 600 mm – 1500 mm) |
| (b) Reinforcement | Steel casing + central bar + high-strength grout | Heavy rebar cage with concrete |
| (c) Load transfer | Primarily **shaft friction**; limited end bearing | Both shaft friction and end bearing |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Describe the **construction sequence** of a soil-nailed wall with a neat sketch. Mention the typical design parameters (length, spacing, inclination) used in design.
> **(b)** Explain the **internal stability** of a soil-nailed wall using the **FHWA bilinear failure surface method**. What checks are performed?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Construction Sequence and Design Parameters (7 marks):**

1. **Survey and slope preparation** — establish benchmarks, drainage, and access.
2. **Stage excavation** — cut down to 1.0 – 2.0 m depth.
3. **Drilling** — rotary or auger drilling at **10°–20°** inclination.
4. **Nail insertion** — place steel bars (typically **25 mm – 32 mm** dia) with centralizers.
5. **Grouting** — tremie-grout the hole with cement grout (W/C 0.45 – 0.55) from the bottom.
6. **Facing construction** — install welded wire mesh, place bearing plate, apply **100 mm – 200 mm** of shotcrete.
7. **Cure and repeat** — advance to the next bench.

**Design parameters:** $L = 0.5H$ to $1.2H$; $S_v = 1.0$ – $1.5$ m; $S_h = 1.0$ – $2.0$ m; inclination = **10°–20°**.

**(b) Internal Stability — FHWA Bilinear Method (7 marks):**

The FHWA method assumes a **bilinear failure surface** consisting of two planar surfaces meeting at a kink above the toe of the wall.

**Steps:**
1. Locate the **failure wedge** (active block above the bilinear plane).
2. Compute the **driving moment** ($M_d$) from the self-weight of the wedge and any surcharge.
3. Compute the **resisting moment** from the soil shear strength along the failure plane.
4. Distribute the **net moment** to each row of nails as a tensile force:

$$T_i = \dfrac{(M_d - M_r) \cdot y_i}{\sum y_i^2 \cdot S_h \cdot \cos\theta_i}$$

5. **Checks performed:**
   - **Nail rupture:** $T_i \leq A_s \cdot f_y$
   - **Nail pull-out:** $T_i \leq Q_{\text{ult},i} / \text{FS}$ (with FS ≥ 1.5)
   - **Facing bending and punching shear** at the head
   - **Global stability** (FS ≥ 1.35 static, ≥ 1.2 seismic)

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a)** What is a **micropile**? Discuss the **FHWA classification** of micropiles (Type A, B, C, D) with sketches and applications.
> **(b)** A Type B micropile of grouted diameter $D_s = 0.25$ m is installed in sandy soil with bond length $L_b = 7$ m, $K = 1.5$, $\delta = 28°$, average $\sigma_v' = 90$ kPa, end-bearing factor $N_q = 35$, and tip stress $\sigma_{vt}' = 160$ kPa. Take FS = 2.0. Compute the **allowable axial compression capacity**.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Micropile Definition and FHWA Classification (7 marks):**

**Definition:** A micropile is a small-diameter (≤ 300 mm), drilled, grouted, and reinforced deep foundation element, characterized by high strength-to-size ratio and the ability to carry significant loads through the grout–ground bond.

**FHWA Classification:**

| Type | Grouting Method | Grout–Ground Bond | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type A** | Gravity grout only | Limited; relies on hole diameter | Light structures, low loads |
| **Type B** | Pressure grouting ≤ 1 MPa during withdrawal | Improved bond via pressure | Moderate loads, underpinning |
| **Type C** | Global pressure grouting ≥ 1 MPa, single post-grout | Strong bond via grout penetration | Heavy loads, poor ground |
| **Type D** | Multiple post-grouting stages | Bulb formation, very strong bond | Critical infrastructure, seismic retrofit |

**(b) Allowable Axial Capacity (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Unit skin friction:

$$f_s = K \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot \tan \delta = 1.5 \times 90 \times \tan 28° = 135 \times 0.5317 = 71.78 \text{ kPa}$$

Step 2 — Shaft resistance:

$$Q_s = \pi \cdot D_s \cdot L_b \cdot f_s = \pi \times 0.25 \times 7 \times 71.78 = 394.7 \text{ kN}$$

Step 3 — End bearing:

$$Q_b = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot N_q \cdot \sigma_{vt}' = 0.7854 \times 0.0625 \times 35 \times 160$$

$$Q_b = 0.7854 \times 0.0625 \times 5600 = 274.9 \text{ kN}$$

Step 4 — Ultimate capacity:

$$Q_{\text{ult}} = 394.7 + 274.9 = 669.6 \text{ kN}$$

Step 5 — Allowable capacity:

$$Q_{\text{all}} = \dfrac{669.6}{2.0} = 334.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Result:** $Q_{\text{all}} \approx 335$ kN.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Soil Nailing and Micropile Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing drill-hole diameter ($D_{\text{DH}}$) with grouted/equivalent diameter ($D_s$):** In *Type A* micropiles, $D_s = D_{\text{DH}}$. In *Type B/C/D*, $D_s$ is significantly larger — this is a **favourite 2-mark trap** by KTU examiners.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of safety on pull-out:** A common mistake is to report $Q_{\text{ult}}$ as the *design* load. Always divide by FS.
> 3. **Ignoring the angle of inclination $\theta$ in nail design:** When using the bilinear method, the force component along the nail is $T \cdot \cos\theta$ — students often miss this.
> 4. **End-bearing omission in micropiles:** Although the end bearing in micropiles is secondary, KTU examiners expect students to **show the calculation** for the full $Q_{\text{ult}} = Q_s + Q_b$.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Bond stress in **kPa** × area in **m²** gives force in **kN** — students sometimes forget to convert units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Exam Hall:**

- **Soil Nailing = Top-Down Passive Reinforcement:** Stage excavation → drill → insert → grout → facing → repeat.
- **Micropile = Small-Diameter Drilled Pile:** Diameter ≤ **300 mm**, grouted, reinforced, load transfer by skin friction.
- **Nail inclination:** **10°–20°** below horizontal.
- **Nail length:** **0.5 H to 1.2 H**, where H = wall height.
- **Spacing:** $S_v = 1.0$ – $1.5$ m, $S_h = 1.0$ – $2.0$ m.
- **Drill-hole diameter:** **100 mm – 150 mm** for soil nails; **100 mm – 300 mm** for micropiles.
- **Bond stress in clay:** $f_s = \alpha \cdot c_u$, with $\alpha = 0.4$ – $0.8$.
- **Bond stress in sand:** $f_s = K \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot \tan \delta$.
- **Ultimate capacity of micropile:** $Q_{\text{ult}} = Q_s + Q_b = \pi D_s L_b f_s + (\pi/4) D_s^2 q_b$.
- **FHWA Type A → D:** A (gravity), B (pressure during withdrawal), C (single post-grout), D (multi-stage post-grout).
- **Factor of Safety:** FS = **1.5** for nail pull-out, **2.0** for micropile compression.
- **Bilinear Method** is the FHWA-preferred approach for soil-nail wall internal stability.
- **Composite stiffness** of micropile: $EA = E_s A_s + E_g A_g$ — needed for buckling in soft clays.
- **Advantages of soil nailing:** fast, flexible, economical, minimal disruption.
- **Disadvantages of soil nailing:** limited to temporary/permanent cuts in self-supporting ground; not suited for soft clays with high water table without drainage.
- **Advantages of micropiles:** installable in restricted access, high capacity in poor ground, low vibration, works in all soils.
- **Disadvantages of micropiles:** costlier than conventional piles for very large loads; quality highly dependent on grouting and operator skill.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earth Reinforcement: Reinforcement Materials, Reinforced Earth Wall, Design Considerations, Construction Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET633 - GROUND IMPROVEMENT TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Earth Reinforcement: Reinforcement Materials, Reinforced Earth Wall, Design Considerations, Construction Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Earth Reinforcement — Formal Definition

**Earth Reinforcement** (also termed *Soil Reinforcement* or *Mechanically Stabilized Earth, MSE*) is a ground improvement technique in which tensile-resistant inclusions (reinforcements) are placed within a soil mass to enhance its overall shear strength, deformation characteristics, and load-bearing capacity. The composite action arises from the mobilization of **frictional and passive resistance** at the soil–reinforcement interface, transferring tensile stress from the soil (which is weak in tension) to the reinforcement (which is strong in tension).

The modern concept was patented by the French engineer **Henri Vidal** in **1966**, and the first major Reinforced Earth (RE) wall was constructed near Menton, France, in 1968.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Earth reinforcement is treated as a category of "in-situ ground improvement" (passive technique) and is the cornerstone of MSE wall design. Pay close attention to the **tie-back wedge (Coherent Gravity) method** — this is the most frequently examined internal stability approach.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine stacking a column of loose sand. It collapses easily because each grain slides over the next. Now imagine embedding thin horizontal rubber sheets every 5 cm inside the sand column. When you load the top, the sand tries to spread sideways; however, the rubber sheets "grip" the sand and resist this spreading (through friction), making the column behave like a much stiffer, stronger block. Reinforced Earth works on exactly the same principle — except the "rubber sheets" are galvanized steel strips, geogrids, or geotextiles, and the system is typically arranged to retain earth at a vertical or near-vertical face.

A second useful analogy: **mud-brick walls with straw**. For thousands of years, ancient builders embedded straw fibres in clay bricks to give the otherwise weak clay tensile strength. RE walls are the modern, engineered descendant of that same idea.

## 1.3 Fundamental Mechanism — The "Membrane Effect"

For reinforcement to function, two conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Tensile Compatibility** — The reinforcement must elongate less than the surrounding soil under load.
2. **Shear Interaction** — Adequate friction / passive resistance must develop at the interface so that stress is transferred.

When these are met, the soil element is effectively "confined" along planes of reinforcement, increasing its apparent cohesion ($c_R$) according to:

$$c_R = \frac{T_f \cdot S_v}{2 \cdot S_h}$$

where $T_f$ = tensile strength of reinforcement, $S_v$ = vertical spacing, $S_h$ = horizontal spacing.

> [!NOTE]
> The soil is NOT turned into a "better" soil. We are creating a new **composite anisotropic material** whose properties differ significantly from the parent soil.

## 1.4 Categories of Reinforcement

Reinforcement materials are broadly classified into two families:

| Family | Examples | Key Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Metallic (Extensible–In-extensible)** | Galvanized steel strips, steel bars, welded wire mesh, ladder strips, steel grids | High tensile capacity (typically 400–550 MPa yield), low extensibility |
| **Non-metallic (Polymeric / Geosynthetic)** | Geogrids (uniaxial/biaxial), Geotextiles (woven/non-woven), Geostrips, Geobands, HDPE strips | Corrosion-resistant, extensible, light weight, easy to handle |
| **Natural / Traditional** | Bamboo, jute, coir, timber, fibrous roots | Eco-friendly, low durability, used for low-cost / temporary works |

The remainder of these notes focuses on the engineered RE wall system, which is the standard KTU module expectation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Reinforced Earth (RE) Wall

A complete RE wall is composed of **three primary elements** that act in synergy:

1. **Backfill / Frictional Soil** — A select, well-graded, free-draining granular soil (typically with $\phi \geq 28°$ and fines content < 15%). It transmits stress to the reinforcement through interface friction.
2. **Reinforcement (Strips/Grids/Sheets)** — The tensile elements laid horizontally in the backfill, connected to the facing. They resist the lateral earth pressure that would otherwise push the wall outward.
3. **Facing Elements (Skin)** — Modular concrete panels (cruciform, hexagonal, rectangular, or square) that retain the soil at the vertical face, prevent local raveling, and provide aesthetic finish. The facing does NOT carry structural earth pressure — it is only a non-structural skin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Mistake Warning:** Students often assume the facing is the load-bearing element. **It is not.** In an RE wall, the soil + reinforcement system carries the entire earth pressure internally. The facing merely prevents surface erosion.

Auxiliary components include: a **coping beam** (cap), a **leveling pad** (M20 concrete strip at base), **drainage layer** (geotextile + gravel), and **connection devices** (pins, loops, bodkins, or HDPE pins).

## 2.2 Why Does Reinforcement Work? — Stress Transfer Mechanism

When the wall tends to move outward, each reinforcement embedded in the active zone is **pulled** away from the soil behind the failure surface. This pull mobilizes two types of resistance:

- **Direct Sliding Friction** — along the top and bottom surfaces of the reinforcement (dominant for strips, $\delta \approx 0.7\phi$ to $\phi$).
- **Passive Resistance / Bearing** — for transversely ribbed strips, grids, or mats, the soil bears against the cross-bars (dominant for geogrids).

The mobilization length (anchorage / effective length) is governed by:

$$F_{pull-out} = 2 \cdot L_e \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta$$

where:
- $L_e$ = anchorage length beyond the active failure surface
- $b$ = width of the reinforcement
- $\sigma_v$ = effective vertical overburden stress
- $\delta$ = soil–reinforcement interface friction angle

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation you are likely to need in the KTU ESE for this topic. Pay special attention to the units and the meaning of each symbol.

| # | Quantity / Check | Formula | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Rankine Active Earth Pressure Coefficient | $K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Rankine Passive Coefficient | $K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Horizontal pressure at depth z | $\sigma_h = K_a \cdot \sigma_v = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z$ | kN/m² |
| 4 | Lateral force per metre run on a strip of width $S_v$ | $T(z) = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z \cdot S_v$ | kN/m |
| 5 | Maximum horizontal force at base ($z=H$) | $T_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2$ | kN/m |
| 6 | Failure wedge angle (Rankine) | $\psi = 45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ measured from horizontal | degrees |
| 7 | Length of failure surface (Rankine wedge) at depth z | $L_R(z) = (H - z) \cdot \tan\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | m |
| 8 | Pull-out / Anchorage length | $L_e = \dfrac{T(z) \cdot FS_p}{2 \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta}$ | m |
| 9 | Total reinforcement length (uniform) | $L = L_R + L_e$ (typically $L \geq 0.7H$) | m |
| 10 | Pull-out resistance per strip | $F_{po} = 2 \cdot L_e \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta$ | kN |
| 11 | Factor of safety — Sliding | $FS_s = \dfrac{\Sigma V \cdot \tan\phi_b + c \cdot B}{\Sigma H}$ | $\geq 1.5$ |
| 12 | Factor of safety — Overturning | $FS_o = \dfrac{\Sigma M_R}{\Sigma M_O}$ | $\geq 2.0$ |
| 13 | Eccentricity of resultant | $e = \dfrac{\Sigma M_R - \Sigma M_O}{\Sigma V}$ | Must satisfy $e \leq B/6$ |
| 14 | Base pressure (max / min) | $q_{max,min} = \dfrac{\Sigma V}{B}\left(1 \pm \dfrac{6e}{B}\right)$ | kN/m² |
| 15 | Vertical spacing of reinforcement | $S_v \leq 0.6\,\text{m}$ (typical) | m |
| 16 | Reinforcement width | $b \geq 30\,\text{mm}$ (strips), grid $1\times 1$ to $4\times 4$ m | m |
| 17 | Apparent cohesion due to reinforcement | $c_R = \dfrac{T_f \cdot S_v}{2 \cdot S_h}$ | kN/m² |
| 18 | Corrosion allowance for steel | $1.5\,\text{mm}$ per exposed face (FHWA / IS 1893) | mm |
| 19 | Standard factor of safety for rupture | $FS_r = T_{ult} / T_d \geq 1.5$ to $2.0$ | — |
| 20 | Standard factor of safety for pull-out | $FS_p \geq 1.5$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Always state the **unit of each answer**. Marks are frequently deducted for "correct number, wrong unit". Also, for any wall height > 6 m, FHWA / IS code prescribes a **higher factor of safety**; mention this in long-answer questions.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why RE Walls Are Used

Reinforced Earth technology has become a default solution in modern transportation geotechnics because it offers:

- **Construction Speed** — 3 to 5 times faster than conventional RCC retaining walls.
- **Cost Economy** — 25–40% cheaper than cantilever RCC walls for heights up to 15 m.
- **Flexibility** — Tolerates large differential settlement (up to 1 in 50) without distress, unlike rigid concrete walls.
- **Seismic Resilience** — Modern codes (AASHTO LRFD, FHWA-NHI-10-024) have well-validated seismic design procedures.
- **Aesthetic Versatility** — Facings can be sculpted, vegetated, or faced with stone/brick.
- **Material Economy** — Less concrete and steel (per m² of wall face) than conventional retaining walls.

Real-world applications: highway bridge abutments, railway overpasses, airport runway ramps, industrial retaining walls, and flood control levees.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Internal Stability — Coherent Gravity Method (Tie-Back Wedge)

The Coherent Gravity Method, codified in **BS 8006** and adopted in KTU references, treats the reinforced block as a **coherent gravity structure** subjected to lateral earth pressure from the unreinforced soil behind the active wedge. We will derive the maximum tension in the reinforcement step by step.

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Active Wedge

For a wall of height $H$ with horizontal backfill and Rankine conditions, the active failure wedge behind the reinforced block is bounded by an inclined plane making an angle:

$$\psi = 45° + \frac{\phi}{2} \quad \text{(measured from horizontal, i.e. the Rankine active plane)}$$

At any depth $z$ measured from the top of the wall, the **horizontal distance** from the back of the facing to the active failure plane is:

$$L_R(z) = (H - z)\,\tan\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

This distance is the **minimum length of reinforcement required to extend past the active wedge** at that level.

### Step 2 — Vertical and Horizontal Stresses

For a horizontal backfill with surcharge $q$ (kN/m²) and unit weight $\gamma$ (kN/m³):

$$\sigma_v(z) = q + \gamma \cdot z$$

The Rankine active horizontal pressure at the same depth is:

$$\sigma_h(z) = K_a \cdot \sigma_v(z) = K_a \cdot (q + \gamma z)$$

where the Rankine coefficient is:

$$K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Tensile Force in a Reinforcement Layer

Consider a single reinforcement layer installed at depth $z$ with vertical spacing $S_v$. The lateral force to be resisted by *one metre run of wall* across this spacing is the horizontal pressure multiplied by $S_v$:

$$T(z) = \sigma_h(z) \cdot S_v = K_a \cdot (q + \gamma z) \cdot S_v$$

At the base of the wall ($z = H$), the maximum is:

$$T_{max} = K_a \cdot (q + \gamma H) \cdot S_v$$

The **maximum total horizontal thrust** on the wall (per metre run) is the area of the pressure diagram, equal to $\tfrac{1}{2}K_a\gamma H^2$ for $q = 0$:

$$\Sigma H = \int_0^H \sigma_h(z)\,dz = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$$

### Step 4 — Required Anchorage (Effective) Length

The reinforcement must extend beyond the active wedge by an **effective length** $L_e$ such that the frictional pull-out resistance exceeds the design tension $T(z)$ by the prescribed factor of safety $FS_p$:

$$F_{po} \geq T(z) \cdot FS_p$$

$$2 \cdot L_e \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v(z) \cdot \tan\delta \;\geq\; T(z) \cdot FS_p$$

Solving for the required anchorage length:

$$L_e = \frac{T(z)\cdot FS_p}{2 \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v(z) \cdot \tan\delta}$$

Substituting $T(z) = K_a \sigma_v S_v$ and simplifying:

$$L_e = \frac{K_a \cdot S_v \cdot FS_p}{2 \cdot b \cdot \tan\delta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For *uniform* vertical spacing $S_v$ and *uniform* $b$, the expression shows that $L_e$ is **independent of depth** $z$ — that is, every layer needs the same anchorage length. This is why KTU problems commonly state "design a uniform-length RE wall."

### Step 5 — Total Length of Reinforcement

The total length of each reinforcement layer is the sum of the Rankine failure-wedge length and the anchorage length:

$$L_{total} = L_R(z) + L_e$$

However, for a uniform design, we use the value at the *deepest* level, where $L_R$ is smallest but $T$ is largest. A common simplified design choice is:

$$L_{total} = L_R(0) + L_e = H \cdot \tan\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) + L_e$$

Design codes further recommend the practical lower bound:

$$L_{total} \geq 0.7 H$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Design the reinforcement length and verify rupture for an RE wall with $H = 8$ m, backfill unit weight $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $\phi = 30°$, $S_v = 0.6$ m, $S_h = 1.0$ m, strip width $b = 60$ mm = 0.06 m, interface friction $\delta = 0.7 \phi = 21°$, $FS_p = 1.5$, allowable tensile load $T_{allow} = 50$ kN, no surcharge.

**Solution:**

First, compute the Rankine coefficient:

$$K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{30°}{2}\right) = \tan^2(30°) = (0.5774)^2 = 0.3333$$

Vertical stress at base:

$$\sigma_v(H) = 0 + 18 \times 8 = 144 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

Maximum tension in deepest layer:

$$T_{max} = K_a \cdot \sigma_v \cdot S_v = 0.3333 \times 144 \times 0.6 = 28.8 \;\text{kN}$$

Since $T_{max} = 28.8 \text{ kN} < T_{allow} = 50 \text{ kN}$, **rupture is safe**.

Now compute the anchorage length using $\sigma_v$ at the same depth:

$$L_e = \frac{T_{max}\cdot FS_p}{2 \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta} = \frac{28.8 \times 1.5}{2 \times 0.06 \times 144 \times \tan 21°}$$

$$\tan 21° = 0.3839$$

$$L_e = \frac{43.2}{2 \times 0.06 \times 144 \times 0.3839} = \frac{43.2}{6.633} = 6.51 \;\text{m}$$

Rankine wedge length at the top (most critical since $L_R$ is largest at $z = 0$):

$$L_R(0) = H \cdot \tan\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = 8 \times 0.5774 = 4.62 \;\text{m}$$

Total reinforcement length:

$$L_{total} = 4.62 + 6.51 = 11.13 \;\text{m}$$

Check the practical lower bound:

$$0.7 H = 0.7 \times 8 = 5.6 \;\text{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{total} = 11.13 \;\text{m is governing}$$

The wall would need very long strips. In practice, this is reduced by using **geogrids** (wider passive resistance) or **stepped length** (shorter strips at top).

## 3.2 External Stability — The Four Mandatory Checks

External stability treats the RE wall as a **rigid gravity block** of width $L$ (total reinforcement length) and height $H$, subjected to:

- Weight $W = \gamma \cdot L \cdot H$ (per m run)
- Lateral earth thrust $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}K_a\gamma H^2$

The **four external checks** that KTU examiners love to set as 7-mark sub-questions are:

### Check 1 — Sliding

The horizontal thrust $P_a$ is resisted by base friction along the soil–foundation interface:

$$FS_s = \frac{W \cdot \tan\phi_b}{P_a} \quad \text{(c = 0 for granular backfill)}$$

where $\phi_b$ is the base friction angle (typically $0.7\phi_{foundation}$). Required $FS_s \geq 1.5$.

### Check 2 — Overturning

Take moments about the **toe** of the wall. The resisting moment is the weight of the reinforced block acting through its centroid; the overturning moment is $P_a$ acting at $H/3$ from the base:

$$FS_o = \frac{W \cdot (L/2)}{P_a \cdot (H/3)}$$

Required $FS_o \geq 2.0$.

### Check 3 — Eccentricity (Location of Resultant)

The resultant of $W$ and $P_a$ must fall within the middle third of the base to avoid tension at the heel:

$$e = \frac{L}{2} - \frac{W \cdot (L/2) - P_a \cdot (H/3)}{W}$$

Equivalent simpler form:

$$e = \frac{L}{2} - \frac{\Sigma M_R - \Sigma M_O}{W}$$

Required condition: $e \leq L/6$ (middle-third rule).

### Check 4 — Bearing Capacity

Compute the equivalent uniform base pressure from $W$ and $e$:

$$q_{max,min} = \frac{W}{L}\left(1 \pm \frac{6e}{L}\right)$$

Check that $q_{max} \leq q_{allowable}$ (bearing capacity of foundation soil) and $q_{min} \geq 0$ (no tension).

## 3.3 Design Considerations — The Master Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Reference:** The following points are commonly tested as short-answer or 3-mark questions. Memorize the design philosophy before memorizing the formulas.

1. **Reinforcement Spacing** — Vertical spacing $S_v$ is typically $0.4$ to $0.6$ m; horizontal $S_h$ is governed by facing panel width (commonly $0.75$ to $1.5$ m).
2. **Length of Reinforcement** — Adopt a uniform length $L$ with $0.7H \leq L \leq 1.0H$. For tall walls (>6 m), use **stepped length** (longer at base, shorter at top) to economize.
3. **Backfill Selection** — Use granular soil with $\phi \geq 28°$, plasticity index $PI \leq 6$, fines < 15%, pH between 5 and 10 (for metallic strips to avoid accelerated corrosion).
4. **Corrosion Protection** — Galvanize steel strips (zinc coating $\geq 85$ $\mu$m); apply a sacrificial thickness of **1.5 mm** to all exposed faces for design life of 75–100 years.
5. **Drainage** — Provide a chimney drain or geotextile + gravel blanket at the back of the reinforced block to prevent hydrostatic build-up; saturated backfill reduces effective stress and ruptures the reinforcement.
6. **Connection Strength** — The facing-to-reinforcement connection must develop full tensile capacity of the strip. Use ribbed loops, bodkin joints, or HDPE pins.
7. **Surcharge and Live Load** — Highway live load is modelled as equivalent surcharge $q_L = 10$ to $20$ kN/m².
8. **Seismic Design** — Apply Mononobe–Okabe pseudo-static method: $K_{ae}$ replaces $K_a$ with seismic coefficient $k_h = 0.5 \alpha_{max}$.
9. **Durability** — Polymeric reinforcements degrade under UV light; never expose to sunlight for more than 2 weeks.
10. **Construction Tolerances** — Verticality of facing $\leq 25$ mm over 3 m; horizontal alignment $\pm 10$ mm.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Define Reinforced Earth. List any four ideal properties of backfill soil used in RE walls.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reinforced Earth** is a composite material formed by the systematic placement of tensile-resistant reinforcement (metallic strips or geosynthetics) within a frictional soil mass to improve its tensile strength and overall shear behaviour. The concept, developed by Henri Vidal in 1966, relies on frictional interaction at the soil–reinforcement interface.

**Four ideal properties of backfill soil:**

1. **High internal friction angle** ($\phi \geq 28°$) for maximum strength mobilisation.
2. **Low fines content** (≤ 15% passing 75 $\mu$m sieve) to ensure good drainage and reduce pore pressure build-up.
3. **Low plasticity index** ($PI \leq 6$) to minimise volume change and creep.
4. **Chemical compatibility** — pH between 5 and 10; low organic content; free from aggressive salts to prevent corrosion of metallic strips.

**Marks Split:** Definition (1) + Four properties (2 × 0.5 = 2). Total = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Differentiate between metallic and non-metallic (geosynthetic) reinforcement in RE walls.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Metallic Reinforcement | Non-Metallic (Geosynthetic) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Material | Galvanized steel strips / grids | HDPE, polyester, polypropylene geogrids/textiles |
| Tensile behaviour | In-extensible (strain < 1%) | Extensible (strain 5–15%) |
| Corrosion | Susceptible (sacrificial thickness 1.5 mm) | Chemically inert |
| Cost | Higher material cost, lower handling cost | Lower material cost, higher placement care |
| Typical use | Highway bridge abutments, tall walls | Aggressive chemical environment, water-retaining structures |
| Design life | 75–100 years with galvanization | 75–120 years (soil-buried, UV-protected) |

**Marks Split:** Any 6 valid differentiating points (0.5 each) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Internal Stability:**
> 1. **Forgetting the surcharge term** $q$ in $\sigma_v = q + \gamma z$ (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Using $\tan(\phi)$ instead of $\tan(\delta)$ in the pull-out formula (loses 2 marks — $\delta \approx 0.7\phi$).
> 3. Forgetting to verify that $T_{max} \leq T_{allow}$ (rupture check) (loses 1 mark).
> 4. Forgetting to state units (loses 0.5 to 1 mark per quantity).
> 5. **Major pitfall:** Computing $L_R$ at the wrong depth. Use $L_R$ at the *top* of the wall to get the maximum Rankine wedge length.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **Coherent Gravity Method** for the internal stability of a Reinforced Earth wall. Derive the expression for the **effective (anchorage) length** of reinforcement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RE wall has the following data: Height $H = 6$ m; backfill $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, $\phi = 32°$; vertical spacing $S_v = 0.5$ m; strip width $b = 75$ mm; interface friction $\delta = 22°$; allowable tensile force per strip $T_{allow} = 35$ kN; factor of safety against pull-out $FS_p = 2.0$. Compute the **uniform length of reinforcement** required. Verify rupture at the base. Take surcharge $q = 0$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Coherent Gravity Method** (also called the Tie-Back Wedge Method, per BS 8006) treats the reinforced zone as a coherent, monolithic gravity block that resists lateral earth thrust through its self-weight, base shear, and most importantly, the **tie-back action** of the reinforcement layers embedded in the backfill beyond the active failure wedge.

The key assumptions are:
1. The wall face is **vertical or near-vertical**; backfill is **horizontal**.
2. The reinforced block behaves as a **single coherent unit**.
3. **Rankine active state** prevails in the backfill; the failure plane is inclined at $\psi = 45° + \phi/2$ to the horizontal.
4. Each reinforcement is subjected to a horizontal force $T(z) = K_a \cdot \sigma_v(z) \cdot S_v$ and resists it through frictional and passive resistance along its anchorage length $L_e$ beyond the failure surface.

**Derivation of anchorage length:**

For equilibrium of a reinforcement element of width $b$ and effective length $L_e$, the available pull-out resistance (considering both top and bottom surfaces) is:

$$F_{po} = 2 \cdot L_e \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta$$

For safety against pull-out:

$$F_{po} \geq T(z) \cdot FS_p$$

$$2 \cdot L_e \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta \geq T(z) \cdot FS_p$$

Substituting $T(z) = K_a \cdot \sigma_v \cdot S_v$:

$$L_e = \frac{K_a \cdot S_v \cdot FS_p}{2 \cdot b \cdot \tan\delta}$$

> **Marks split (Part a):** Coherent Gravity concept (2) + Assumptions (1.5) + Derivation of $L_e$ (3.5) = **7 marks**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Rankine Active Coefficient:**

$$K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{32°}{2}\right) = \tan^2(29°) = (0.5543)^2 = 0.3073$$

**Step 2 — Vertical stress at base ($z = H = 6$ m):**

$$\sigma_v = q + \gamma H = 0 + 17 \times 6 = 102 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Maximum tension in deepest reinforcement:**

$$T_{max} = K_a \cdot \sigma_v \cdot S_v = 0.3073 \times 102 \times 0.5 = 15.67 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Rupture check:**

$$T_{max} = 15.67 \;\text{kN} \;<\; T_{allow} = 35 \;\text{kN} \quad \checkmark \;\;\text{SAFE}$$

Factor of safety against rupture:

$$FS_r = \frac{T_{allow}}{T_{max}} = \frac{35}{15.67} = 2.23 \quad (> 1.5 \;\text{OK})$$

**Step 5 — Effective (anchorage) length:**

$$L_e = \frac{T_{max} \cdot FS_p}{2 \cdot b \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta} = \frac{15.67 \times 2.0}{2 \times 0.075 \times 102 \times \tan 22°}$$

$$\tan 22° = 0.4040$$

$$L_e = \frac{31.34}{2 \times 0.075 \times 102 \times 0.4040} = \frac{31.34}{6.181} = 5.07 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Rankine failure-wedge length at top ($z = 0$):**

$$L_R(0) = H \cdot \tan\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = 6 \times 0.5543 = 3.33 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Total length of reinforcement:**

$$L_{total} = L_R + L_e = 3.33 + 5.07 = 8.40 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 8 — Practical lower bound check:**

$$0.7H = 0.7 \times 6 = 4.2 \;\text{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{total} = 8.40 \;\text{m is governing}$$

> **Final Answer:** $L_{total} \approx \mathbf{8.4 \text{ m}}$ (uniform), $FS_r = 2.23$ (safe).

> **Marks split (Part b):** $K_a$ (1) + $\sigma_v$, $T_{max}$ (2) + Rupture check (1) + $L_e$ (2) + $L_R$, $L_{total}$ (1) = **7 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4] *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **components of a Reinforced Earth wall**. Explain the role of the facing in an RE wall. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **construction procedure** of an RE wall step by step. What are the **quality control measures** to be adopted during construction? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Components of RE Wall (7 marks):**

An RE wall consists of three primary components working in synergy:

1. **Backfill (Frictional Soil):** A select, well-graded, free-draining granular soil with $\phi \geq 28°$. Provides the confining stress and frictional interface for the reinforcement.

2. **Reinforcement (Tensile Element):** Horizontal layers of galvanized steel strips, geogrids, or geotextiles, connected to the facing. Transfers lateral stress from soil into tension, mobilising the wall's composite strength.

3. **Facing (Skin Element):** Modular precast concrete panels (cruciform, hexagonal, square, or rectangular) connected to the reinforcement via loops, pins, or bodkins. Functions:
   - **Retention of surface soil** to prevent local raveling and erosion.
   - **Architectural finish** (smooth, textured, or vegetated).
   - **Anchorage point** for reinforcement — it does NOT carry structural earth pressure; it is purely a non-structural skin.

Auxiliary components: **coping beam** (cap at top), **leveling pad** (M20 concrete strip at base), **drainage layer** (chimney drain / geotextile + gravel), and **foundation soil**.

> **Marks split (Part a):** Three components × 1.5 marks each + Facing role (1.5) + Auxiliary components (1) = **7 marks**.

---

**Part (b) — Construction Procedure & Quality Control (7 marks):**

**Step-by-step Construction Procedure:**

1. **Foundation Preparation:** Excavate to the design depth; level the base; remove organic soil and weak clay.
2. **Leveling Pad Casting:** Cast an M20 concrete leveling pad (typically 150 mm thick × 300 mm wide) to provide a flat, plumb base for the first row of facing panels.
3. **Erect First Row of Facings:** Set panels on the leveling pad using a crane; check verticality with plumb bob and horizontal alignment with theodolite; temporarily prop with wooden braces.
4. **Place First Layer of Reinforcement:** Lay the bottom-most reinforcement strips at the prescribed $S_v$, ensuring correct orientation (perpendicular to the wall face).
5. **Connect Reinforcement to Facing:** Use the connection system (loops, pins, or HDPE bodkin joints) to lock each strip to its designated slot on the back of the facing panel.
6. **Backfill Placement:** Place a 300 mm loose lift of select backfill over the reinforcement; avoid dropping soil directly onto the strips.
7. **Compaction:** Compact each lift to 95% of MDD (modified Proctor) using a vibratory roller; keep compaction equipment at least 1.5 m away from the back of the facing to avoid panel displacement.
8. **Repeat Cycles:** Repeat steps 4 to 7 for each subsequent layer until the design height is reached.
9. **Drainage Layer:** Install a chimney drain (gravel + geotextile) at the back of the reinforced block during construction.
10. **Coping Beam:** Cast the M25 reinforced concrete coping beam at the top for aesthetics and integrity.

**Quality Control Measures:**

| Stage | Check | Tolerance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Backfill | Sieve analysis, Atterberg limits, density | $\phi \geq 28°$, $PI \leq 6$ |
| Compaction | Sand-cone / nuclear density test | $\geq 95\%$ MDD |
| Facing | Verticality, alignment, elevation | $\pm 25$ mm in 3 m height |
| Reinforcement | Galvanizing thickness, tensile test, dimensions | $\geq 85$ $\mu$m coating |
| Connection | Pull-out test on bodkin/loop | $\geq T_{allow}$ |
| Drainage | Permeability test, geotextile specification | $k \geq 10^{-3}$ m/s for gravel |

> **Marks split (Part b):** 10 construction steps (3.5 marks) + 6 quality checks (3.5 marks) = **7 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Earth Reinforcement):**

- **Inventor:** Henri Vidal (1966) — first wall at Menton, France (1968).
- **Working Principle:** Soil is weak in tension; reinforcement is strong in tension → frictional stress transfer creates a *composite* material.
- **Three components of RE wall:** (1) Backfill (frictional), (2) Reinforcement (strips/grids), (3) Facing (skin, non-structural).
- **Key coefficient:** $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2) = (1 - \sin\phi)/(1 + \sin\phi)$.
- **Active wedge angle:** $\psi = 45° + \phi/2$ from horizontal.
- **Maximum tension at base:** $T_{max} = K_a \cdot \gamma H \cdot S_v$ (no surcharge).
- **Anchorage length:** $L_e = K_a S_v FS_p / (2b \tan\delta)$, **independent of depth** for uniform $S_v$ and $b$.
- **Total length:** $L = L_R + L_e$, with practical minimum $L \geq 0.7H$.
- **Four external stability checks:** Sliding ($FS \geq 1.5$), Overturning ($FS \geq 2.0$), Eccentricity ($e \leq L/6$), Bearing ($q_{max} \leq q_{allow}$).
- **Backfill requirements:** $\phi \geq 28°$, fines $\leq 15\%$, $PI \leq 6$, pH 5–10, free-draining.
- **Corrosion allowance for steel:** $1.5$ mm per exposed face.
- **Construction sequence:** Foundation → Leveling pad → Facings → Reinforcement → Backfill → Compaction → Repeat.
- **Compaction target:** 95% MDD; keep roller $\geq 1.5$ m from facing.
- **Vertical spacing $S_v$:** typically $0.4$–$0.6$ m.
- **Minimum strip width:** 30 mm (metallic) or 1 m (geogrid).
- **Live load surcharge** (highway): equivalent $q_L = 10$–$20$ kN/m².
- **Seismic design:** Mononobe–Okabe $K_{ae}$ with $k_h = 0.5 \alpha_{max}$.
- **Common examiner traps:** Forgetting surcharge $q$, using $\tan\phi$ instead of $\tan\delta$, omitting rupture check, missing units.
- **Golden formula to remember:** $L_e = \dfrac{K_a S_v FS_p}{2 b \tan\delta}$ — universally applicable for *uniform* reinforcement design.
